**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation and Design Modification (7 Marks):**

*Step 1: Substitute Numerical Values (3 Marks)*

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{2}\,(3.3 + (-0.7))}{1 + \sqrt{2}}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{2}\,(2.6)}{1 + \sqrt{2}}$$

$$\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + 1.4142 \times 2.6}{1 + 1.4142} = \frac{0.6 + 3.6769}{2.4142} = \frac{4.2769}{2.4142}$$

$$V_M \approx 1.7715 \text{ V}$$

*Step 2: Verification (1 Mark)*
This is much higher than $V_{DD}/2 = 1.65$ V, indicating the inverter is biased too strongly towards the PMOS side (despite $k_R = 2$, the PMOS has a smaller magnitude of threshold).

*Step 3: Design Modification (3 Marks)*
To bring $V_M$ back to $V_{DD}/2 = 1.65$ V, we must reduce the effective $k_R$. Solving the equation $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ for $k_R$:

$$1.65 = \frac{0.6 + \sqrt{k_R}\,(2.6)}{1 + \sqrt{k_R}}$$

$$1.65(1 + \sqrt{k_R}) = 0.6 + 2.6\sqrt{k_R}$$

$$1.65 + 1.65\sqrt{k_R} = 0.6 + 2.6\sqrt{k_R}$$

$$1.05 = 0.95\sqrt{k_R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{k_R} = 1.1053 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_R \approx 1.22$$

So the designer should reduce $k_R$ from $2.0$ to approximately $1.22$ by either:
- Decreasing $W_N/L_N$ (reducing NMOS strength), or
- Increasing $W_P/L_P$ (boosting PMOS strength).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified for 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) curve** of a CMOS inverter, define and locate the following parameters: $V_{OL}$, $V_{OH}$, $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, and $V_M$. Explain the physical significance of $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ as **unity-gain points**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric CMOS inverter is designed with $V_{DD} = 2.5$ V and $V_{TN} = \vert V_{TP}\vert = 0.5$ V. Calculate $V_{IL}$, $V_{IH}$, $V_M$, $NM_L$, and $NM_H$. Comment on the noise immunity of this design. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**Part (a) — VTC Curve and Parameter Definition (7 Marks):**

*Step 1: VTC Curve Description (3 Marks)*
The Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) of a CMOS inverter is an S-shaped curve plotted with $V_{in}$ on the horizontal axis and $V_{out}$ on the vertical axis, ranging from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. The five regions (A, B, C, D, E) of operation are clearly marked.

*Step 2: Parameter Definitions (3 Marks)*

- **$V_{OL}$ (Output LOW Voltage):** The output voltage when the input is at logic HIGH. For CMOS, $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.
- **$V_{OH}$ (Output HIGH Voltage):** The output voltage when the input is at logic LOW. For CMOS, $V_{OH} \approx V_{DD}$.
- **$V_{IL}$:** The maximum input voltage that is still recognized as logic LOW. Defined as the point where the small-signal gain $dV_{out}/dV_{in} = -1$ in Region B.
- **$V_{IH}$:** The minimum input voltage that is still recognized as logic HIGH. Defined as the point where the small-signal gain $dV_{out}/dV_{in} = -1$ in Region D.
- **$V_M$:** The point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. This is the geometric crossing of the VTC curve with the line $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

*Step 3: Physical Significance of Unity-Gain Points (1 Mark)*
At $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$, the small-signal gain crosses $-1$. For inputs between $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$, the magnitude of the gain exceeds 1, meaning the circuit is in the high-gain transition region. Outside this range ($V_{in} < V_{IL}$ or $V_{in} > V_{IH}$), the gain magnitude is less than 1, and the circuit is in a stable logic region.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

*Step 1: Compute $V_M$ (1 Mark)*
Since the inverter is symmetric with $V_{TN} = -V_{TP}$ and $k_N = k_P$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{2.5}{2} = 1.25 \text{ V}$$

*Step 2: Compute $V_{IL}$ (1.5 Marks)*

$$V_{IL} = \frac{3V_{DD} - 4V_T}{8} = \frac{3(2.5) - 4(0.5)}{8} = \frac{7.5 - 2.0}{8} = \frac{5.5}{8} = 0.6875 \text{ V}$$

*Step 3: Compute $V_{IH}$ (1.5 Marks)*

$$V_{IH} = \frac{3V_{DD} + 4V_T}{8} = \frac{3(2.5) + 4(0.5)}{8} = \frac{7.5 + 2.0}{8} = \frac{9.5}{8} = 1.1875 \text{ V}$$

*Step 4: Compute $NM_L$ (1 Mark)*

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.6875 - 0 = 0.6875 \text{ V}$$

*Step 5: Compute $NM_H$ (1 Mark)*

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.1875 = 1.3125 \text{ V}$$

*Step 6: Comment on Noise Immunity (1 Mark)*

| Parameter | Value | Comment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $NM_L / V_{DD}$ | $0.275$ | **27.5%** — below the 30% industry guideline |
| $NM_H / V_{DD}$ | $0.525$ | **52.5%** — comfortably above 30% |

**Comment:** The HIGH noise margin is excellent (52.5% of $V_{DD}$), but the LOW noise margin is slightly weak (27.5%, just below the 30% rule of thumb). This is the typical CMOS asymmetry. To improve $NM_L$, the designer could increase the threshold voltage $V_T$ or use a different process technology. The overall design is robust enough for most general-purpose digital applications, but may be marginal for ultra-low-voltage or high-noise environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Many students confuse the **switching threshold $V_M$** with the **arithmetic mean of $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$**. These are *not* equal. $V_M$ is the geometric crossing where $V_{in} = V_{out}$, not the midpoint of the unity-gain points.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** When deriving $V_M$, students often forget to take the **positive square root** and may end up with a sign error. Always verify that $V_M$ lies between $0$ and $V_{DD}$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In noise margin problems, students often write $V_{OL} \neq 0$ or $V_{OH} \neq V_{DD}$ for an ideal CMOS inverter. In the **ideal** case, $V_{OL} = 0$ and $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ exactly. (Non-ideal $V_{OL}/V_{OH}$ arise only when current is drawn from the output.)
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** The general $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ formulas (involving $k_R$) are valid for **symmetric threshold** inverters only. If $V_{TN} \neq \vert V_{TP}\vert$, a more complex derivation is required.
> 5. **Mark Loss:** Failing to state the operating regions (saturation vs. linear) for both NMOS and PMOS at the switching threshold costs **1 to 2 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Static behavior** of a CMOS inverter is characterized by the **VTC curve**, which has five distinct operating regions.
- The **Switching Threshold $V_M$** is the input voltage where $V_{in} = V_{out}$, and both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation**.
- The general formula is $V_M = \dfrac{V_{TN} + \sqrt{k_R}\,(V_{DD} + V_{TP})}{1 + \sqrt{k_R}}$, where $k_R = k_N / k_P$.
- For a **symmetric** inverter ($V_{TN} = -V_{TP}$ and $k_N = k_P$), $V_M = V_{DD}/2$.
- **Noise margins** are defined as $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ and $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are the **unity-gain points** where the small-signal voltage gain equals $-1$.
- For a symmetric inverter: $V_{IL} = (3V_{DD} - 4V_T)/8$ and $V_{IH} = (3V_{DD} + 4V_T)/8$.
- For ideal CMOS, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{OL} = 0$ V.
- CMOS noise margins are **asymmetric**: typically $NM_H > NM_L$ because the PMOS is inherently weaker than the NMOS (lower hole mobility).
- A well-designed digital gate should have $NM_H \ge 0.3 V_{DD}$ and $NM_L \ge 0.3 V_{DD}$.
- The PMOS-to-NMOS width ratio to achieve a symmetric $V_M$ is approximately $W_P / W_N \approx 2.5$ (to compensate for $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2.5$).
- $V_M$ affects **propagation delay** symmetry: a balanced $V_M$ gives equal rise and fall times.
- Noise margins are a **static (DC)** concept — they do not account for dynamic noise, glitches, or coupling effects (those are handled by dynamic analysis).
- In modern sub-32 nm CMOS, noise margins shrink as $V_{DD}$ scales down, making **noise-aware design** a critical VLSI challenge.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Behavior - Device Capacitances - Propagation Delay - Power Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Behavior - Device Capacitances - Propagation Delay - Power Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dynamic Behavior of MOSFET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus – PEICT526):**
> The **Dynamic Behavior** of a CMOS circuit refers to the *time-dependent, transient response* of the circuit when the inputs switch between logic levels. Unlike static (DC) behavior, dynamic behavior is governed by the **device capacitances** (intrinsic + parasitic) and the **transient currents** that charge and discharge these capacitances through the nonlinear MOSFET resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study dynamic behavior?**
> In modern digital ICs operating at GHz frequencies, a circuit is rarely in steady state. The *time taken* to transition from logic **0 → 1** or **1 → 0** determines the **maximum clock frequency** and hence the throughput of the entire processor.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World):**
Imagine a **water tank** (representing a load capacitor $C_L$) connected to a **garden pipe with a tap** (the MOSFET acting as a switch). When you open the tap, water flows *gradually* — the tank does not fill instantly. The **diameter of the pipe** is analogous to the MOSFET's *on-resistance* $R_{on}$, and the **size of the tank** is the *load capacitance* $C_L$. The product $R_{on} \cdot C_L$ gives the **time constant** $\tau$ — the metric that defines how fast the circuit can operate.

---

### 1.2 Device Capacitances in MOSFET

A MOSFET exhibits **five principal intrinsic capacitances** that dominate its dynamic response:

| Capacitance | Location | Origin |
|-------------|----------|--------|
| $C_{gs}$ | Gate ↔ Source | Channel charge (intrinsic) + overlap |
| $C_{gd}$ | Gate ↔ Drain | Channel charge (intrinsic) + overlap |
| $C_{gb}$ | Gate ↔ Body | Channel charge (intrinsic) |
| $C_{sb}$ | Source ↔ Body | Reverse-biased p-n junction |
| $C_{db}$ | Drain ↔ Body | Reverse-biased p-n junction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{gb}$ together form the **gate capacitance** $C_g = C_{gs} + C_{gd} + C_{gb}$. The relative partition between them depends on the **operating region** (cutoff / triode / saturation), which is a **frequently asked KTU question**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the MOSFET as a **multi-room apartment**:
- The **gate oxide** is the wall between the "control room" (gate) and the "living room" (channel). The **dielectric constant** of this wall and its **thickness** decide the main capacitance. (Equation: $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$).
- The **p-n junctions** at source and drain are the *basement walls* — they are always reverse-biased and behave like a **variable-width** capacitor (a *varactor*).

---

### 1.3 Propagation Delay

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Propagation Delay ($t_p$)** is the *time difference* between the **50 % transition** of the input waveform and the **50 % transition** of the output waveform. It quantifies how fast a logic gate responds to a change at its input.

Two components are always distinguished in CMOS:
- $t_{pLH}$: Delay for **L**ow-to-**H**igh output transition (PMOS charges $C_L$).
- $t_{pHL}$: Delay for **H**igh-to-**L**ow output transition (NMOS discharges $C_L$).

The **average propagation delay** is:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$$

---

### 1.4 Power Consumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **Total Power** dissipated in a CMOS gate has two principal components:
> 1. **Dynamic Power ($P_{dyn}$)** — energy dissipated while *switching* the load capacitance.
> 2. **Static Power ($P_{static}$)** — energy dissipated due to *leakage currents* even when the circuit is idle.

A third, smaller component, **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{sc}$)**, occurs because both NMOS and PMOS conduct simultaneously during the brief input transition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants** (used throughout Module 1):
> - **$\epsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \cdot \epsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-11} \text{ F/m}$** (permittivity of SiO₂)
> - **$\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$**
> - **$1 \text{ fF/}\mu\text{m}^2 \approx 3.45 \text{ aF/nm}^2$** for $t_{ox} = 3.45 \text{ nm}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Oxide Capacitance (Per Unit Area)

The **gate oxide capacitance per unit area** is the foundation for all dynamic analysis:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} \quad [\text{F/m}^2]$$

- $\epsilon_{ox}$ = permittivity of SiO₂ = **$3.45 \times 10^{-11}$ F/m**.
- $t_{ox}$ = physical thickness of gate oxide (in meters).

The **total gate capacitance** for a transistor of width $W$ and length $L$ is:

$$C_{gate} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $C_{ox}$ the most important single parameter?**
> It directly determines:
> - The **transconductance** $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}$ (drives $R_{on}$).
> - The **intrinsic capacitances** $C_{gs}, C_{gd}, C_{gb}$.
> - The **dynamic power** $P_{dyn} \propto C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$.

---

### 2.2 Intrinsic Capacitances in Each Operating Region

The channel charge $Q$ is shared between source and drain in different ways depending on the region. **$C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{gb}$ are summarized in the table below** — a *must-know* for KTU short-answer questions.

| Region | $C_{gs}$ | $C_{gd}$ | $C_{gb}$ | Notes |
|--------|----------|----------|----------|-------|
| **Cutoff** ($V_{gs} < V_{th}$) | $C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ | $C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ | $0$ | Only overlap capacitances |
| **Triode / Linear** ($V_{ds} \approx 0$) | $\frac{1}{2} C_{ox} W L$ | $\frac{1}{2} C_{ox} W L$ | $0$ | Channel charge shared equally |
| **Saturation** ($V_{ds} \geq V_{dsat}$) | $\frac{2}{3} C_{ox} W L$ | $0$ | $0$ | Pinch-off removes $C_{gd}$ intrinsic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Overlap capacitances** $C_{ov} = C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ (where $L_{ov}$ is the lateral diffusion, typically **$0.05$–$0.1 \mu m$**) *always* exist in parallel with the intrinsic capacitances — they are the dominant parasitic in **saturation**.

---

### 2.3 Junction Capacitances (Source-Body & Drain-Body)

For an **abrupt p-n junction** (the source and drain are diffusion islands in the bulk), the capacitance per unit area is:

$$C_j = \frac{C_{j0}}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{V_{sb}}{V_{bi}}}}$$

Where:
- $C_{j0}$ = zero-bias junction capacitance per unit area (F/m²).
- $V_{sb}$ = reverse-bias voltage across the junction (always $> 0$ in MOSFET).
- $V_{bi}$ = built-in potential (**$\approx 0.7$ V** for typical doping).

The **bottom-plate** and **side-wall** components:

$$C_{sb} = C_{j,sw} \cdot P_{s,sw} + C_{j,bot} \cdot A_{s,bot}$$
$$C_{db} = C_{j,sw} \cdot P_{d,sw} + C_{j,bot} \cdot A_{d,bot}$$

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **board-exam ready** reference. Note the use of `\mid` (not the literal pipe character) for all set/norm bars to preserve table formatting.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \frac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 2 | $C_{gate} = C_{ox} W L$ | Total gate capacitance of a transistor | F |
| 3 | $C_{gs}^{sat} = \frac{2}{3} C_{ox} W L + C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ | Gate-to-source in saturation | F |
| 4 | $C_{gd}^{sat} \approx C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ | Gate-to-drain in saturation (≈ overlap) | F |
| 5 | $C_j(V) = \frac{C_{j0}}{\sqrt{1 + V_{sb}/V_{bi}}}$ | Reverse-biased junction capacitance | F |
| 6 | $R_{on} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{gs} - V_{th})}$ | NMOS on-resistance in linear region | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 R_p C_L$ | Low-to-High delay (PMOS charging) | s |
| 8 | $t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 R_n C_L$ | High-to-Low delay (NMOS discharging) | s |
| 9 | $t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$ | Average propagation delay | s |
| 10 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching (dynamic) power | W |
| 11 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Leakage power | W |
| 12 | $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per switching event | J |
| 13 | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | Power-Delay Product | J |
| 14 | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | Energy-Delay Product | J·s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - **Intel / TSMC design teams** use Equation (10) to estimate the *power envelope* of an entire chip *before* tape-out. A chip with $10^9$ transistors switching at $3$ GHz, each driving a $1$ fF node at $1$ V with $\alpha = 0.1$, consumes $P_{dyn} \approx 0.3$ W per gate-type-block.
> - Equation (6) is the *first-order delay estimator* used in **synthesis tools (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler)** during early design exploration.
> - The **EDP (Equation 14)** is the *figure of merit* used to compare competing logic families (CMOS vs. adiabatic logic vs. domino).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay (RC Model)

**Problem Setup:** A symmetric CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L$. The NMOS has on-resistance $R_n$ and PMOS has on-resistance $R_p$. We assume a *step input* and derive $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$.

**Step 1 — Equivalent circuit during $1 \rightarrow 0$ output transition:**

The output node transitions from $V_{DD}$ to $0$ V through the NMOS, which acts as a *non-linear resistor* $R_n(V_{out})$. The load $C_L$ discharges.

**Step 2 — KCL at the output node:**

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -i_{D,n}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the linear-region current of the NMOS:**

For $V_{ds} \ll 2(V_{gs} - V_{th})$, the NMOS behaves as a linear resistor of value:

$$R_n = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{gs} - V_{th})}$$

Substituting $i_{D,n} = V_{out} / R_n$:

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_n}$$

**Step 4 — Solving the first-order ODE:**

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{V_{out}} = -\frac{dt}{R_n C_L}$$

Integrating from $V_{out}(0) = V_{DD}$ to $V_{out}(t)$:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{out}(t)}{V_{DD}}\right) = -\frac{t}{R_n C_L}$$

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_n C_L)}$$

**Step 5 — Propagation delay at the 50 % point ($V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$):**

$$t_{pHL} = R_n C_L \cdot \ln(2) = 0.69 \, R_n C_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Same derivation for the PMOS** (charging phase) yields:
> $$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \, R_p C_L$$

**Step 6 — Average propagation delay:**

$$t_p = \frac{0.69}{2} (R_n + R_p) C_L = 0.69 \, R_{eq} \, C_L$$

Where the equivalent resistance is $R_{eq} = (R_n + R_p)/2$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Dynamic Power Consumption

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the load capacitor at $V_{DD}$:**

When the output is at $V_{DD}$, the energy stored is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy supplied by the $V_{DD}$ rail during charging:**

The charge delivered to the capacitor is $Q = C_L V_{DD}$. The energy supplied is:

$$E_{supplied} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS during charging:**

$$E_{dissipated, charge} = E_{supplied} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4 — Energy dissipated in the NMOS during discharge:**

When the capacitor discharges from $V_{DD}$ to $0$, all $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ stored energy is dissipated in the NMOS (none returned to the supply).

**Step 5 — Total energy per full switching cycle ($0 \rightarrow 1 \rightarrow 0$):**

$$E_{cycle} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2}_{\text{charge (PMOS)}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2}_{\text{discharge (NMOS)}} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6 — Switching activity factor $\alpha$:**

In a typical digital circuit, a node does not switch every clock cycle. Let $\alpha$ be the average number of $0 \to 1$ transitions per clock period $T = 1/f$. The **average dynamic power** is:

$$P_{dyn} = \frac{E_{cycle} \cdot \alpha}{T} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $\alpha V_{DD}^2$ is the master lever**: dropping $V_{DD}$ from $1.0$ V to $0.7$ V reduces dynamic power by **~51 %**, which is *why the semiconductor industry aggressively scales $V_{DD}$*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Short-Circuit (Crowbar) Power

During the input transition, both NMOS and PMOS conduct for a brief interval $\Delta t \approx t_r / 2$ (where $t_r$ is the rise time of the input). The short-circuit current is approximately:

$$I_{sc, peak} = \frac{\beta}{2} \left(\frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{th}}{1}\right) \cdot \text{(time fraction)}$$

The integrated energy per switching event is:

$$E_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot t_r$$

The **average short-circuit power** is:

$$P_{sc} = \alpha f \cdot E_{sc} = \frac{\alpha f \beta t_r}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering tip:** $P_{sc}$ is minimized when $t_r$ is very small (sharp input edges) and when the gate is **not** loaded with a large $C_L$ (otherwise the output transition is slow and both devices conduct longer). This is why **input slew rate** matters in static timing analysis.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Question:** A CMOS inverter drives $C_L = 50$ fF. Given $R_n = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_p = 1.3$ k$\Omega$, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $f = 500$ MHz, $\alpha = 0.2$. Calculate (a) $t_{pHL}$, (b) $t_{pLH}$, (c) average $t_p$, (d) $P_{dyn}$.

**Solution:**

**(a)** $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_n \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1000 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15}$

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-11} = 3.45 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s} = 34.5 \text{ ps}$$

**(b)** $t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_p \cdot C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1300 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15}$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot 6.5 \times 10^{-11} = 4.485 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s} = 44.85 \text{ ps}$$

**(c)** $t_p = (34.5 + 44.85) / 2 = 39.675 \text{ ps} \approx 39.7 \text{ ps}$

**(d)** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.8)^2 \cdot 500 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 3.24 \cdot 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \cdot 50 \cdot 3.24 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-15+8}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 162 \times 10^{-7} = 1.62 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 16.2 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MOSFET Capacitance Map

This diagram maps the **five intrinsic capacitances** of a MOSFET and their relationship to the operating region. It is the *single most important visual* for KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef capNode fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef regNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef extNode fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    IN[Input Node Gate]:::extNode
    OUT[Output Node Drain]:::extNode
    SRC[Source Node]:::extNode
    BDY[Bulk / Body]:::extNode

    COX["Cox per unit area = epsilon_ox divided by t_ox"]:::capNode
    CGS["Cgs Gate to Source\nIntrinsic + Overlap"]:::capNode
    CGD["Cgd Gate to Drain\nIntrinsic + Overlap (Miller)"]:::capNode
    CGB["Cgb Gate to Body\nZero in Strong Inversion"]:::capNode
    CSB["Csb Source to Body\nReverse Biased Junction"]:::capNode
    CDB["Cdb Drain to Body\nReverse Biased Junction (Miller)"]:::capNode
    COV["Cov Overlap Capacitance\n= Cox * W * L_ov"]:::capNode

    CUT[Cutoff Region]:::regNode
    TRI[Triode Region]:::regNode
    SAT[Saturation Region]:::regNode

    IN -->|gate oxide| COX
    COX -->|splits into| CGS
    COX -->|splits into| CGD
    COX -->|splits into| CGB
    IN -.->|overlap lateral| COV
    COV -->|adds to| CGS
    COV -->|adds to| CGD

    SRC --> CSB
    OUT --> CDB
    BDY --> CSB
    BDY --> CDB
    BDY --> CGB

    CUT -->|"only Cov in Cgs, Cgd"| CGS
    CUT -->|"only Cov in Cgd"| CGD
    CUT -->|"Cgb approx 0"| CGB

    TRI -->|"Cgs = half Cox W L"| CGS
    TRI -->|"Cgd = half Cox W L"| CGD

    SAT -->|"Cgs = 2 by 3 Cox W L"| CGS
    SAT -->|"Cgd approx Cov only"| CGD
    SAT -->|"Cgb approx 0"| CGB
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — CMOS Inverter Dynamic Behavior (Charging & Discharging)

This block-level architecture shows the *signal flow* during the two switching phases, which is the most common KTU Part B 14-mark question structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef cmosNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flowNode fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    VIN[Input Vin]:::flowNode
    VDD[Supply VDD]:::flowNode
    GND[Ground GND]:::flowNode

    PMOS["PMOS Transistor\nPull Up Network"]:::cmosNode
    NMOS["NMOS Transistor\nPull Down Network"]:::cmosNode

    CL[Load Capacitor CL]:::outNode
    VOUT[Output Voltage Vout]:::outNode

    VIN -->|gate input| PMOS
    VIN -->|gate input| NMOS

    VDD --> PMOS
    PMOS -->|charging current| CL
    CL --> VOUT

    NMOS -->|discharging current| CL
    VOUT --> CL
    CL --> GND

    PHASE1[Phase 1: Vin = 0\nPMOS ON, NMOS OFF\nCharging through Rp\nt_pLH = 0.69 Rp CL]:::flowNode
    PHASE2[Phase 2: Vin = 1\nPMOS OFF, NMOS ON\nDischarging through Rn\nt_pHL = 0.69 Rn CL]:::flowNode

    PHASE1 --> PMOS
    PHASE2 --> NMOS
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Power Consumption Decomposition

This diagram decomposes **total CMOS power** into its three principal components, which is the *expected answer structure* for KTU Part A and Part B questions on "Power Consumption in CMOS".

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef rootNode fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef dynNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef statNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef scNode fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    PTOT[Total Power Dissipation P_total]:::rootNode

    PDYN[Dynamic Power P_dyn\nSwitching Power]:::dynNode
    PSTAT[Static Power P_static\nLeakage Power]:::statNode
    PSC[Short Circuit Power P_sc\nCrowbar Power]:::scNode

    PTOT --> PDYN
    PTOT --> PSTAT
    PTOT --> PSC

    PDYN --> PDYN_EQ["P_dyn = alpha * C_L * VDD^2 * f"]:::dynNode
    PDYN --> PDYN_FACTORS["Depends on activity factor alpha, capacitance, supply voltage, clock frequency"]:::dynNode

    PSTAT --> PSTAT_EQ["P_static = VDD * I_leak"]:::statNode
    PSTAT --> PSTAT_FACTORS["Depends on subthreshold leakage, gate leakage, junction leakage"]:::statNode

    PSC --> PSC_EQ["P_sc proportional to input rise time t_r"]:::scNode
    PSC --> PSC_FACTORS["Depends on VDD, transistor beta, and load capacitance CL"]:::scNode

    PDYN_FACTORS --> PSTRATEGY1[Strategy: Reduce VDD and C]:::dynNode
    PSTAT_FACTORS --> PSTRATEGY2[Strategy: Use high Vth, multi Vth design]:::statNode
    PSC_FACTORS --> PSTRATEGY3[Strategy: Sharpen input slew]:::scNode
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

> Define the term **propagation delay** of a CMOS inverter. Derive the expression for the **average propagation delay** in terms of the equivalent on-resistance and load capacitance.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Propagation delay $t_p$ is the time between the *50 % transition point* of the input voltage and the *50 % transition point* of the output voltage, when the input is driven by a step waveform.

**Derivation:** When the input switches, the output node charges/discharges through the MOSFET on-resistance.

For an **H → L transition**, the NMOS discharges $C_L$:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t/(R_n C_L)}$$

At the 50 % point ($V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$):

$$t_{pHL} = R_n C_L \cdot \ln 2 = 0.69 \, R_n C_L$$

Similarly, for the **L → H transition**:

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \, R_p C_L$$

Therefore the **average propagation delay** is:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = \frac{0.69}{2}(R_n + R_p) C_L = 0.69 \, R_{eq} C_L$$

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

> List the **five principal capacitances** of a MOSFET and state the value of $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{gb}$ in the **saturation region**.

**Model Answer:**

| Capacitance | Type | Saturation Region Value |
|-------------|------|-------------------------|
| $C_{gs}$ | Intrinsic + Overlap | $\frac{2}{3} C_{ox} W L + C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ |
| $C_{gd}$ | Overlap only | $\approx C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ |
| $C_{gb}$ | — | $\approx 0$ (channel pinched off) |
| $C_{sb}$ | Reverse-biased junction | $C_{j0} A_s / \sqrt{1 + V_{sb}/V_{bi}}$ |
| $C_{db}$ | Reverse-biased junction | $C_{j0} A_d / \sqrt{1 + V_{db}/V_{bi}}$ |

> **Physical reason for $C_{gd}^{sat} \approx 0$:** In saturation, the channel *pinches off* near the drain, so there is no channel charge controlled by the drain. Only the **gate-to-drain overlap** contributes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **NOT** confuse $C_{ox}$ (per unit area) with $C_{gate}$ (total). Many students write $C_{ox} = C_{ox} W L$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> - In the dynamic-power derivation, you **must show all 5 steps** (charge supplied, energy stored, energy dissipated, per cycle, average). A single-line answer receives only **partial credit** (max 5/14).
> - Always state the **assumptions** (step input, single-pole RC, NMOS in deep triode) before plugging in numbers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **different device capacitances** associated with a MOSFET. Discuss how $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, and $C_{gb}$ vary between the cutoff, triode, and saturation regions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CMOS inverter with $W_p/L_p = 2$ and $W_n/L_n = 1$ drives a load capacitance of $C_L = 100$ fF. Given $C_{ox} = 8.5$ fF/$\mu$m², $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100$ $\mu$A/V², $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ $\mu$A/V², $V_{DD} = 2.5$ V, $V_{th} = 0.5$ V, and $L = 0.25$ $\mu$m. Calculate $t_{pHL}$, $t_{pLH}$, and the average propagation delay $t_p$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to the *MOSFET Capacitance Map* in **Section 4.1**. The five capacitances are $C_{gs}, C_{gd}, C_{gb}, C_{sb}, C_{db}$. The variation across regions is:

- **Cutoff:** Only overlap capacitances $C_{ov} = C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ are present in $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$. $C_{gb} = 0$.
- **Triode:** Channel forms; $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox} W L + C_{ox} W L_{ov}$. $C_{gb} = 0$.
- **Saturation:** Pinch-off at the drain. $C_{gs} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox} W L + C_{ov}$. $C_{gd} \approx C_{ov}$. $C_{gb} = 0$.

> The junction capacitances $C_{sb}$ and $C_{db}$ are present in all regions and are voltage-dependent (Eq. 5 of Formula Sheet).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the on-resistances using $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{gs} - V_{th})}$.

For NMOS: $V_{gs} - V_{th} = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0$ V

$$R_n = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (1/0.25) \cdot 2.0} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 4 \cdot 2.0}$$

$$R_n = \frac{1}{800 \times 10^{-6}} = 1250 \, \Omega = 1.25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For PMOS: $V_{gs} - V_{th} = -2.5 - (-0.5) = -2.0$ V $\Rightarrow$ $\mid V_{gs} - V_{th} \mid = 2.0$ V

$$R_p = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (2/0.25) \cdot 2.0} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 8 \cdot 2.0}$$

$$R_p = \frac{1}{800 \times 10^{-6}} = 1250 \, \Omega = 1.25 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Compute the delays.

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \, R_n C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1250 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-15}$$

$$t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-10} = 8.625 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s} \approx 86.25 \text{ ps}$$

$$t_{pLH} = 0.69 \, R_p C_L = 0.69 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-10} = 86.25 \text{ ps}$$

**Step 3:** Average propagation delay.

$$t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2} = \frac{86.25 + 86.25}{2} = 86.25 \text{ ps}$$

> **Note:** Because the $W_p/L_p$ ratio is double the $W_n/L_n$ ratio, the PMOS and NMOS are *matched* for equal drive strength, and $t_{pLH} = t_{pHL}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **dynamic power consumption** of a CMOS inverter. Explain the factors on which it depends and the techniques used to reduce it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An 8-bit ripple carry adder operating at $f = 1$ GHz and $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V has an average load capacitance of $C_L = 200$ fF per full-adder bit. If the average switching activity is $\alpha = 0.15$, calculate:
- (i) the **dynamic power** dissipated by the entire adder.
- (ii) the **energy consumed per clock cycle** by the adder.
- (iii) the **average power saved per second** if $V_{DD}$ is scaled to $0.9$ V (assume $f$ and $\alpha$ remain constant).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Dynamic Power:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes (full 6-step derivation). The key result is:

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$$

**Factors:**
- Switching activity $\alpha$ (architecture-level, clock gating reduces it).
- Load capacitance $C_L$ (smaller transistors, shorter wires).
- Supply voltage $V_{DD}^2$ (most powerful lever).
- Operating frequency $f$ (governed by the clock and pipeline depth).

**Reduction techniques:**
1. Clock gating / power gating → reduces $\alpha$.
2. Transistor sizing optimization → reduces $C_L$.
3. Voltage scaling (DVFS) → reduces $V_{DD}^2$.
4. Pipelining → reduces $C$ per stage at cost of latency.
5. Adiabatic / charge-recovery logic → recycles $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Dynamic power of the 8-bit adder:**

Total load capacitance:

$$C_{total} = 8 \times 200 \text{ fF} = 1600 \text{ fF} = 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$

Substituting into $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$:

$$P_{dyn} = 0.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (1.2)^2 \cdot 1 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.15 \cdot 2.304 \times 10^{-3} = 3.456 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} \approx 345.6 \, \mu\text{W}}$$

**(ii) Energy consumed per clock cycle:**

$$E_{cycle} = P_{dyn} \cdot T = \frac{P_{dyn}}{f} = \frac{3.456 \times 10^{-4}}{10^9}$$

$$\boxed{E_{cycle} = 3.456 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J} = 0.3456 \text{ pJ}}$$

**Alternative check:** $E_{cycle} = \alpha C_{total} V_{DD}^2 = 0.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 1.44 = 3.456 \times 10^{-13}$ J ✓

**(iii) Power saved by scaling $V_{DD}$ to $0.9$ V:**

New dynamic power:

$$P_{dyn}' = 0.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (0.9)^2 \cdot 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn}' = 0.15 \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.81 \cdot 10^9$$

$$P_{dyn}' = 0.15 \cdot 1.296 \times 10^{-3} = 1.944 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \approx 194.4 \, \mu\text{W}$$

**Power saved per second:**

$$\Delta P = P_{dyn} - P_{dyn}' = 345.6 - 194.4 = 151.2 \, \mu\text{W}$$

> **Ratio check:** $P'/P = (0.9/1.2)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$. So $P' = 0.5625 \times 345.6 = 194.4$ µW ✓ — a **43.75 %** power reduction just from a 25 % voltage scale-down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Oxide capacitance:** $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$; total gate cap $C_g = C_{ox} W L$ (intrinsic only).
- **Region-wise intrinsic caps:**
  - Cutoff → $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = C_{ov}$; $C_{gb} = 0$.
  - Triode → $C_{gs} = C_{gd} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{ox} W L + C_{ov}$.
  - Saturation → $C_{gs} = \tfrac{2}{3} C_{ox} W L + C_{ov}$; $C_{gd} \approx C_{ov}$.
- **Overlap capacitance:** $C_{ov} = C_{ox} W L_{ov}$ — *always present*.
- **Junction capacitance:** $C_j = C_{j0} / \sqrt{1 + V_{sb}/V_{bi}}$ — *voltage dependent*, *non-linear*.
- **On-resistance (NMOS, linear):** $R_{on} = 1 / [\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{gs} - V_{th})]$.
- **Propagation delay:** $t_{pHL} = 0.69 R_n C_L$, $t_{pLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L$, $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$.
- **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- **Static power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$.
- **Short-circuit power:** Depends on input rise time $t_r$ and $\beta$.
- **Energy per transition:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.
- **Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $PDP = P_{avg} t_p$ — the energy dissipated per switching event.
- **Energy-Delay Product (EDP):** $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ — the figure of merit for logic families.
- **Miller effect:** $C_{gd}$ is multiplied by the voltage gain when the output swings — a *common pitfall* in delay estimation for cascaded stages.
- **Scaling trend:** $V_{DD}$ scaling gives *quadratic* power savings but *linear* delay penalty → designers use **DVFS** to balance.
- **The factor of 0.69 = ln(2):** comes from the 50 % delay point of a first-order RC; remember it for the exam.
- **Symmetric inverter rule:** $W_p/W_n = \mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to match $t_{pLH}$ and $t_{pHL}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Fabrication Processes: N-Tub
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CMOS Fabrication Processes: N-Tub

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**N-Well CMOS Process** is a dominant CMOS integrated circuit fabrication technology in which **PMOS transistors are fabricated inside an N-type well (N-Well)** that is selectively diffused/implanted into a lightly doped **P-type silicon substrate (P-Sub)**, while the **NMOS transistors are fabricated directly in the P-type substrate** itself. This single-well architecture (also called an *N-Tub process*) is one of the two historical CMOS well structures (the other being P-Well), and it has become the industry standard for submicron and deep-submicron digital VLSI because n-channel MOSFETs offer superior carrier mobility (electrons ≈ 1350 cm²/V·s) compared to holes in p-channel devices (≈ 480 cm²/V·s), allowing the natural P-substrate to host the higher-performance nMOS devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Under the topic *CMOS Fabrication Processes*, the N-Well (N-Tub) flow is mandatory. Students are expected to draw the **process flow with cross-section**, list every **mask level**, and justify the **choice of well type** in 14-mark ESE questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Two-Story Building on a Single Plot"

Imagine a large residential **plot of land (the P-type silicon wafer)**. You want to construct two different types of homes:

- **Type-A homes (NMOS transistors)** are built **directly on the ground floor (P-substrate)** because the soil (p-type doping) is naturally suited for them, and they need a *strong, fast foundation* (high electron mobility).
- **Type-B homes (PMOS transistors)** need an *opposite kind of soil* (n-type doping) for their foundation. Instead of digging out a *separate huge plot* for them, you simply **selectively pour an n-type chemical into specific pits** in the existing p-type plot — this creates the **N-Well** islands. The Type-B homes are then built on these islands.

> Result: One unified wafer, two transistor types, **one masking/implant step** for the well, and **minimum cost**. This is the essence of the N-Well CMOS process.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electron mobility | $\mu_n$ | **1350** | cm²/V·s |
| Hole mobility | $\mu_p$ | **480** | cm²/V·s |
| Silicon permittivity | $\varepsilon_{si}$ | **1.04 × 10⁻¹²** | F/cm |
| Oxide permittivity | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **3.45 × 10⁻¹³** | F/cm |
| Intrinsic carrier conc. | $n_i$ | **1.45 × 10¹⁰** | cm⁻³ |
| Substrate doping (P) | $N_A$ | **10¹⁵ – 10¹⁶** | cm⁻³ |
| N-Well surface doping | $N_D$ | **10¹⁶ – 10¹⁷** | cm⁻³ |
| Thermal oxide growth T | — | **900 – 1100** | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> All these numerical values are **industry-standard process corners** and are required for solving threshold-voltage and sheet-resistance problems in the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why N-Well Dominates the Industry

The selection between N-Well and P-Well in CMOS is governed by three engineering trade-offs:

1. **Mobility Advantage:** Electrons are ~**2.8× faster** than holes. By placing nMOS in the *native* P-substrate (no well needed for it), the designer avoids the additional **junction capacitance** and **body-effect** losses that would degrade nMOS performance if it were placed in a P-Well on an N-substrate.
2. **Latch-up Immunity:** The lightly doped substrate gives a high substrate resistance $R_{sub}$, which *helps suppress* the parasitic SCR (Silicon Controlled Rectifier) latch-up path.
3. **Process Simplicity:** Only **one well mask** (the N-Well mask) is required. The P-Well process would require a P-Well mask *plus* an N-substrate, raising cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** "Why N-Well over P-Well?" is one of the most frequently asked 7-mark sub-questions. Always lead with the **electron-mobility argument**, then follow with latch-up and process-economy reasons.

## 2.2 The N-Well CMOS Process Flow — Step-by-Step

The complete fabrication consists of **~10–12 major steps** and uses **~7–9 mask levels**. The sequence is:

| Step # | Process Step | Mask Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P-type wafer cleaning | — | Remove native oxide, organic residue |
| 2 | Pad-oxide growth (~50 nm) | — | Stress-relief layer for nitride |
| 3 | Si₃N₄ deposition | — | Selectively mask oxidation |
| 4 | **N-Well photolithography** | **Mask 1 (N-Well)** | Pattern photoresist to open well regions |
| 5 | **N-Well implantation** (Phosphorus, ~100–150 keV, dose ≈ 10¹³ cm⁻²) | — | Dope well regions n-type |
| 6 | **N-Well drive-in** (1100 °C, several hours) | — | Diffuse dopants to target depth |
| 7 | LOCOS / STI formation | Mask 2 (Active Area) | Define transistor active regions |
| 8 | **Threshold-adjust implant** (Boron, low dose) | — | Fine-tune $V_T$ of both nMOS & pMOS |
| 9 | **Gate-oxide growth** (~10 nm) | — | Grow thin SiO₂ for gate dielectric |
| 10 | **Polysilicon deposition & doping** (POCl₃ or in-situ) | — | Form gate material |
| 11 | **Poly patterning** | Mask 3 (Poly Gate) | Define gate electrodes |
| 12 | **N⁺ S/D implant** (As, 50 keV) | Mask 4 (N⁺ S/D) | Form nMOS source/drain |
| 13 | **P⁺ S/D implant** (BF₂, 40 keV) | Mask 5 (P⁺ S/D) | Form pMOS source/drain (in N-Well) |
| 14 | Sidewall spacer formation | — | Enable LDD / offset spacer |
| 15 | Contact & metallization | Masks 6, 7, 8 | Interconnects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mask Count (KTU Exam Favorite):** A *baseline* N-Well CMOS process needs **minimum 5 masks** (N-Well, Active, Poly, N⁺, P⁺) but production processes use 7–9 masks to add contacts, vias, and multiple metal layers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_j = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$ | Junction depth after diffusion | cm |
| 2 | $N_s = N_0 \sqrt{\pi D t}$ | Sheet dopant concentration | cm⁻² |
| 3 | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{q \mu N_s}$ | Sheet resistance | Ω/□ |
| 4 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential | V |
| 5 | $V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A \, 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}}$ | NMOS threshold voltage | V |
| 6 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate-oxide capacitance per unit area | F/cm² |
| 7 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | Body-effect coefficient | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 8 | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | Saturation drain current (long-channel) | A |
| 9 | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_T)$ | Transconductance | A/V |
| 10 | $L_{\min} = 0.4\,[x_j \, t_{ox}(W_d + W_L)]^{1/3}$ | Classical minimum channel length (Brewer) | µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formulas 4–7 govern the **threshold-voltage design** of both nMOS (in P-sub) and pMOS (in N-Well). Because pMOS sits in a *separately doped* well, the *body effect* on pMOS is mitigated by tying the N-Well to $V_{DD}$, while the P-substrate is tied to $V_{SS}$ — this is the **body-bias convention** unique to the N-Well process.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The N-Well CMOS process underpins nearly all modern digital ICs: **microprocessors** (Intel, AMD), **ASICs**, **FPGA logic fabrics**, **SRAM/DRAM peripherals**, and **mixed-signal SoCs**. It is the **default process** in foundry PDKs (e.g., TSMC, GlobalFoundries, Samsung) for technologies from 0.5 µm down to 28 nm planar nodes. For *deeply scaled* nodes (<28 nm), it evolves into **retrograde N-Well** and **triple-well** structures, but the foundational N-Well concept remains unchanged.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage of NMOS in the P-Substrate of an N-Well CMOS Process

We derive the **long-channel nMOS threshold voltage** $V_{TN}$ for the NMOS device built directly in the P-type substrate of the N-Well CMOS process.

### Step 1 — Define the flat-band voltage

$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}}$$

where $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$ is the work-function difference between the **n⁺ polysilicon gate** and the **P-silicon substrate**, and $Q_{ox}$ is the fixed oxide charge.

### Step 2 — Write the surface potential at strong inversion

At strong inversion, the surface bends by $2\phi_F$ below the bulk Fermi level:

$$\phi_{s,\text{inv}} = 2\phi_F$$

### Step 3 — Express the maximum depletion charge

Poisson's equation in the depletion region of the P-substrate (doping $N_A$) gives:

$$Q_{B,\max} = \sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the gate-voltage balance

The gate voltage must support the flat-band shift, the surface band-bending, and the depletion charge:

$$V_{GS} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{Q_{B,\max}}{C_{ox}}$$

### Step 5 — Identify the threshold condition

By definition, $V_{GS} = V_{TN}$ at the onset of strong inversion:

$$\boxed{\,V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}\,}$$

This is the **standard nMOS threshold voltage** used universally in CMOS VLSI textbooks and is a **direct KTU exam formula**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Junction Depth of the N-Well after Drive-In

The N-Well is formed by **ion implantation** followed by a high-temperature **drive-in diffusion**. The final junction depth is dominated by the drive-in step.

### Step 1 — Use the diffusion length relation

The dopant diffuses with diffusivity $D$ for time $t$. The characteristic diffusion length is:

$$L_D = \sqrt{D \cdot t}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the junction depth

The p-n junction between the N-Well and the P-substrate forms where the diffused n-type concentration equals the p-type background $N_A$:

$$N_D(x_j, t) = N_A$$

For a Gaussian diffusion profile, the junction occurs at:

$$\boxed{\,x_j = \sqrt{D \cdot t}\,\cdot \sqrt{2\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{Q_{total}}{N_A \sqrt{\pi D t}}\right)}\,}$$

For long drive-in times, the second factor approaches unity, recovering the well-known approximation:

$$x_j \approx \sqrt{D \cdot t}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Suppose: $D = 2 \times 10^{-13}$ cm²/s at 1100 °C, $t = 4$ hours = 14,400 s.

$$x_j \approx \sqrt{(2 \times 10^{-13}) \times (1.44 \times 10^{4})}$$

$$x_j \approx \sqrt{2.88 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 5.37 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{cm} \approx 0.54\,\mu m$$

> **Interpretation:** A 4-hour, 1100 °C drive-in gives an N-Well depth of **~0.54 µm**, which is typical for a 0.5 µm CMOS technology node.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sheet Resistance of the N⁺ Source/Drain Region

Used when computing parasitic S/D resistance in the KTU "Layout Design Rules" module.

### Step 1 — Junction sheet dopant

After implant of dose $\Phi$ (cm⁻²) with projected range $R_p$ and straggle $\Delta R_p$:

$$N_s = \Phi$$

### Step 2 — Sheet resistance

$$\boxed{\,R_s = \dfrac{1}{q\,\mu_n\,N_s}\,}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical evaluation

For nMOS N⁺ region: $\mu_n \approx 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\Phi = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻²:

$$R_s = \dfrac{1}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1350)(5 \times 10^{15})}$$

$$R_s = \dfrac{1}{1.08 \times 10^{0}} = 0.926\,\Omega/\square$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing N-Well vs P-Well

| Parameter | N-Well CMOS (N-Tub) | P-Well CMOS (P-Tub) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Substrate | P-type | N-type | — |
| Hosted nMOS in | P-substrate (native) | P-Well (implanted) | N-Well |
| Hosted pMOS in | N-Well (implanted) | N-substrate (native) | P-Well |
| nMOS mobility | Native (high) | Slightly degraded by well | **N-Well** |
| Latch-up robustness | Higher $R_{sub}$ | Lower $R_{sub}$ | **N-Well** |
| Body bias of nMOS | $V_{SS}$ | $V_{SS}$ (via P-Well) | Tie |
| Body bias of pMOS | $V_{DD}$ (via N-Well) | $V_{DD}$ | Tie |
| Process complexity | 1 well mask | 1 well mask | Tie |
| Radiation hardness | Better (epitaxial P-sub) | Weaker | **N-Well** |
| Modern usage | **Industry standard** | Obsolete (< 1 µm) | **N-Well** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 N-Well CMOS Process Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

The diagram below renders the full process flow as a Mermaid functional architecture. Each node represents a process module, and arrows show the linear sequence of mask and thermal steps.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start P-type Wafer]) --> stepB[Pad Oxide Growth 50 nm]
    stepB --> stepC[Si3N4 Deposition]
    stepC --> stepD{{Mask 1 N-Well Photolithography}}
    stepD --> stepE[Phosphorus Implant 100 keV 1e13 cm2]
    stepE --> stepF[N-Well Drive-in 1100 C 4 hr]
    stepF --> stepG[Strip Nitride LOCOS Oxidation]
    stepG --> stepH{{Mask 2 Active Area}}
    stepH --> stepI[Threshold Adjust Implant Boron]
    stepI --> stepJ[Gate Oxide Growth 10 nm]
    stepJ --> stepK[Polysilicon Deposition and Doping]
    stepK --> stepL{{Mask 3 Poly Gate Patterning}}
    stepL --> stepM{{Mask 4 N+ S and D Implant Arsenic}}
    stepM --> stepN{{Mask 5 P+ S and D Implant BF2}}
    stepN --> stepO[Sidewall Spacer Formation]
    stepO --> stepP{{Mask 6 Contact Cut}}
    stepP --> stepQ[Metal 1 Deposition]
    stepQ --> stepR{{Mask 7 Metal 1 Patterning}}
    stepR --> finishS([Passivation and Test])
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Completed N-Well CMOS Inverter

The figure below is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping the physical device structure of a CMOS inverter fabricated using the N-Well process. (Mermaid cannot render true semiconductor cross-sections, so we map regions as labeled blocks to convey the spatial layout.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph wafer[P-type Silicon Substrate]
        direction TB
        nmos1[NMOS Active Region in P-Sub]
        wellA[N-Well Region n-type]
        pmos1[PMOS Active Region inside N-Well]
    end
    nmos1 --- gate1[Poly Gate of NMOS shared]
    pmos1 --- gate2[Poly Gate of PMOS shared]
    gate1 --- gate2
    nmos1 --- nsd1[N+ Source and Drain of NMOS]
    pmos1 --- psd1[P+ Source and Drain of PMOS]
    wellA --- wellcontact[N-Well Contact tied to VDD]
    nsd1 --- gndrail[Ground Rail VSS]
    psd1 --- vddrail[VDD Rail]
    nmos1 -.- junc1[pn junction between N-Well and P-Sub forms parasitic bipolar]
    pmos1 -.- junc1
```

## 4.3 Mask Sequence & Layer Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1[Mask 1 N-Well] --> m2[Mask 2 Active Area]
    m2 --> m3[Mask 3 Poly Gate]
    m3 --> m4[Mask 4 N+ Source and Drain]
    m4 --> m5[Mask 5 P+ Source and Drain]
    m5 --> m6[Mask 6 Contact]
    m6 --> m7[Mask 7 Metal 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in all Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `stepB`, `wellA`, `m1`) prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. All labels are **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without markdown formatting, as required by the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the N-Well CMOS process. Why is it preferred over the P-Well process in modern VLSI?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The N-Well (N-Tub) CMOS process fabricates **PMOS transistors inside an N-type well** that is selectively implanted into a **P-type silicon substrate**, while **NMOS transistors are formed directly in the P-substrate**. It is preferred over the P-Well process for three reasons:

1. **Higher NMOS performance:** Electrons have ~2.8× higher mobility than holes, and the nMOS is hosted in the *native* P-substrate, preserving its full mobility.
2. **Better latch-up immunity:** The lightly doped P-substrate offers higher substrate resistance, suppressing the parasitic thyristor action.
3. **Process economy:** Only one well-mask step is needed; the nMOS does not require an additional well.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three masks used in the N-Well CMOS process and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mask | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Mask 1 – N-Well** | Defines the n-type well region for PMOS transistors (1 mark) |
| **Mask 3 – Poly Gate** | Patterns the polysilicon gate electrodes of both nMOS and pMOS (1 mark) |
| **Mask 4 – N⁺ S/D** | Opens the windows for arsenic implant to form NMOS source and drain (1 mark) |

*(Alternatively, P⁺ S/D mask or Contact mask may be quoted.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-section, explain the **fabrication of an N-Well CMOS inverter** step-by-step. List all the masks used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor** in the P-substrate of an N-Well CMOS process and explain how the **body effect** modifies this threshold when the source is not at substrate potential. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 – Substrate Preparation (1 mark):**
A P-type silicon wafer of doping $N_A \approx 10^{15}$ cm⁻³ is taken. A thin pad-oxide (~50 nm) is grown, followed by silicon-nitride deposition.

**Step 2 – N-Well Formation (1 mark):**
The first photolithographic step uses the **N-Well Mask (Mask 1)** to expose the well regions. A **phosphorus implant** (dose ≈ $10^{13}$ cm⁻², energy ≈ 100 keV) is performed, followed by **drive-in at 1100 °C for 4 hours** to form the well of depth ~0.5–1 µm.

**Step 3 – LOCOS Isolation (1 mark):**
The nitride is stripped and a **field oxide (~500 nm)** is grown selectively using the **Active-Area Mask (Mask 2)**, isolating the active transistor regions.

**Step 4 – Gate Formation (1 mark):**
A thin **gate oxide (~10 nm)** is grown, then **n⁺ polysilicon** is deposited and patterned using the **Poly-Gate Mask (Mask 3)** to form the shared gate of the inverter.

**Step 5 – Source/Drain Formation (1 mark):**
- The **N⁺ S/D Mask (Mask 4)** opens regions around the NMOS; an **arsenic implant** forms the NMOS source/drain.
- The **P⁺ S/D Mask (Mask 5)** opens regions around the PMOS (inside the N-Well); a **BF₂ implant** forms the PMOS source/drain.

**Step 6 – Contacts & Metallization (1 mark):**
Contacts are cut and aluminum is deposited and patterned using the **Contact Mask (Mask 6)** and **Metal Mask (Mask 7)**.

**Final Structure (1 mark):**
The cross-section shows: NMOS on the left in the P-substrate with N⁺ S/D; PMOS on the right inside the N-Well with P⁺ S/D; the poly gate spans both; the N-Well is tied to $V_{DD}$ via an N⁺ contact; the P-substrate is tied to $V_{SS}$ via a P⁺ contact.

**Total Masks = 7** (N-Well, Active, Poly, N⁺, P⁺, Contact, Metal-1).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The threshold voltage of an NMOS in the P-substrate is derived as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes:

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\,\varepsilon_{si}\,q\,N_A\,(2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}}$$

where
$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$, $\phi_F = (kT/q)\ln(N_A/n_i)$, and $C_{ox} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$.

**Body Effect Explanation (2 marks):**

When the source is biased at $V_S \neq 0$ (with substrate at 0 V), the source-to-substrate potential $V_{SB} = V_S$ increases the reverse bias on the source-substrate junction, widening the depletion region. The new threshold becomes:

$$V_{TN}' = V_{TN0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$

where $\gamma = \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A}/C_{ox}$ is the **body-effect coefficient** (V$^{1/2}$). This body effect is significant in **stacked transistors** (e.g., in NAND gates) and is a key design consideration in N-Well CMOS because the nMOS is in the *native* P-substrate that is common to the entire chip.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the **N-Well and P-Well CMOS processes** in terms of mobility, latch-up, process complexity, and modern usage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 0.5 µm N-Well CMOS process uses a **P-substrate doping of $N_A = 5 \times 10^{15}$ cm⁻³**, **gate-oxide thickness of 10 nm**, and **n⁺ poly gate**. Calculate the **threshold voltage $V_{TN}$** of the NMOS. Given: $\phi_{MS} = -0.95$ V, $Q_{ox}/C_{ox} = -0.1$ V, $n_i = 1.45 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³, $T = 300$ K. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Criterion | N-Well | P-Well | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mobility | nMOS in native P-sub (best) | pMOS in native N-sub (sub-optimal) | N-Well wins (1) |
| Latch-up | Higher $R_{sub}$, better immunity | Lower $R_{sub}$, more vulnerable | N-Well wins (1) |
| Process complexity | 1 well mask | 1 well mask (similar) | Tie (1) |
| Modern usage | Industry standard (all nodes) | Obsolete below 1 µm | N-Well wins (1) |
| Radiation hardness | Better (epitaxial option) | Weaker | N-Well wins (1) |
| Body-effect on faster device | None for nMOS (substrate tied) | None for pMOS | Symmetric (1) |
| Cost | Lower for digital CMOS | Higher | N-Well wins (1) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The N-Well process is the **universally preferred** architecture for submicron CMOS VLSI.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\phi_F$ (1 mark):**

$$\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5 \times 10^{15}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}}\right)$$

$$= 0.0259 \times \ln(3.448 \times 10^{5}) = 0.0259 \times 12.75 = 0.330\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{ox}$ (1 mark):**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{F/cm}}{10 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{cm}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{F/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{FB}$ (1 mark):**

$$V_{FB} = \phi_{MS} - \dfrac{Q_{ox}}{C_{ox}} = -0.95 - (-0.1) = -0.85\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the depletion term (2 marks):**

$$Q_{B,\max} = \sqrt{2 \times (1.04 \times 10^{-12}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5 \times 10^{15}) \times (2 \times 0.330)}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 1.04 \times 1.6 \times 5 \times 0.66 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$= \sqrt{1.099 \times 10^{-15}} = 3.32 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute $V_{TN}$ (2 marks):**

$$V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{Q_{B,\max}}{C_{ox}}$$

$$= -0.85 + 2(0.330) + \dfrac{3.32 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= -0.85 + 0.660 + 0.0962$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{TN} \approx -0.094\,\text{V} \approx -0.09\,\text{V}\,}$$

> **Interpretation:** A *slightly negative* threshold indicates that the device is **lightly depletion-mode** or that the threshold-adjust implant was not yet applied. In a real process, a **boron threshold-adjust implant** would shift $V_{TN}$ to a typical enhancement-mode value of **+0.5 V to +0.7 V**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $\phi_{MS}$:** Many students drop the negative sign of $\phi_{MS}$ for an n⁺ poly gate on P-substrate. Always verify using $\phi_{MS} = \phi_M - \phi_S$; for n⁺ poly on P-Si, $\phi_{MS}$ is **negative**.
> 2. **Unit of $C_{ox}$:** Expressing $C_{ox}$ in **F/cm²** (not F/m²) avoids a $10^4$ error in $V_{TN}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $2\phi_F$:** The depletion term uses **$2\phi_F$**, not $\phi_F$.
> 4. **Confusing $N_A$ and $N_D$:** The nMOS threshold uses the **P-substrate doping $N_A$**, *not* the N-Well doping $N_D$.
> 5. **Mask count:** Writing "5 masks" without specifying LOCOS/Active area loses a mark. Always state **N-Well, Active, Poly, N⁺, P⁺, Contact, Metal = 7 masks** for a full process.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **N-Well CMOS** = PMOS inside an N-type well, NMOS in native P-substrate.
- ✅ **N-Well is preferred** because electrons are ~2.8× faster than holes, so the *faster* nMOS must be hosted natively.
- ✅ **Standard mask sequence** = N-Well → Active → Poly → N⁺ S/D → P⁺ S/D → Contact → Metal (7 masks).
- ✅ **N-Well drive-in** is done at ~1100 °C for several hours; depth $x_j \approx \sqrt{Dt}$.
- ✅ **Body biasing convention:** N-Well → $V_{DD}$, P-Substrate → $V_{SS}$.
- ✅ **Threshold voltage formula:** $V_{TN} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$.
- ✅ **Body-effect equation:** $V_{TN}' = V_{TN0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$.
- ✅ **Sheet resistance:** $R_s = 1/(q\mu N_s)$; for N⁺ S/D, $R_s$ is typically ~1 Ω/□.
- ✅ **Latch-up** is suppressed by high $R_{sub}$ (lightly doped P-substrate) and by body ties.
- ✅ **LOCOS** (or modern STI) provides electrical isolation between adjacent transistors.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the **cross-section** with **N⁺, P⁺, poly, gate-oxide, well, and substrate** all clearly labeled.
- ✅ **Modern extension:** For sub-28 nm nodes, **retrograde wells** and **triple-well** structures evolve from the basic N-Well concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P-Tub and Twin Tub; MOS Circuit Layout - Stick Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** P-Tub and Twin Tub; MOS Circuit Layout - Stick Diagrams # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 P-Tub Process (P-Well CMOS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A *P-Tub* (or P-Well) CMOS process fabricates the **n-channel MOSFETs (NMOS)** inside a **p-type well (tub)** that is diffused into an **n-type epitaxial/substrate layer**, while the **p-channel MOSFETs (PMOS)** are built directly on the n-substrate. The P-tub is also called the **"tub"** because it acts as a "container" that isolates the NMOS transistors from the host n-type wafer. The p-tub is connected to the **lowest voltage (GND/0 V)** to keep the source-body junction reverse-biased, while the n-substrate is tied to **VDD**. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > Think of a swimming pool complex with two pools. Imagine the **n-type substrate** as a giant concrete deck (a "sea" of electrons). The **p-tub** is a small, sunken, separately-chlorinated pool carved into that deck where the NMOS swimmers (electrons as majority carriers) can move freely. The PMOS swimmers (holes) swim in the surrounding deck itself. Each "pool" can have its own water chemistry (doping level) tuned independently — but in the P-tub process, the deck chemistry is fixed and only the inner pool can be tuned, limiting flexibility. --- ## 1.2 N-Tub Process (N-Well CMOS) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A *N-Tub* (or N-Well) CMOS process fabricates the **p-channel MOSFETs (PMOS)** inside an **n-type well** diffused into a **p-type substrate**, while the **n-channel MOSFETs (NMOS)** are built directly on the p-substrate. This is the **mirror image** of the P-tub process. Historically, the N-tub process became the **industry standard** because electrons (NMOS carriers) have higher mobility in a lightly doped p-substrate, giving faster NMOS transistors. --- ## 1.3 Twin-Tub (Twin-Well) Process > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A *Twin-Tub* (or Twin-Well) CMOS process creates **both a p-tub and an n-tub** in a **lightly doped n-type or p-type epitaxial substrate**, allowing **independent optimization** of the doping concentration and threshold voltage of both NMOS and PMOS transistors. This is the **most advanced and flexible** of the three CMOS processes and is the basis of all modern sub-micron CMOS technologies (e.g., 180 nm, 65 nm, 28 nm, and below). ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > If the P-tub and N-tub processes are like having only **one tunable pool** in a fixed concrete deck, the **Twin-Tub** is like having **two independent, separately designed pools** (one for electrons, one for holes) carved into a clean, neutral floor. Both chemistries can be **tuned simultaneously** for the best performance, much like a high-end aquatics centre with Olympic-grade pools in parallel. --- ## 1.4 MOS Circuit Layout & Stick Diagrams > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** A *Stick Diagram* is a **simplified, color-coded, symbolic representation** of the physical layout of a CMOS circuit, in which each layer (polysilicon, diffusion, metal, contact) is represented by a **straight colored line (a "stick")** without regard to exact geometric dimensions. It is an **intermediate abstraction** between the circuit schematic and the full mask-level geometric layout. | Schematic → Stick Diagram → Mask Layout | |:---:| | (logical) → (symbolic) → (geometric) | ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > If a circuit schematic is a **musical score**, the stick diagram is the **lead sheet** showing which instruments play and in what order, and the mask layout is the **full orchestral arrangement** with exact note durations, rests, and dynamics. Stick diagrams are used by VLSI designers to **plan the floorplan** and the **Euler path** of polysilicon gates before drawing the full mask layout. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are Tubs Needed? In bulk CMOS, both NMOS and PMOS transistors must be **electrically isolated** from each other. Without tubs, you cannot have both transistor types on the same wafer. The tub serves three critical purposes: 1. **Junction Isolation** — The pn-junction between the tub and the substrate is reverse-biased, providing electrical isolation. 2. **Body Terminal Access** — Each tub provides a separate body (bulk) terminal so that the source-body voltage $V_{SB}$ can be controlled, reducing the **body effect**. 3. **Doping Control** — The tub's doping concentration $N_A$ (or $N_D$) can be tuned **independently** of the substrate to set the desired **threshold voltage** $V_{TH}$. ## 2.2 Comparison of CMOS Tub Processes | Parameter | **P-Tub (P-Well)** | **N-Tub (N-Well)** | **Twin-Tub (Twin-Well)** | |---|---|---|---| | NMOS location | Inside p-tub | Directly on p-substrate | Inside p-tub | | PMOS location | Directly on n-substrate | Inside n-tub | Inside n-tub | | Substrate type | n-type | p-type | Lightly doped n or p | | NMOS body contact | P-tub (tied to GND) | P-substrate (tied to GND) | P-tub (tied to GND) | | PMOS body contact | N-substrate (tied to VDD) | N-tub (tied to VDD) | N-tub (tied to VDD) | | Independent $V_{TH}$ tuning | Only NMOS | Only PMOS | Both NMOS and PMOS | | Latch-up susceptibility | Higher | Higher | Lower (with guard rings) | | Modern usage | Obsolete | Older processes | **Modern standard** | | Process complexity | Low | Low | High (extra masking step) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the P-tub process, since the n-substrate also acts as the PMOS body, any change to optimize NMOS $V_{TH}$ in the p-tub can **inadvertently alter** the PMOS $V_{TH}$. The Twin-Tub process eliminates this coupling. ## 2.3 Threshold Voltage with Body Effect — KTU Formula Sheet The threshold voltage of a MOS transistor is sensitive to the **source-body bias** $V_{SB}$, an effect known as the **body effect** (or back-bias effect). $$ V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right) $$ where: - $V_{TH0}$ = zero-bias threshold voltage (when $V_{SB} = 0$) - $\gamma$ = body-effect coefficient (in $\mathrm{V^{1/2}}$) - $\phi_F$ = Fermi potential of the body (in Volts) - $V_{SB}$ = source-to-body voltage (must be $\geq 0$ for an enhancement-mode NMOS) The body-effect coefficient is given by: $$ \gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\, 2 q \, \epsilon_s \, N_A \,}}{C_{ox}} = \frac{\sqrt{\, 2 q \, \epsilon_s \, N_A \,}}{\epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}} $$ where: - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \mathrm{C}$ (electron charge) - $\epsilon_s = 11.7 \, \epsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \; \mathrm{F/cm}$ (silicon permittivity) - $N_A$ = doping concentration of the body (in $\mathrm{cm^{-3}}$) - $C_{ox} = \epsilon_{ox}/t_{ox}$ = gate-oxide capacitance per unit area (in $\mathrm{F/cm^2}$) - $t_{ox}$ = gate-oxide thickness (in cm) - $\epsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \, \epsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-13} \; \mathrm{F/cm}$ (silicon dioxide permittivity) ### Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet (KTU Exam Ready) | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2q\epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | $\mathrm{V^{1/2}}$ | Quantifies $V_{TH}$ shift with $V_{SB}$ | | Threshold voltage | $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma\!\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F+V_{SB}\vert}-\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F\vert}\right)$ | V | Determines transistor turn-on | | Depletion width | $W_{dep} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\epsilon_s \phi_s}{qN_A}}$ | cm | Sets junction capacitance | | Oxide capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\epsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | $\mathrm{F/cm^2}$ | Sets drive current and speed | | Transconductance | $k_n' = \mu_n C_{ox}$ | $\mathrm{A/V^2}$ | Determines drain current | | Drain current (sat.) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | A | Sets switching performance | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Engineers use the body-effect equation to predict how $V_{TH}$ shifts when a transistor's source is not at the same potential as its body. This is critical in **dynamic logic**, **source followers**, and **cascode stages** where the source voltage swings above ground. ## 2.4 Stick Diagram — Layer Color Code (Standard CMOS) | Color | Layer | Function | |---|---|---| | 🟡 **Yellow / Gold** | Polysilicon (Poly) | Gate electrode; also used for short interconnects | | 🟢 **Green** | n$^+$ diffusion | Source / Drain of NMOS transistors | | 🔴 **Red / Brown** | p$^+$ diffusion | Source / Drain of PMOS transistors | | 🔵 **Blue** | Metal 1 (M1) | Primary horizontal interconnect | | 🔴 **Bright Red** | Metal 2 (M2) | Secondary vertical interconnect | | ⬛ **Black ✕ (cross)** | Contact cut | Vertical connection between Poly and M1, or diffusion and M1 | | ⬛ **Black solid square** | Buried contact | Direct Poly-to-diffusion contact (older processes) | | ⚪ **Black dashed line** | Well / Tub boundary | Indicates the edge of the p-tub or n-tub | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule:** In stick diagrams, **two sticks of the same layer crossing each other DO NOT connect**; only an **explicit contact cut (✕)** makes a vertical connection. Two **different layer** sticks crossing **do** connect (e.g., poly crossing diffusion **always** forms a transistor). ## 2.5 Rules for Drawing Stick Diagrams 1. **Poly over diffusion** = transistor (gate automatically formed). 2. **Same-layer crossing** = no connection (use a contact cut). 3. **Different-layer crossing** = connection at the crossing point. 4. **n$^+$ and p$^+$ regions must never touch directly** — they need a **metal bridge** or **substrate contact** in between to avoid a forward-biased pn-junction. 5. The **substrate (or tub) contact** must be placed for every transistor group. 6. The **VDD and GND rails** are drawn as **horizontal metal lines** at the top and bottom. 7. Use an **Euler path** to minimize the number of poly breaks and improve layout density. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Body-Effect Coefficient We start with the **depletion-region charge per unit area** in the body of a MOS transistor under the gate: $$ Q_B = -q N_A W_{dep} $$ where $W_{dep}$ is the depletion-region width. Solving the 1-D Poisson equation in the depletion region, the surface potential $\phi_s$ required to deplete a width $W_{dep}$ of doping $N_A$ is: $$ \phi_s = \frac{q N_A W_{dep}^2}{2 \epsilon_s} $$ Solving this for $W_{dep}$ gives: $$ W_{dep} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s \phi_s}{q N_A}} $$ Substituting back into $Q_B$: $$ Q_B = -q N_A \sqrt{\frac{2 \epsilon_s \phi_s}{q N_A}} = -\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A \phi_s} $$ The **threshold voltage** of an NMOS transistor is the gate voltage required to make the surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ (strong inversion) plus the voltage drop across the oxide: $$ V_{TH} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A \, (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox}} $$ where $V_{FB}$ is the flat-band voltage. Now, when a source-body bias $V_{SB}$ is applied, the body-to-source junction adds $V_{SB}$ to the surface potential required for inversion, so $\phi_s = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$: $$ V_{TH}(V_{SB}) = V_{FB} + (2\phi_F + V_{SB}) + \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A \, (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}} $$ Subtracting the zero-bias case ($V_{SB}=0$) eliminates $V_{FB}$ and the $2\phi_F$ term: $$ \Delta V_{TH} = V_{TH}(V_{SB}) - V_{TH0} = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}} \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right) $$ Defining the **body-effect coefficient** $\gamma$: $$ \boxed{\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{2 q \epsilon_s N_A}}{C_{ox}}} $$ we recover the KTU formula: $$ V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert \,} - \sqrt{\, \vert 2\phi_F \vert \,} \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A **higher tub doping $N_A$** → larger $\gamma$ → **more body effect** → larger $V_{TH}$ shift. In twin-tub processes, designers choose **moderate $N_A$ in the p-tub** to keep $\gamma$ small for the NMOS, which often switches the source above ground. ## 3.2 Euler Path Method for Stick Diagrams (Step-by-Step Algorithm) The **Euler path** of a CMOS logic gate is a path through the **pull-up and pull-down graphs** that traverses every transistor **exactly once**. The Euler path directly determines the order in which polysilicon gates appear in the stick diagram, and finding a valid Euler path **minimizes the number of poly breaks**, yielding a compact layout. ### Algorithm (KTU 2024 Standard) **Step 1:** Draw the **pull-up graph (PUN)** using PMOS transistors in parallel branches, and the **pull-down graph (PDN)** using NMOS transistors in series branches. **Step 2:** Treat each transistor as an **edge** of a graph and each circuit node (VDD, OUT, intermediate nodes, GND) as a **vertex**. **Step 3:** Compute the **degree of each vertex** (number of transistors connected to it). An Euler path exists if and only if **zero or two vertices have odd degree**. **Step 4:** If two vertices have odd degree, the Euler path **starts at one and ends at the other**. If zero, the path is a closed Euler **circuit**. **Step 5:** Trace a path that visits every edge exactly once, **alternating** between PUN and PDN edges with the **same input signal** wherever possible. **Step 6:** Lay out the transistors **along the Euler path** in the stick diagram. The poly gate of each transistor crosses the diffusion at the position dictated by the path. ## 3.3 Worked Example: CMOS NAND Gate Stick Diagram The CMOS NAND gate has the following structure: $$ \text{NAND}(A, B) = \overline{A \cdot B} $$ - **Pull-up network (PUN):** Two PMOS transistors **in parallel** between VDD and OUT, gated by A and B. - **Pull-down network (PDN):** Two NMOS transistors **in series** between OUT and GND, gated by A and B. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1:** Identify graph vertices and edges. | Vertex | Connected Transistors | Degree | |---|---|---| | VDD | PMOS_A, PMOS_B | 2 (even) | | OUT | PMOS_A, PMOS_B, NMOS_A | 3 (odd) | | X (mid-node) | NMOS_A, NMOS_B | 2 (even) | | GND | NMOS_B | 1 (odd) | **Step 2:** Two vertices have odd degree (OUT and GND) → **Euler path exists from OUT to GND** (or vice versa). **Step 3:** Trace the Euler path: $$ \text{OUT} \xrightarrow{\text{PMOS}_A \,(A)} \text{VDD} \xleftarrow{\text{PMOS}_B \,(B)} \text{OUT} \xrightarrow{\text{NMOS}_A \,(A)} X \xrightarrow{\text{NMOS}_B \,(B)} \text{GND} $$ **Step 4:** The order of polysilicon gate signals along the path is: **A, B** (from OUT to GND) or **B, A** (from GND to OUT). The diffusion regions are laid out so that: - The **p$^+$** region runs along the top (VDD-rail → OUT) and contains the two PMOS transistors in parallel. - The **n$^+$** region runs along the bottom (OUT → GND) and contains the two NMOS transistors in series. - A **metal line** connects the p$^+$ OUT node to the n$^+$ OUT node. **Step 5:** Add VDD and GND metal rails, the OUT metal rail, and place the substrate/tub contacts. ### Resulting Stick Diagram (textual representation) ``` VDD (Metal1) ───────────────────────────────────── │ ✕ │ ✕ ┌─┴─────────────────┴─┐ p+ diffusion → │ PMOS_A │ │ PMOS_B │ ← p+ diffusion │ ┃ A │ │ ┃ B │ └───╂─────┘ └───╂──────┘ ┃ ┃ (Poly A and Poly B ┃ ┃ continue downward) OUT (Metal1) ──────╂────────────╂──────── ┃ ┃ ┌───╂────────────╂───┐ n+ diffusion → │ NMOS_A │ │ NMOS_B │ ← n+ diffusion │ ┃ A │ │ ┃ B │ │ ┃ │ │ ┃ │ └───┴────┘ └───┴────┘ ✕ ✕ GND (Metal1) ───────────────────────────────────── ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **two PMOS transistors share a single p$^+$ strip** (parallel connection), and the **two NMOS transistors share a single n$^+$ strip with a node X in between** (series connection). The poly lines for A and B run **vertically** through both n$^+$ and p$^+$ regions, forming the gates. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: P-Tub vs N-Tub vs Twin-Tub | Criterion | P-Tub (P-Well) | N-Tub (N-Well) | Twin-Tub (Twin-Well) | |---|---|---|---| | Mask count | 1 well mask | 1 well mask | **2 well masks** | | Independent $V_{TH}$ for NMOS | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Independent $V_{TH}$ for PMOS | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Substrate bias flexibility | Limited | Limited | **Full** | | Susceptibility to latch-up | High | High | Low (with epi) | | Used in modern nanometer CMOS | ❌ | Sometimes as base | ✅ **Standard** | | Body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ for NMOS | Tunable via p-tub | Fixed by substrate | **Tunable via p-tub** | | Body-effect coefficient $\gamma$ for PMOS | Fixed by n-substrate | Tunable via n-tub | **Tunable via n-tub** | | Process complexity (relative) | 1× | 1× | ~1.3× | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Twin-Tub Process — Cross-Sectional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **architectural layers** of a Twin-Tub CMOS process. (Physical cross-sections cannot be drawn in Mermaid, so the block-level functional architecture is shown instead.) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SUB["Lightly Doped n-type Substrate (epi layer)"] direction TB A1["Substrate Contact (n+) tied to VDD"] end subgraph NTUB["n-Tub Region (hosts PMOS)"] direction TB B1["n-Tub Contact (n+) tied to VDD"] B2["p+ Source"] B3["p+ Drain"] B4["PMOS Gate (Polysilicon)"] end subgraph PTUB["p-Tub Region (hosts NMOS)"] direction TB C1["p-Tub Contact (p+) tied to GND"] C2["n+ Source"] C3["n+ Drain"] C4["NMOS Gate (Polysilicon)"] end A1 -. forms junction with .-> C1 NTUB --- PTUB B4 --> B2 B4 --> B3 C4 --> C2 C4 --> C3 ``` > [!NOTE] > The arrows denote **electrical connection** (gate controls source/drain), and the dashed line denotes the **reverse-biased pn-junction isolation** between the p-tub and the n-type substrate. The two tubs are **side-by-side** and each is independently doped. ## 4.2 Stick Diagram — Construction Flow for a CMOS Inverter ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Step 1: Draw circuit schematic
VDD-PMOS-NMOS-GND
