<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the two fundamental design rules of static CMOS logic that guarantee zero static power dissipation.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **No DC Path Rule:** For every valid input combination, there must exist *no* direct conducting path between $V_{DD}$ and GND. The PUN and PDN must be **logically dual** — they are never simultaneously ON.
> 2. **Active Drive Rule:** The output node must be connected to *either* $V_{DD}$ *or* GND through a low-resistance path in every steady state — never left floating. This ensures a **full rail-to-rail output swing** ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0$).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Why is the NAND gate preferred over the NOR gate in static CMOS designs of identical fan-in?**

**Model Answer:**

> In a **2-input NOR gate**, the PMOS pull-up network uses **two PMOS transistors in series**. Since hole mobility $\mu_p \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\mu_n$, each series PMOS must be widened to compensate, leading to **larger area and slower rise time**.
>
> In contrast, the **2-input NAND gate** uses series **NMOS** transistors (faster device) in the pull-down path. Series NMOS is faster than series PMOS because of higher electron mobility. Hence, **NAND gates occupy less silicon area, switch faster, and have better noise margins** for the same fan-in. This makes NAND the preferred gate in deep-submicron static CMOS design.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> • In $V_M$ derivation questions, students frequently forget to **take the square root** of $k_p/k_n$ and lose 4 marks.
> • In sizing questions, students wrongly make *all* transistors wider instead of only the **series-stacked** transistors — losing 3 marks.
> • Marks are also deducted if $V_T$ is used **without absolute value** for PMOS.
> • Always include **units** in numerical answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the expression for the switching threshold $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. How does the PMOS-to-NMOS sizing ratio $(W_p/L_p)/(W_n/L_n)$ affect $V_M$?  (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of $V_M$:** The switching threshold is the input voltage $V_{in} = V_M$ at which $V_{out} = V_{in}$ and both transistors operate in saturation with equal drain currents.

**Step 2 — Current Equations:**

$$ I_{DN,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GSn} - V_{T_n})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{T_n})^2 $$

$$ I_{DP,sat} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SGp} - \vert V_{T_p}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{T_p}\vert)^2 $$

**Step 3 — Equate Currents:**

$$ \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{T_n})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{T_p}\vert)^2 $$

**Step 4 — Solve by Taking Square Root:**

$$ \sqrt{k_n}(V_M - V_{T_n}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{T_p}\vert) $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for $V_M$:**

$$ V_M(\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{T_n} + \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - \vert V_{T_p}\vert) $$

$$ V_M = \frac{V_{T_n} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{T_p}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}} $$

**Step 6 — Effect of Sizing Ratio:** As $\dfrac{W_p/L_p}{W_n/L_n}$ increases, the ratio $k_p/k_n$ increases, causing $V_M$ to shift **upward** (closer to $V_{DD}$). Conversely, decreasing $W_p$ shifts $V_M$ **downward**. For symmetric switching at $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, the sizing ratio is set such that:

$$ \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{T_n}}{V_{DD}/2 - \vert V_{T_p}\vert} $$

---

#### (b) Design a static CMOS gate that realizes $Y = \overline{A + B \cdot C}$. Draw the transistor schematic and explain its operation for all input combinations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify PDN (realizes $A + B \cdot C$):**

- NMOS for $A$ in **parallel** with the series combination of NMOS for $B$ and $C$.

**Step 2 — Identify PUN (realizes $\overline{A + BC}$, i.e., $\bar{A} \cdot (\bar{B} + \bar{C})$):**

- PMOS for $A$ in **series** with the parallel combination of PMOS for $B$ and $C$.

**Step 3 — Transistor Schematic (logical representation):**

- **PDN:** N1 (gate = A) ∥ [N2 (B) — N3 (C) series]
- **PUN:** P1 (A) — [P2 (B) ∥ P3 (C)]

**Step 4 — Truth Table Verification:**

| A | B | C | PDN Path | PUN Path | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | OFF | ON (P1 series; P2,P3 parallel ON) | 1 |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | OFF | ON | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | ON (N1) | OFF | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | ON (N1) | OFF | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | ON (N1) | OFF | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | ON (N1 + series B,C) | OFF | 0 |

**Step 5 — Verification:** When $A=0$, both $B$ and $C$ are "don't care" for the PDN, and P1 (PMOS) conducts — output = $V_{DD}$ = 1. When $A=1$, N1 pulls output to 0 regardless of B, C — output = 0. This correctly realizes $Y = \overline{A + BC}$.

**Step 6 — Sizing:** N1 (single parallel) = 1× width. N2, N3 (series pair) = 2× width each. P1 (series) = 2× width. P2, P3 (parallel) = 1× width each.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### (a) Explain with a neat schematic the operation of a static CMOS 2-input NAND gate. Also compute the noise margins assuming $V_{DD} = 2.5$ V, $V_{T_n} = 0.5$ V, $|V_{T_p}| = 0.6$ V, $k_n = 100\ \mu A/V^2$, $k_p = 50\ \mu A/V^2$. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Schematic Description:** The 2-input NAND has:
- **PUN:** Two PMOS in parallel (each gated by A and B).
- **PDN:** Two NMOS in series.

**Step 2 — Operation:** When $A = B = 1$, both NMOS conduct → output pulled to 0. For any other input combination, at least one PMOS conducts → output pulled to $V_{DD}$.

**Step 3 — Switching Threshold Computation:**

$$ \sqrt{k_p/k_n} = \sqrt{50/100} = \sqrt{0.5} = 0.7071 $$

$$ V_M = \frac{0.5 + 0.7071 \cdot (2.5 - 0.6)}{1 + 0.7071} = \frac{0.5 + 1.3435}{1.7071} = \frac{1.8435}{1.7071} \approx 1.080 \text{ V} $$

**Step 4 — Noise Margin Approximation (using standard KTU model):**

For a first-order estimate, with $V_M = 1.08$ V:

$$ V_{IL} \approx \frac{V_{DD}/2 + V_{T_n} - |V_{T_p}|}{2} \approx \frac{1.25 + 0.5 - 0.6}{2} \approx 0.575 \text{ V} $$

$$ V_{IH} \approx \frac{3V_{DD}/2 - V_{T_n} + |V_{T_p}|}{2} \approx \frac{3.75 - 0.5 + 0.6}{2} \approx 1.925 \text{ V} $$

$$ NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 0.575 - 0 = 0.575 \text{ V} $$

$$ NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.925 = 0.575 \text{ V} $$

**Step 5 — Observation:** $V_M \neq V_{DD}/2$, indicating an asymmetric inverter — the PMOS is too weak relative to NMOS. To balance, the PMOS must be widened by a factor:

$$ \text{width multiplier} = \left(\frac{V_{DD}/2 - V_{T_n}}{V_{DD}/2 - |V_{T_p}|}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} $$

---

#### (b) Discuss the sources of power dissipation in static CMOS circuits. Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation and state two techniques to reduce it. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sources of Power Dissipation:**

1. **Dynamic (switching) power** — due to charging/discharging of load capacitances.
2. **Short-circuit power** — due to momentary current during input transitions when both NMOS and PMOS are simultaneously ON.
3. **Static (leakage) power** — subthreshold + gate leakage, even when the gate is idle.

**Step 2 — Dynamic Power Derivation:**

For a single 0→1 transition, the energy drawn from $V_{DD}$ to charge $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$ is:

$$ E_{VDD} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot dV = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$

Half of this energy is stored on the capacitor, the other half is dissipated in the PMOS resistance:

$$ E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \quad ; \quad E_{dissipated} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$

For $\alpha$ transitions per clock cycle at frequency $f_{clk}$:

$$ P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} $$

**Step 3 — Two Reduction Techniques:**

1. **Voltage Scaling:** Since $P \propto V_{DD}^2$, reducing $V_{DD}$ from 2.5 V to 1.2 V yields a $\sim 4.3\times$ reduction in dynamic power. (Used in **DVFS** — Dynamic Voltage Frequency Scaling in modern SoCs.)
2. **Clock Gating:** Disabling the clock to idle circuit blocks eliminates $\alpha$ transitions, saving power without affecting functionality. (Used in nearly every mobile CPU/GPU.)

**Step 4 — Bonus Mention:** Activity factor reduction, multi-Vt libraries, and power gating are additional industry-standard techniques.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Static MOS**

- **Static CMOS = Complementary PUN (PMOS) + PDN (NMOS)** — never ON together.
- **Zero static current** in the ideal case → zero standby power.
- **Output swing:** $V_{OL} = 0$, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (full rail-to-rail).
- **Switching threshold:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{T_n} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{T_p}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$
- **Symmetric inverter condition:** $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ requires $k_p \approx k_n$, hence $\dfrac{W_p}{W_n} \approx \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2$ to $3$.
- **Series NMOS sizing** — multiply width by $N$ for $N$ transistors in series.
- **Parallel transistors** — sizing remains 1× (single device drives full current).
- **NAND vs NOR:** NAND uses series NMOS (faster) → **preferred** in deep-submicron.
- **Noise margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$.
- **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$.
- **Dual-network rule:** PUN of $F$ = PDN of $F'$ (topology preserved with PMOS replacing NMOS).
- **No floating outputs** — every output node is always tied to one rail.

> **Mastery Tip:** Practice the $V_M$ derivation at least 5 times — it is the single most repeated question across KTU past papers for VLSI Design.

---

topics.title: Complementary MOS - Ratioed Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Complementary MOS - Ratioed Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary MOS (CMOS)** is a static VLSI logic family in which the pull-up network (PUN) and pull-down network (PDN) are constructed using **dual, complementary transistors** (PMOS for pull-up, NMOS for pull-down). For every input combination, **exactly one** of the two networks conducts, connecting the output either to **VDD (logic 1)** or **GND (logic 0)**, but never both. Because steady-state current ideally never flows from VDD to GND, CMOS is called **ratio-less logic** — the geometric ratio of PMOS to NMOS transistor sizes does *not* determine the logic levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ratioed Logic** refers to a class of static CMOS-style gates (e.g., **Pseudo-nMOS**, **DCVSL**) in which the output low level (VOL) and noise margins **depend on the geometric ratio** of the pull-up device strength to the pull-down device strength. This is in direct contrast to pure CMOS where the ratio is irrelevant to logic correctness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap System

Think of a bathroom shower with **two perfectly matched handles** — one for hot water, one for cold water:
- In a true **CMOS gate**, whenever the "hot" (PMOS pull-up) valve opens, the "cold" (NMOS pull-down) valve **closes completely**, and vice-versa. Water (current) only flows briefly during the *switch-over* (switching transient), not in steady state. The sizes of the valves don't matter — both are guaranteed to be either fully open or fully closed.
- In a **ratioed logic gate** (pseudo-nMOS), imagine the cold-water valve is *always* open a little. The hot valve must overpower the cold one to deliver hot water (high output). If the cold valve is too big, you'll only get lukewarm water — that's why the **size ratio** matters here.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **VDD** : Supply voltage (typically **1.8 V – 5 V**)
- **Vth_n** : NMOS threshold voltage (**≈ 0.4–0.7 V** for modern processes)
- **Vth_p** : PMOS threshold voltage (**≈ −0.4 to −0.7 V**)
- **VOL, VOH** : Output Low / Output High voltages
- **VIL, VIH** : Input Low / Input High thresholds
- **NMH, NML** : Noise Margins (High and Low)
- **k_n, k_p** : Process transconductance parameters for NMOS / PMOS
- **r_n, r_p** : Equivalent ON-resistances of NMOS / PMOS

## 1.4 Topic Map (Module 3 Context)

| Sub-topic | Coverage in this Note |
|---|---|
| CMOS Inverter | ✓ Detailed |
| CMOS NAND / NOR | ✓ Included |
| Compound CMOS Gates | ✓ AOI / OAI |
| Pseudo-nMOS | ✓ Detailed |
| DCVSL | ✓ Detailed |
| Pass Transistor Logic | ✓ Brief reference |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CMOS Inverter — Operational Logic

The CMOS inverter consists of:
- **PMOS transistor (Mp)** with source connected to **VDD**, gate to input **VIN**, drain to output **VOUT**.
- **NMOS transistor (Mn)** with source connected to **GND**, gate to input **VIN**, drain to output **VOUT**.

**Operating regions:**

1. **VIN = 0 (Logic 0):**
   - PMOS is ON (since VGS_p = −VDD < Vth_p)
   - NMOS is OFF (since VGS_n = 0 < Vth_n)
   - Output is pulled up to VDD → **VOUT = VOH = VDD**

2. **VIN = VDD (Logic 1):**
   - PMOS is OFF (since VGS_p = 0 > Vth_p)
   - NMOS is ON (since VGS_n = VDD > Vth_n)
   - Output is pulled down to GND → **VOUT = VOL = 0**

3. **Switching region (0 < VIN < VDD):**
   - Both transistors are in saturation, output transitions sharply.

## 2.2 Why CMOS is Ratio-Less

Because the PMOS and NMOS are **never simultaneously ON** in steady state, the ON-resistance of one network does not "fight" the other through a DC path. Hence, the **W/L ratio of PMOS to NMOS does not affect logic levels** — it only affects *speed* and *drive strength*.

## 2.3 CMOS NAND and NOR Gates

**CMOS NAND (2-input):**
- 2 PMOS in **parallel** (pull-up)
- 2 NMOS in **series** (pull-down)

**CMOS NOR (2-input):**
- 2 PMOS in **series** (pull-up)
- 2 NMOS in **parallel** (pull-down)

**General Rule:**
- **NMOS in series ↔ AND** (must all be ON for conduction)
- **NMOS in parallel ↔ OR**
- **PMOS network is the dual** of the NMOS network.

## 2.4 Ratioed Logic Families — Operational Logic

### (a) Pseudo-nMOS Logic

- A **single PMOS transistor with gate tied to GND** acts as a *constant load* (always ON, in linear/saturation region).
- The PDN is built from NMOS transistors implementing the logic function.
- When the PDN is OFF, output rises to VDD via the PMOS load.
- When the PDN is ON, a **DC current flows** from VDD through PMOS to GND through NMOS, and the output settles at some **VOL > 0**.
- VOL depends on the **ratio** (k_p/k_n) of the load PMOS to the pull-down NMOS.

### (b) DCVSL (Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic)

- Uses **two NMOS pull-down trees** that compute the function and its complement.
- The load is a **cross-coupled PMOS latch**.
- Output and its complement are available simultaneously.
- Fully differential, but still has a static current path during switching.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CMOS Static Power | $P_{static} = I_{static} \cdot V_{DD} \approx 0$ | Watts (ideal) |
| 2 | CMOS Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts |
| 3 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = I_{peak} \cdot V_{DD} \cdot t_{sc} \cdot f$ | Watts |
| 4 | Switching Threshold (βR = k_n/k_p) | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{th,n} + \sqrt{\beta_R}\,(V_{DD}-\vert V_{th,p}\vert)}{1+\sqrt{\beta_R}}$ | Volts |
| 5 | Noise Margin High | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Noise Margin Low | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Volts |
| 7 | Pseudo-nMOS VOL (approx.) | $V_{OL} \approx \dfrac{V_{th,n}}{1 + \sqrt{k_n/k_p} \cdot \sqrt{r)}}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Input Low Voltage (VIL) | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{2V_{OUT} + V_{th,p} - V_{DD} + \beta_R V_{th,n}}{1+\beta_R}$ | Volts |
| 9 | Input High Voltage (VIH) | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{V_{DD} + V_{th,p} + \beta_R (2V_{OUT}+V_{th,n})}{1+\beta_R}$ | Volts |
| 10 | CMOS Propagation Delay | $t_p = \dfrac{0.69 \cdot C_L}{2}(R_{n}+R_{p})$ | seconds |
| 11 | Pseudo-nMOS Power (static) | $P_{static} = I_{DC} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Watts (non-zero) |
| 12 | Resistive Ratio (Pseudo-nMOS) | $r = \dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** For questions on noise margins, examiners expect students to compute **NMH and NML explicitly** by finding VIL and VIH using the βR relationship. Memorize Formula #8 and #9 above.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CMOS** is the *industry standard* for nearly all digital ICs (microprocessors, memory, ASICs) due to its near-zero static power consumption — critical for **battery-powered mobile devices and IoT sensors**.
- **Pseudo-nMOS** is used in very specific high-speed circuits such as **static ROMs**, **PLAs**, and the **address decoding** of certain SRAM arrays where its smaller area (one PMOS instead of many) and higher speed outweigh the static power penalty.
- **DCVSL** is used in **domino logic pre-charge stages** and **differential signal processing** paths where complementary outputs are simultaneously needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CMOS Switching Threshold (V_M)

The switching threshold **V_M** is the point on the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) where **VIN = VOUT = V_M**, and both transistors operate in **saturation**.

**Step 1 — Write the saturation current expressions for both transistors:**

For NMOS in saturation:
$$I_{D,n} = \frac{k_n}{2} \cdot (V_{GS,n} - V_{th,n})^{2} = \frac{k_n}{2} \cdot (V_M - V_{th,n})^{2}$$

For PMOS in saturation:
$$I_{D,p} = \frac{k_p}{2} \cdot (V_{GS,p} - V_{th,p})^{2} = \frac{k_p}{2} \cdot (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p})^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the output node (no current leaves to C_L in steady DC analysis):**

$$I_{D,n} = I_{D,p}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{th,n})^{2} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p})^{2}$$

Define $\beta_R = \dfrac{k_n}{k_p}$:

$$\sqrt{\beta_R}\,(V_M - V_{th,n}) = (V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p})$$

(since $V_{GS,p} = V_M - V_{DD}$, and for PMOS $V_{th,p} < 0$, so $(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p})$ is positive during switching)

**Step 4 — Expand and collect V_M terms:**

$$\sqrt{\beta_R} \cdot V_M - \sqrt{\beta_R} \cdot V_{th,n} = V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p}$$

$$V_M(1 - \sqrt{\beta_R}) = V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R} - V_{DD} - V_{th,p}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by −1 and divide:**

$$V_M(\sqrt{\beta_R} - 1) = V_{DD} + V_{th,p} - V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R}$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$V_M = \frac{V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R} + V_{DD} + V_{th,p}}{\sqrt{\beta_R}+1} \equiv \frac{V_{DD} + V_{th,p} + \sqrt{\beta_R}\,V_{th,n}}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> To make V_M symmetric at VDD/2, designers choose **βR ≈ 1**, i.e., the NMOS is made **2–3× wider** than PMOS (since μn ≈ 2–3 μp).

## 3.2 Derivation: Pseudo-nMOS VOL

In pseudo-nMOS, when output is low, the PMOS load operates in **triode (linear) region** and the NMOS pulldown operates in **saturation**.

**Step 1 — PMOS current (triode, VSD small):**

$$I_{Dp} = k_p \left[(V_{SG} - \vert V_{th,p}\vert)V_{SD} - \frac{V_{SD}^{2}}{2}\right]$$

With $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{SD} = V_{DD} - V_{OL}$:

$$I_{Dp} = k_p\left[(V_{DD} - \vert V_{th,p}\vert)(V_{DD}-V_{OL}) - \frac{(V_{DD}-V_{OL})^{2}}{2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — NMOS current (saturation):**

$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{th,n})^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply I_Dp = I_Dn:**

For simplicity, assuming small VOL and strong PMOS, the commonly quoted **simplified ratioed result** is:

$$V_{OL} \approx \frac{(V_{DD} - V_{th,n})}{\sqrt{r}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}$$

where $r = \dfrac{(W/L)_n}{(W/L)_p}$ is the **ratio of NMOS strength to PMOS strength**. The larger the NMOS device (larger r), the lower (better) VOL becomes — but at the cost of more DC current.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CMOS vs Pseudo-nMOS vs DCVSL

| Parameter | CMOS (Ratio-less) | Pseudo-nMOS | DCVSL |
|---|---|---|---|
| PMOS Count | N (one per input) | **1** (always ON) | 2 (cross-coupled) |
| NMOS Count | N | N | 2N (dual trees) |
| Static Power | **≈ 0** (ideal) | High (DC path) | Lower than pseudo-nMOS |
| VOL | **0** | > 0 (ratio-dependent) | ≈ 0 |
| VOH | VDD | VDD | VDD |
| Complementary Outputs | No (need inverter) | No | **Yes** |
| Noise Margin | Excellent | Reduced | Good |
| Area | Larger | **Smallest** | Medium |
| Speed | Moderate | **Fast** (low C_load) | Fast |
| Used In | Universal logic | ROM, PLA, mux | High-speed diff. paths |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A CMOS inverter is fabricated in a 0.25 μm process with V_DD = 2.5 V, V_th,n = 0.4 V, V_th,p = −0.4 V, k_n = 120 μA/V², k_p = 40 μA/V². Determine the switching threshold V_M.

**Solution:**

$$\beta_R = \frac{k_n}{k_p} = \frac{120}{40} = 3.0$$

$$\sqrt{\beta_R} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$V_M = \frac{V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R} + V_{DD} + V_{th,p}}{1+\sqrt{\beta_R}}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.4 \times 1.732 + 2.5 + (-0.4)}{1 + 1.732}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6928 + 2.5 - 0.4}{2.732} = \frac{2.7928}{2.732}$$

$$\boxed{V_M \approx 1.022 \text{ V}}$$

Since VDD/2 = 1.25 V, the threshold is below the ideal midpoint — designers would increase PMOS width to raise V_M.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!NOTE]
> The two PMOS are in **parallel** (either one ON connects output to VDD), and the two NMOS are in **series** (both must be ON to pull output to GND). This realizes the NAND function: Y = NOT(A AND B).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define CMOS logic and explain why it is called ratio-less logic.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

CMOS (Complementary MOS) logic uses a **PMOS pull-up network (PUN)** and an **NMOS pull-down network (PDN)** such that for any input combination, exactly one of the networks conducts, connecting the output to either **VDD** or **GND**, but never both simultaneously.

It is called **ratio-less** because the output levels **VOL** and **VOH** are independent of the **W/L ratios** of the transistors — these ratios affect only switching speed, drive strength, and the V_M point, not the logic correctness. In steady state, no DC current flows between VDD and GND, giving **near-zero static power consumption**.

---

### Q2. With a neat diagram, explain pseudo-nMOS logic. Mention its main drawback.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Pseudo-nMOS uses a **single PMOS transistor with its gate permanently tied to GND** as a load device (always ON), replacing the entire PMOS pull-up network of a CMOS gate. The NMOS pull-down network implements the desired logic function.

When the NMOS network is OFF, the PMOS pulls the output up to VDD. When the NMOS network is ON, a **DC current path** exists between VDD and GND, causing static power dissipation.

**Main drawback:** Continuous DC current flow (static power consumption) and ratio-dependent VOL — sizing of transistors directly affects logic levels, reducing noise margins.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a CMOS inverter and explain its operation in detail with the help of the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the switching threshold V_M of a CMOS inverter. Calculate V_M for V_DD = 3.3 V, V_th,n = 0.6 V, V_th,p = −0.7 V, and β_R = k_n/k_p = 2.5. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 3]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CMOS Inverter Circuit & VTC:**

- **Circuit:** A PMOS with source at VDD, gate at VIN, drain at VOUT; an NMOS with source at GND, gate at VIN, drain at VOUT. Gates are tied together.
- **VTC Description:**
  - **Region A (VIN < V_th,n):** NMOS OFF, PMOS in triode; VOUT = VOH = VDD.
  - **Region B (V_th,n ≤ VIN ≤ V_M):** NMOS in saturation, PMOS in triode; VOUT begins to drop.
  - **Region C (VIN = V_M):** Both in saturation; VOUT = VIN = V_M (switching threshold).
  - **Region D (V_M ≤ VIN ≤ V_DD + V_th,p):** NMOS in triode, PMOS in saturation.
  - **Region E (VIN > V_DD + V_th,p):** PMOS OFF, NMOS in triode; VOUT = VOL = 0.
- The transition is **sharp** between VIL and VIH, giving excellent noise margins.

**(b) Derivation of V_M:**

Both transistors operate in **saturation** at the switching threshold. Equating currents:

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{th,n})^{2} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_M - V_{DD} - V_{th,p})^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{\beta_R}\,(V_M - V_{th,n}) = V_{DD} + V_{th,p} - V_M$$

Solving for V_M:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R} + V_{DD} + V_{th,p}}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: V_DD = 3.3 V, V_th,n = 0.6 V, V_th,p = −0.7 V, β_R = 2.5, √β_R ≈ 1.581.

$$V_M = \frac{(0.6)(1.581) + 3.3 + (-0.7)}{1 + 1.581}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.9486 + 3.3 - 0.7}{2.581} = \frac{3.5486}{2.581}$$

$$\boxed{V_M \approx 1.375 \text{ V}}$$

Since V_DD/2 = 1.65 V, the threshold is skewed low; designer would enlarge PMOS to raise V_M.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain DCVSL (Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic) with a suitable diagram. List its advantages over pseudo-nMOS. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare CMOS, pseudo-nMOS and DCVSL logic families in terms of static power, area, speed, and noise margins. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 3]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DCVSL Explanation:**

DCVSL uses **two NMOS pull-down trees** that implement the function F and its complement F̄. The load is a **cross-coupled PMOS latch** (two PMOS transistors whose gates are cross-connected to the opposite outputs).

**Operation:**
- When one NMOS tree is ON and the other is OFF, the corresponding PMOS is turned ON, latching the high output to VDD.
- Both true and complementary outputs are **simultaneously available**, eliminating the need for an extra inverter.

**Advantages over Pseudo-nMOS:**
1. Provides complementary outputs without extra inverter.
2. **Eliminates static DC current** after the latch settles — lower static power.
3. Better noise margins because VOL reaches true 0 V.
4. Faster switching due to positive feedback of cross-coupled PMOS.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | CMOS | Pseudo-nMOS | DCVSL |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static Power | ≈ 0 | High (DC) | Low (after latch) |
| Area | Largest (2N transistors) | Smallest (N+1) | Medium (2N+2) |
| Speed | Moderate | Fast (small C_load) | Fast |
| Noise Margin | Excellent | Reduced (VOL > 0) | Good |
| Complementary Outputs | No | No | Yes |
| VOL | 0 V | > 0 V (ratio-dependent) | ≈ 0 V |
| VOH | VDD | VDD | VDD |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing pseudo-nMOS with full CMOS:** Many students incorrectly state that pseudo-nMOS has zero static power. Always remember — pseudo-nMOS has a **continuous DC path** when output is LOW.
> 2. **Forgetting the dual-network rule:** In CMOS NAND/NOR, the PMOS network must be the **exact dual** of the NMOS network. Drawing them independently is a common error.
> 3. **Mixing up VIL and VIH formulas:** Memorize carefully — VIL uses (2VOUT + Vth,p − VDD + βR·Vth,n), while VIH uses (VDD + Vth,p + βR·(2VOUT + Vth,n)). Sign errors cost 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring βR when computing V_M:** Always compute √βR first; do not skip the square root step.
> 5. **Not mentioning ratio-dependence:** When explaining pseudo-nMOS VOL, always state explicitly that VOL depends on the W/L ratio of PMOS to NMOS.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CMOS** uses **complementary PMOS (PUN) + NMOS (PDN)** networks and is **ratio-less**.
- ✅ **Steady-state** in ideal CMOS: **no DC current**, so **P_static ≈ 0**.
- ✅ **Dynamic power** dominates: $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^{2} f$.
- ✅ **Switching threshold** V_M is given by $V_M = \dfrac{V_{th,n}\sqrt{\beta_R} + V_{DD} + V_{th,p}}{1 + \sqrt{\beta_R}}$.
- ✅ **Pseudo-nMOS** uses **one always-ON PMOS load** — has **static power** and **ratio-dependent VOL**.
- ✅ **DCVSL** uses **two NMOS trees + cross-coupled PMOS latch** — provides **complementary outputs** and **low static power**.
- ✅ **Noise Margins:** NMH = VOH − VIH, NML = VIL − VOL — must be **maximized** for robust design.
- ✅ **Design Rule:** Make PMOS ≈ **2–3×** wider than NMOS to compensate for lower hole mobility (μp ≈ μn/2 to μn/3).
- ✅ **KTU-Favorite Questions:** CMOS inverter VTC, derivation of V_M, pseudo-nMOS circuit + drawbacks, comparison table.
- ✅ **Speed tip:** Smaller parasitic C_L → faster switching; pseudo-nMOS gates are faster due to single load PMOS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pass Transistor Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pass Transistor Logic # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** is a digital CMOS design methodology in which individual MOS transistors (either NMOS, PMOS, or both) operate as voltage-controlled switches to *pass* an input logic signal directly to the output node, rather than restoring it through a complementary pull-up/pull-down network as in static CMOS logic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** In Pass Transistor Logic, the source of the input signal is fed to the source/drain terminal of the MOSFET, and the gate is driven by the control signal. The output is taken from the other terminal. The transistor acts as a "pass gate" — *conducting* when ON (forming a low-resistance channel) and *isolating* when OFF. The three principal variants studied in Module 3 are: 1. **Single NMOS Pass Transistor** – passes a strong logic `0`, but a *degraded* logic `1` (subject to threshold drop). 2. **Single PMOS Pass Transistor** – passes a strong logic `1`, but a *degraded* logic `0`. 3. **CMOS Transmission Gate (TG)** – a parallel combination of NMOS and PMOS with complementary controls; passes both logic levels *rail-to-rail* without degradation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Tap" View Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected between a tank (input) and a bucket (output): - The **handle** of the tap is the **gate** of the MOSFET. - The **pipe** inside the tap is the **channel** of the MOSFET. - When you turn the handle ON, water flows from tank → bucket. - When you turn it OFF, the bucket is isolated. | Tap Property | MOSFET Equivalent | Logic Meaning | |---|---|---| | Handle turned ON | $V_{GS} > V_{TN}$ (NMOS) | Transistor ON — signal passes | | Handle turned OFF | $V_{GS} < V_{TN}$ | Transistor OFF — output holds previous value | | Pipe slightly leaky at high pressure | Weak drive near $V_{DD}$ | **Threshold voltage drop** | | Two taps in parallel (NMOS + PMOS) | Transmission Gate | **Perfect, full-rail transfer** | > [!NOTE] > The "leaky pipe" analogy captures the most critical concept in PTL: a single NMOS transistor cannot deliver a *full* $V_{DD}$ to the output. Just as a real pipe might lose some pressure at the joint, the NMOS loses $V_{TN}$ volts at its output when sourcing a high level. ## 1.3 Why PTL Matters in Modern VLSI > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Point:** PTL achieves the same Boolean function with **fewer transistors** than static CMOS, leading to **lower parasitic capacitance**, **smaller silicon area**, and **potentially lower power and higher speed** — at the cost of degraded logic levels and reduced noise margins. For example, a 2-to-1 multiplexer in static CMOS requires **6 transistors (4 in TG + 2 inverters)**, while a pure PTL XOR/MUX can sometimes be implemented with just **2 to 4 transistors**. ## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Recall) | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **1.2 V / 1.8 V / 2.5 V / 3.3 V** | Volts | | NMOS Threshold Voltage | $V_{TN0}$ | **0.4 – 0.7 V** | Volts | | PMOS Threshold Voltage | $V_{TP0}$ | **−0.4 to −0.7 V** | Volts | | Body-effect Coefficient | $\gamma$ | **0.3 – 0.5** | $\sqrt{V}$ | | Bulk Potential | $2\phi_F$ | **0.6 – 0.7 V** | Volts | | Channel Length Modulation | $\lambda$ | **0.05 – 0.1** | $V^{-1}$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Analysis — Step by Step ### 2.1.1 NMOS Pass Transistor Operation **Circuit topology:** Source = Input signal ($V_{IN}$), Drain = Output node ($V_{OUT}$), Gate = Control signal ($V_{C}$), Bulk = Ground (typically tied to $V_{SS}$). **Case 1 — Passing a Logic 0 (NMOS strong '0'):** - $V_{IN} = 0\,\text{V}$, $V_{C} = V_{DD}$, NMOS is ON. - $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD} > V_{TN}$, so channel is inverted. - Output is pulled firmly to **0 V** with low $R_{on}$. - **Why?** The NMOS pulls down *strongly* because $V_{GS}$ remains large throughout the discharge. **Case 2 — Passing a Logic 1 (NMOS weak '1' — the famous problem):** - $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$, $V_{C} = V_{DD}$. - As $V_{OUT}$ rises toward $V_{DD}$, $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT}$ *decreases*. - The transistor turns OFF when $V_{GS} = V_{TN}$, i.e., when: $$V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$ - **Why does it stop?** Because once $V_{GS} < V_{TN}$, the inversion layer disappears — the channel pinches off. No more current can flow into the output capacitance. > [!NOTE] > **The Threshold Drop:** An NMOS pass transistor *cannot* pass a voltage higher than $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$. This is the single most-tested concept in KTU Module 3 examinations. ### 2.1.2 PMOS Pass Transistor Operation **Case 1 — Passing a Logic 0 (PMOS weak '0'):** - $V_{IN} = 0$, $V_{C} = 0$, PMOS is ON. - $V_{SG} = 0 - V_{OUT} = -V_{OUT}$. - The PMOS stops conducting when $V_{SG} = \vert V_{TP} \vert$, giving: $$V_{OUT,\min} = -\vert V_{TP} \vert$$ So the PMOS cannot pull the output below $\vert V_{TP} \vert$ when passing a low level. **Case 2 — Passing a Logic 1 (PMOS strong '1'):** - $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$, $V_{C} = 0$, $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$ (very strong drive). - Output rises cleanly to $V_{DD}$. **No degradation.** ### 2.1.3 Body Effect in Pass Transistors Because the source of the pass transistor is *floating* (it is the data input, not tied to $V_{SS}$ or $V_{DD}$), the source-to-bulk voltage $V_{SB}$ becomes non-zero. This triggers the **body effect**, which *increases* the threshold voltage: $$V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{\,2\phi_F + V_{SB}\,} - \sqrt{\,2\phi_F\,} \right)$$ - When passing a logic 1 through an NMOS, $V_{SB} = V_{OUT}$ (positive and growing), so $V_{TN}$ **increases**, worsening the degradation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Because of body effect, the actual maximum output of an NMOS pass transistor is often even *lower* than $V_{DD} - V_{TN0}$. Many KTU problems give you $\gamma$ and $2\phi_F$ and ask you to compute the *real* degraded output using the body-effect equation. ### 2.1.4 The CMOS Transmission Gate (TG) — The Solution A **transmission gate** is constructed by placing an NMOS and PMOS **in parallel**: - NMOS gate = Control signal $C$ - PMOS gate = Inverted control $\overline{C}$ - Source/Drain of both = common input/output nodes **Why does it pass both levels rail-to-rail?** - When **C = 1** (passing): Both transistors ON. NMOS handles the strong '0' path; PMOS handles the strong '1' path. At any intermediate $V_{OUT}$, **at least one transistor has a strong $V_{GS}$** to keep the channel conducting. - The parallel combination has a **lower effective resistance** than either alone — roughly: $$R_{eq} \approx R_{on,NMOS} \parallel R_{on,PMOS}$$ - When **C = 0** (blocking): Both transistors OFF → output is high-impedance (Hi-Z), holding its previous state on the load capacitance. | Input Level | NMOS Role | PMOS Role | Final Output | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$ (passing '1') | Weak / OFF near top | **Strong drive** | Full $V_{DD}$ ✓ | | $V_{IN} = 0$ (passing '0') | **Strong drive** | Weak / OFF near bottom | Full 0 V ✓ | ### 2.1.5 Resistance Comparison Across Logic Styles In KTU problems, you are often asked to compare equivalent ON-resistances. The NMOS and PMOS resistances vary with $V_{OUT}$: $$R_{on,NMOS}(V_{OUT}) = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TN} - V_{OUT})}$$ $$R_{on,PMOS}(V_{OUT}) = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{OUT} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)}$$ Because $\mu_n \approx 2\mu_p$, designers often choose $(W/L)_p \approx 2 \cdot (W/L)_n$ in a TG to *equalize* the rising and falling resistances. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Table | # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | NMOS Pass Transistor Max Output | $V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ | Weak '1' level | ★★★★★ | | 2 | PMOS Pass Transistor Min Output | $V_{OUT,\min} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$ | Weak '0' level | ★★★★★ | | 3 | Body-Effect Threshold | $V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ | Floating-source threshold shift | ★★★★★ | | 4 | NMOS ON-resistance | $R_{on,N} = 1 / [\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{DD} - V_{TN} - V_{OUT})]$ | Pass-transistor drive strength | ★★★★ | | 5 | PMOS ON-resistance | $R_{on,P} = 1 / [\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{OUT} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)]$ | Pass-transistor drive strength | ★★★★ | | 6 | TG Equivalent Resistance | $R_{TG} \approx R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$ | Parallel drive strength | ★★★★ | | 7 | Pass Transistor Delay (first-order) | $t_d \approx 0.69 \, R_{on} \, C_{out}$ | RC propagation delay | ★★★ | | 8 | Noise Margin (degraded) | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | Reduced when output < $V_{DD}$ | ★★★ | > [!NOTE] > **Pipe-Shift Rule Applied:** Because markdown tables break on the `\|` symbol, the threshold-drop formulas in this table deliberately use the form $V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ (no absolute-value pipes inside table cells). This preserves clean table rendering in PDF/Docx export pipelines. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of PTL | Application Area | Why PTL is Used | |---|---| | **Multiplexers / Data Selectors** | Single TG implements 2:1 mux with just 2 transistors + 1 inverter | | **XOR / XNOR Gates** | 4-transistor PTL XOR exists; static CMOS needs 12 transistors | | **Full Adder Sum Circuits** | PTL-based adders achieve ~30% lower delay in 45 nm and below | | **SRAM Cell Read Path** | Pass transistors form the read access to the storage node | | **Dynamic Logic & Charge Sharing** | PTL is fundamental to dynamic node evaluation | | **Barrel Shifters & Routing Networks** | Each bit-routing switch uses a TG | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Modern CMOS processes** (90 nm and below) often use PTL inside standard-cell libraries for XOR, MUX, and adder cells — every KTU Module 3 question on this topic is essentially testing your grasp of when and why this is done. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Output Voltage of an NMOS Pass Transistor **Problem Statement:** An NMOS pass transistor has gate tied to $V_{DD}$, drain connected to the output node $V_{OUT}$, source receiving an input signal $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$. Derive the steady-state value of $V_{OUT}$ when the transistor reaches the boundary of conduction. **Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough:** Consider the NMOS in the linear region initially (low $V_{DS}$). As $V_{OUT}$ rises, $V_{DS} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$ shrinks. We are interested in the **maximum** $V_{OUT}$ at which current can still flow. The fundamental conduction condition for an NMOS is: $$V_{GS} \geq V_{TN}$$ For our pass transistor: $$V_{GS} = V_{G} - V_{S} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT}$$ Setting this equal to the threshold (the *boundary* of conduction): $$ \begin{aligned} V_{DD} - V_{OUT,\max} &= V_{TN} \\ V_{OUT,\max} + V_{TN} &= V_{DD} \\ V_{OUT,\max} &= V_{DD} - V_{TN} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion (boxed for revision):** $$V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN} \quad \text{(NMOS Pass Transistor)}$$ **Logical Reasoning Summary:** - As $V_{OUT}$ rises, $V_{GS}$ falls linearly. - Once $V_{GS}$ drops below $V_{TN}$, the inversion layer vanishes. - No more drain current → output **stops rising**. - Steady state is reached at $V_{OUT} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Output Voltage of a PMOS Pass Transistor **Problem Statement:** A PMOS pass transistor has gate tied to 0 V, source connected to $V_{IN} = 0\,\text{V}$ (logic low), and drain to $V_{OUT}$. Derive the lowest voltage the output can settle to. **Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough:** For a PMOS, conduction requires $V_{SG} \geq \vert V_{TP}\vert$. Source is at $V_{IN} = 0$ (this is the *source*, by convention the higher-potential terminal of a PMOS channel), gate is at 0 V, so: $$V_{SG} = V_{S} - V_{G} = 0 - 0 = 0$$ Wait — this is wrong. The PMOS is conducting and *pulling* the output toward 0. As $V_{OUT}$ falls below 0 (which it cannot, since ground is 0 V), we instead need to consider what happens at the **drain side**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Convention:** When a PMOS passes a logic 0, the source is at the *input* (which is at $V_{IN} = 0$) and the drain is the *output*. The PMOS pulls $V_{OUT}$ down toward 0. The terminal at the *higher potential* is defined as the source. Therefore during the "pull-down" the actual source remains $V_{IN} = 0$, and the drain = $V_{OUT} > 0$. During pull-down, $V_{OUT}$ starts high and falls. The PMOS conduction condition becomes: $$V_{SG} = V_{S} - V_{G} = V_{IN} - V_{G} = 0 - V_{G}$$ But $V_G$ of the PMOS is fixed at 0 (control signal), so $V_{SG} = 0$ when $V_{IN} = 0$ — meaning the PMOS is *just barely* on at the very start. The more useful form uses the fact that the PMOS turns OFF when its drain-source voltage crosses the threshold. Equivalently, considering the terminal that ends up *lowest* in voltage (the output) and noting that PMOS conduction requires: $$\vert V_{GS}\vert \geq \vert V_{TP}\vert$$ with $V_{GS} = V_{OUT} - V_{G} = V_{OUT} - 0 = V_{OUT}$: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{OUT} &\geq \vert V_{TP}\vert \quad \text{(conduction condition, with magnitudes)} \\ V_{OUT,\min} &= \vert V_{TP}\vert \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion (boxed for revision):** $$V_{OUT,\min} = \vert V_{TP}\vert \quad \text{(PMOS Pass Transistor)}$$ The PMOS **cannot pull the output lower than $\vert V_{TP}\vert$** when sourcing a logic 0. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Degraded Output Including Body Effect **Problem Statement:** An NMOS pass transistor passes a logic 1 from $V_{IN} = 1.8\,\text{V}$. Given $V_{TN0} = 0.5\,\text{V}$, $\gamma = 0.4\,\text{V}^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.6\,\text{V}$, compute the actual degraded output voltage accounting for the body effect. **Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough:** **Step A — Identify $V_{SB}$ at steady state:** The source is the terminal receiving the input. Since the input is high (1.8 V) and the bulk is tied to ground (0 V): $$V_{SB} = V_{S} - V_{B} = V_{IN} - 0 = 1.8\,\text{V}$$ But wait — at steady state $V_{OUT}$ is *less* than $V_{IN}$ because of the threshold drop. However, for the **source terminal** we use $V_{IN}$ because that's where the input voltage physically appears before the channel resistance. Actually, in the steady state where the channel is just on the verge of turning OFF: $$V_S = V_{OUT,\max} \quad \text{(source and output are connected via the channel)}$$ So: $$V_{SB} = V_{OUT,\max} - 0 = V_{OUT,\max}$$ **Step B — Apply the body-effect equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{TN} &= V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right) \\ V_{TN} &= 0.5 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT,\max}} - \sqrt{0.6} \right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step C — Apply the conduction boundary:** At the boundary: $V_{GS} = V_{TN}$, with $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT,\max}$: $$V_{DD} - V_{OUT,\max} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{OUT,\max}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$ **Step D — Substitute and solve numerically:** $$ \begin{aligned} 1.8 - V_{OUT,\max} &= 0.5 + 0.4 \left( \sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT,\max}} - 0.7746 \right) \\ 1.3 - V_{OUT,\max} &= 0.4 \sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT,\max}} - 0.3098 \\ 1.6098 - V_{OUT,\max} &= 0.4 \sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT,\max}} \end{aligned} $$ Let $x = V_{OUT,\max}$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1.6098 - x &= 0.4\sqrt{0.6 + x} \\ (1.6098 - x)^2 &= 0.16 \,(0.6 + x) \\ 2.5915 - 3.2196 x + x^2 &= 0.096 + 0.16 x \\ x^2 - 3.3796 x + 2.4955 &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ Solving the quadratic using $x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= \frac{3.3796 \pm \sqrt{11.4217 - 9.9820}}{2} \\ x &= \frac{3.3796 \pm \sqrt{1.4397}}{2} \\ x &= \frac{3.3796 \pm 1.1999}{2} \end{aligned} $$ We take the physically valid root ($x < V_{DD}$): $$x = \frac{3.3796 - 1.1999}{2} = \frac{2.1797}{2} = 1.0899\,\text{V}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{V_{OUT,\max} \approx 1.09\,\text{V} \quad \text{(vs. ideal } 1.30\,\text{V without body effect)}}$$ **Engineering Insight:** - Without body effect: $V_{OUT,\max} = 1.8 - 0.5 = 1.3\,\text{V}$ - With body effect: $V_{OUT,\max} \approx 1.09\,\text{V}$ - **Body effect causes an additional ~0.21 V drop.** --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Resistance of a CMOS Transmission Gate **Problem Statement:** At the midpoint of a transient where $V_{OUT} = V_{DD}/2 = 0.9\,\text{V}$, derive the equivalent ON-resistance of a TG given equal NMOS and PMOS sizing. **Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough:** At $V_{OUT} = V_{DD}/2$: **NMOS (gate at $V_{DD}$, source at $V_{OUT}$, $V_{DS} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT}$):** For simplicity, assume the TG is mid-transition with $V_{DS}$ small (linear region): $$ \begin{aligned} R_{on,N} &= \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N \, (V_{DD} - V_{TN} - V_{OUT})} \\ &= \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N \, (1.8 - 0.5 - 0.9)} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.4 \cdot \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N} \end{aligned} $$ **PMOS (gate at 0, source at $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$, $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{on,P} &= \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P \, (V_{OUT} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)} \\ &= \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P \, (0.9 - 0.5)} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.4 \cdot \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P} \end{aligned} $$ **Equivalent Parallel Resistance:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{TG} &= R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P} = \frac{R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}}{R_{on,N} + R_{on,P}} \end{aligned} $$ If $(W/L)_P = 2 \cdot (W/L)_N$ (standard sizing for equal drive) and $\mu_n = 2\mu_p$: $$R_{on,N} = \frac{1}{0.4 \cdot 2\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_N} = \frac{1}{0.8 \, \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_N}$$ $$R_{on,P} = \frac{1}{0.4 \cdot \mu_p C_{ox} \cdot 2(W/L)_N} = \frac{1}{0.8 \, \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_N}$$ So $R_{on,N} = R_{on,P} = R$. Then: $$\boxed{R_{TG} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{1}{1.6 \, \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_N}}$$ **Conclusion:** The TG halves the effective resistance and provides full rail-to-rail swing simultaneously. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | NMOS PTL | PMOS PTL | CMOS TG | |---|---|---|---| | Strong level passed | Logic 0 | Logic 1 | Both 0 and 1 | | Degraded level | Logic 1 → $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ | Logic 0 → $\vert V_{TP}\vert$ | None (full swing) | | Voltage drop magnitude | $\sim 0.4$–$0.7\,\text{V}$ | $\sim 0.4$–$0.7\,\text{V}$ | $\sim 0\,\text{V}$ | | Body effect impact | Severe (worst case at high output) | Mild (only at low output) | Mitigated by parallel device | | Transistor count (for 2:1 MUX) | 2 (NMOS only) + 1 INV | 2 (PMOS only) + 1 INV | 4 (NMOS + PMOS pair) + 1 INV | | Power consumption | Lower (no static path) | Lower | Slightly higher (more parasitic C) | | Noise margin | Reduced | Reduced | Preserved | | Speed | Fast (less C) | Fast | Slightly slower (more C) | | Typical KTU application | MUX data path | MUX data path | Full-adder, XOR, bus driver | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** When a KTU question asks "design a 2:1 mux using pass transistor logic", the expected answer is a **transmission-gate implementation** (4 transistors + 1 inverter) with an explanation of why full-rail swing matters. # 4.
