KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Explain why an NMOS pass transistor cannot pass a full logic '1' to the output node. Derive the expression for the maximum output voltage.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** When the gate of an NMOS pass transistor is tied to $V_{DD}$ and the input rises to $V_{DD}$, the output follows. However, as $V_{OUT}$ increases, the gate-to-source voltage: $$V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT}$$ **decreases**. The NMOS channel pinches off when $V_{GS}$ drops to the threshold voltage $V_{TN}$. Setting the boundary condition: $$V_{DD} - V_{OUT,\max} = V_{TN}$$ $$\therefore V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$ Thus the output can never reach $V_{DD}$ — there is a permanent threshold drop of $V_{TN}$. This is called the **weak '1' problem** of single NMOS pass-transistor logic. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is a CMOS transmission gate? Why is it preferred over a single NMOS or PMOS pass transistor?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **CMOS transmission gate (TG)** is a parallel combination of an NMOS and a PMOS transistor whose gates are driven by complementary control signals ($C$ and $\overline{C}$). It acts as a bidirectional analog switch. **Preference over single-pass-transistor logic** is due to: 1. **Full-rail signal transfer** — It passes both logic `0` and logic `1` without any threshold-voltage degradation. 2. **Lower equivalent ON-resistance** — The two transistors in parallel yield $R_{TG} = R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$. 3. **Bidirectional conduction** — Signal can flow in either direction (source ↔ drain), making it ideal for bus and MUX applications. The only drawback is the **higher transistor count** (2 per switch + 1 inverter for the complementary control). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question is for 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each. The internal choice (OR) is at the **question level**, not sub-part level. We provide two complete independent question sets below. --- ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` **(a)** *With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an NMOS pass transistor when passing logic '0' and logic '1'. Discuss the concept of threshold voltage drop. State the expression for the maximum output voltage achievable. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Operation:** Consider an NMOS transistor with: - Gate = $V_{C}$ (control, here $= V_{DD}$) - Source = $V_{IN}$ - Drain = $V_{OUT}$ - Bulk = $V_{SS}$ (ground) **Passing Logic '0' (Strong '0'):** - $V_{IN} = 0$, $V_{C} = V_{DD}$ - $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD} \gg V_{TN}$ → transistor is strongly ON. - Output is discharged to $V_{SS} = 0$ V through a low ON-resistance. - **Result:** Strong, clean logic '0'. No degradation. **Passing Logic '1' (Weak '1' — the threshold drop problem):** - $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$, $V_{C} = V_{DD}$ - As $V_{OUT}$ rises, $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{OUT}$ decreases. - Conduction stops when $V_{GS} = V_{TN}$. - Maximum output: $$V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$ **Threshold Voltage Drop Explained:** The transistor self-limits because the channel pinch-off condition prevents further current flow. This produces a permanent **drop of $V_{TN}$ volts** between input and output, which reduces noise margin and may cause static-power dissipation in subsequent inverter stages. **Circuit Diagram (Text Representation):** ``` VC = VDD │ G ┌────┤ │ NMOS VIN ┤ │ S D ──── VOUT (degraded to VDD - VTN) │ GND (Bulk) ``` --- **(b)** *Explain the working of a CMOS transmission gate with a neat diagram. Show that it can pass both logic levels rail-to-rail. Discuss its role in implementing a 2:1 multiplexer. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **CMOS Transmission Gate — Construction:** A transmission gate is formed by connecting an NMOS (MN) and a PMOS (MP) in **parallel**, with their sources and drains tied together. The gates are driven by **complementary** control signals $C$ and $\overline{C}$. **Operation Table:** | Control $C$ | $\overline{C}$ | MN | MP | TG State | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 (High) | 0 (Low) | ON | ON | **Passing** | | 0 (Low) | 1 (High) | OFF | OFF | **Isolated (Hi-Z)** | **Passing Logic '1' ($V_{IN} = V_{DD}$):** - NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{DD} = 0$ → tends to turn off, but - PMOS: $V_{SG} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$ → **strongly ON**. - The PMOS sources the full $V_{DD}$ to the output. ✓ **Passing Logic '0' ($V_{IN} = 0$):** - PMOS: $V_{SG} = 0$ → tends to turn off, but - NMOS: $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$ → **strongly ON**. - The NMOS sinks the output to 0 V. ✓ **Therefore, full rail-to-rail swing is achieved.** **2:1 Multiplexer Implementation:** A 2:1 MUX selects between two inputs $D_0$ and $D_1$ based on a select line $S$: $$Y = \overline{S} \cdot D_0 + S \cdot D_1$$ **TG Implementation:** - **TG₁** (top): passes $D_0$ when $S = 0$ (gates driven by $\overline{S}$ and $S$). - **TG₂** (bottom): passes $D_1$ when $S = 1$ (gates driven by $S$ and $\overline{S}$). - **Inverter** generates $\overline{S}$ from $S$. - Outputs of both TGs tied to common node $Y$. Total transistor count: **4 (2 TGs) + 2 (inverter) = 6 transistors**, providing full-rail output with no static power dissipation. **Block Diagram:** ``` S ──┬────────────────┐ │ │ [INV] ── S-bar │ │ │ D0 ──[TG₁: S-bar, S]──┤ ├── Y = (S-bar)D0 + S·D1 D1 ──[TG₂: S, S-bar]──┘ ``` --- ### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** *Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS pass transistor when passing a high level, including the body effect. Given $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$, $V_{TN0} = 0.5\,\text{V}$, $\gamma = 0.4\,\text{V}^{1/2}$, $2\phi_F = 0.6\,\text{V}$, calculate the degraded output voltage. **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** For an NMOS pass transistor passing $V_{IN} = V_{DD}$: - At steady state: $V_S = V_{OUT}$ (the source and output are connected via the channel). - Bulk is at $V_B = 0$, so $V_{SB} = V_{OUT}$. - Threshold with body effect: $$V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$ At the conduction boundary: $$V_{GS} = V_{TN} \Rightarrow V_{DD} - V_{OUT} = V_{TN}$$ Combining: $$V_{DD} - V_{OUT} = V_{TN0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{OUT}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$ **Numerical Calculation:** Substituting $V_{DD} = 1.8$, $V_{TN0} = 0.5$, $\gamma = 0.4$, $2\phi_F = 0.6$: $$1.8 - V_{OUT} = 0.5 + 0.4\left(\sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT}} - \sqrt{0.6}\right)$$ $$1.3 - V_{OUT} = 0.4\sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT}} - 0.3098$$ $$1.6098 - V_{OUT} = 0.4\sqrt{0.6 + V_{OUT}}$$ Squaring both sides (and solving the resulting quadratic — see Section 3.3 for full algebra): $$V_{OUT,\max} \approx 1.09\,\text{V}$$ **Comparison:** | Case | $V_{OUT,\max}$ | |---|---| | Ideal (no body effect) | $1.8 - 0.5 = 1.3\,\text{V}$ | | With body effect | **$\approx 1.09\,\text{V}$** | The body effect introduces an additional ~0.21 V drop beyond the basic threshold drop. --- **(b)** *Compare NMOS pass-transistor logic, PMOS pass-transistor logic, and CMOS transmission gates in terms of (i) signal swing, (ii) transistor count for a 2:1 MUX, (iii) noise margin, and (iv) speed. Which is preferred in modern digital design and why? **(7 marks)*** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | NMOS PTL | PMOS PTL | CMOS TG | |---|---|---|---| | **(i) Signal Swing** | 0 → $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ (weak '1') | $\vert V_{TP}\vert \rightarrow V_{DD}$ (weak '0') | **0 → $V_{DD}$** (full rail) | | **(ii) Transistor Count (2:1 MUX)** | 2 NMOS + 1 INV = 3 | 2 PMOS + 1 INV = 3 | 2 NMOS + 2 PMOS + 1 INV = 6 | | **(iii) Noise Margin** | Reduced ($NM_H$ shrinks) | Reduced ($NM_L$ shrinks) | **Full, preserved** | | **(iv) Speed (Delay)** | Fast (low C) | Fast (low C) | Slightly slower (higher C) | **Preferred Choice — CMOS Transmission Gate:** In modern digital VLSI design, the **CMOS transmission gate is preferred** for the following reasons: 1. **Full rail-to-rail swing** preserves logic levels through cascaded stages. 2. **No static power dissipation** in the following inverter (no partially-on transistor). 3. **Bidirectional** — suitable for buses, MUXs, and bidirectional I/O. 4. **Predictable noise margin** — essential for reliable operation in deep-submicron processes where $V_{DD}$ is already small (1.0–1.2 V). 5. **Better drive strength** at intermediate voltages due to parallel $R_{on}$ paths. The **only trade-off** is the slightly higher transistor count and parasitic capacitance. However, for any practical design operating at $V_{DD} < 2.0$ V, the loss of noise margin in single-pass-transistor logic makes it unusable — hence the dominance of TG-based design. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting the body effect.** Many students write only $V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ and lose 2–3 marks when the question explicitly gives $\gamma$ and $2\phi_F$. > 2. **Confusing source vs. drain in PMOS.** The source of a PMOS is the *higher-potential* terminal. When passing logic '0', students often mis-identify source/drain and get the wrong degraded voltage. > 3. **Skipping the inverter in MUX design.** A TG-based 2:1 MUX *requires* an inverter to generate $\overline{S}$. Students who omit it get only partial credit. > 4. **Calling TG "two transistors in series."** It is a **parallel** combination — series would never conduct. > 5. **Stating $V_{OUT,\max} = 0$ for a PMOS passing a low level.** It is $\vert V_{TP}\vert$, not 0. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam:** - ☐ **NMOS passes strong '0', weak '1'.** The weak '1' is at $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$, not $V_{DD}$. - ☐ **PMOS passes strong '1', weak '0'.** The weak '0' is at $\vert V_{TP}\vert$, not 0 V. - ☐ **CMOS Transmission Gate = NMOS ∥ PMOS** (parallel, not series) with complementary gate signals. - ☐ **TG passes both levels rail-to-rail** — full $V_{DD}$ swing without degradation. - ☐ **Body effect worsens** the NMOS threshold drop because $V_{SB}$ becomes non-zero. Always check for the $\gamma$ and $2\phi_F$ parameters in problem statements. - ☐ **Equivalent ON-resistance** of TG = $R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$, always less than either alone. - ☐ **2:1 MUX using TG = 4 transistors + 1 inverter = 6 transistors total.** This is the standard KTU benchmark number. - ☐ **Static CMOS XOR gate needs 12 transistors; PTL XOR can use 4 transistors.** This is why PTL is used in adder/MUX cells in modern standard-cell libraries. - ☐ **Key formulas to memorize verbatim:** - $V_{OUT,\max} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ (NMOS weak '1') - $V_{OUT,\min} = \vert V_{TP}\vert$ (PMOS weak '0') - $V_{TN} = V_{TN0} + \gamma(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F})$ (body effect) - ☐ **PTL advantages:** fewer transistors, lower area, lower parasitic capacitance, faster in some cases. - ☐ **PTL disadvantages:** degraded levels, reduced noise margin, no signal restoration — **requires buffering** at output for cascaded logic. - ☐ **Bidirectional nature of TG** makes it the universal switch for buses, MUXs, flip-flops (master-slave latches), and SRAM read/write paths. - ☐ **When asked to "design using PTL," always prefer the transmission gate unless the question explicitly asks for single-transistor pass logic.**

topics.title: Differential Pass Transistor Logic - Transmission Gate Logic, Dynamic MOS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Pass Transistor Logic - Transmission Gate Logic, Dynamic MOS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Differential Pass Transistor Logic (DPTL)

**Formal Definition:**
Differential Pass Transistor Logic (DPTL) is an advanced CMOS logic family that uses **complementary (differential) signal pairs** along with **NMOS pass transistor networks** to implement Boolean functions. The differential nature allows the circuit to recover full-swing output signals without suffering from the threshold voltage drop problem inherent in conventional single-ended pass transistor logic (PTL).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DPTL is covered under "Alternative CMOS Logic Styles" and is examinable in ESE Module 3. The student must emphasize how DPTL *eliminates* the static power dissipation of conventional PTL by using a complementary signal path.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Pipe" (Bidirectional Flow):**
Imagine a water supply system with a single pipe. If water enters with reduced pressure (representing a weak logic '1' due to threshold drop), the output tap receives low-pressure water. Now imagine a **dual-pipe system** — one carries normal-pressure water (logic '1') and the other carries the *complementary low-pressure* signal in such a way that the **difference** between the two pipes is detected. This differential measurement always yields a clean, full-swing output, just like DPTL recovers full-rail signals.

### 1.2 Transmission Gate (TG) Logic

**Formal Definition:**
A **CMOS Transmission Gate** is a bidirectional switch constructed by paralleling an **NMOS** and a **PMOS** transistor, with **complementary control signals** applied to their gates. It transmits both logic '0' and logic '1' signals with **minimal voltage degradation** (ideally zero), making it the workhorse of modern pass-transistor design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Favorite):** "A transmission gate is an analog switch that passes analog or digital signals bidirectionally when enabled, and presents high impedance (open circuit) when disabled. It is formed by the parallel combination of an NMOS and a PMOS controlled by complementary clock/select signals."

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Door Revolving Door":**
Think of an electrical corridor guarded by two doors. When the corridor is open, both an "IN" door (PMOS, opens for high signals) and an "OUT" door (NMOS, opens for low signals) are simultaneously open. Passers carrying high-voltage goods go through the PMOS door; low-voltage carriers go through the NMOS door. Either way, the signal reaches the other side *unattenuated* because each door specializes in its own signal type.

### 1.3 Dynamic MOS Logic

**Formal Definition:**
Dynamic MOS Logic is a clocked logic style that relies on **temporary charge storage on the parasitic gate capacitance of the load** instead of a static pull-up/pull-down network. The circuit operates in two distinct phases: a **Precharge phase** (when clock φ is low) and an **Evaluate phase** (when clock φ is high).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Dynamic logic offers **smaller area**, **lower power** (no static current path), and **faster operation** than static CMOS — but it suffers from **charge sharing**, **clock feedthrough**, and **leakage** problems. These are frequently asked 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Balloon Storage":**
Imagine filling a water balloon (the load capacitance $C_L$) during the precharge phase. Once filled, the balloon holds the water even after the source is disconnected — this is the dynamic storage. During the evaluate phase, you either *let the water out* (discharge through a pass network) or *keep the balloon inflated* (output remains high). The "leakage" of an old balloon slowly losing water is analogous to **leakage current** in real dynamic nodes.

### 1.4 Key Comparison Metrics

| Logic Style | Transistors per Function | Full-Swing Output | Static Power | Speed |
|-------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Static CMOS | High (≈2N for N inputs) | Yes | None | Moderate |
| Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) | Low (≈N) | **No** (threshold drop) | Possible | Fast |
| Differential PTL (DPTL) | Moderate | **Yes** | None | Fast |
| Transmission Gate | 2 per switch | **Yes** | None | Moderate |
| Dynamic CMOS | N + 2 (clock) | **Yes** | None | **Very Fast** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Differential Pass Transistor Logic — Deep Analysis

**Operational Steps (DPTL Cell):**
1. **Inputs** are provided in *complementary pairs*: $(A, \bar{A})$, $(B, \bar{B})$.
2. A **pure NMOS pass-transistor network** evaluates the function $F$ using the true inputs, producing $F_{out}$ (which may suffer a $V_{Tn}$ drop for logic '1').
3. A **complementary NMOS network** evaluates $\bar{F}$ using the inverted inputs, producing $\overline{F}_{out}$ (which may suffer a $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ drop for logic '0').
4. A **CMOS cross-coupled sense amplifier / differential output stage** amplifies the *difference* $(F_{out} - \overline{F}_{out})$ to restore **full rail-to-rail swing**.

**Why Differential Signaling Works (The Core Principle):**

- If a '1' is passed through an NMOS, the output rises only to $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$. The complementary path passes a '0', and the cross-coupled amplifier interprets the small differential voltage as a logical '1' and pulls the output the rest of the way up to $V_{DD}$.
- **Static power dissipation is zero** because the output is never left at the threshold-degraded value — the amplifier is self-latching.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Differential"?** The logic cell uses *both* the function $F$ and its complement $\bar{F}$ in a symmetric fashion. This is the key conceptual difference from standard PTL (which uses only $F$).

### 2.2 Transmission Gate — Deep Analysis

**Operational Steps:**
1. When $C = 1, \bar{C} = 0$: Both NMOS and PMOS are ON. The TG acts as a **low-resistance bidirectional switch** (typical $R_{on} \approx 100$–$1000 \ \Omega$).
2. When $C = 0, \bar{C} = 1$: Both transistors are OFF. The TG is a **high-impedance open circuit** ($\geq 10^{12} \ \Omega$).
3. For $V_{in}$ near $V_{DD}/2$, **both transistors are simultaneously ON**, giving the **lowest combined resistance** — a unique property of CMOS TG.

**On-Resistance Derivation (Crucial for Board Exams):**

The on-resistance of a single NMOS in the linear region is:

$$R_{on,N} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{Tn} - V_{DS}/2)}$$

Similarly for the PMOS (using $\mu_p$ and $V_{Tp}$):

$$R_{on,P} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert - V_{DS}/2)}$$

The **effective transmission-gate resistance** is the parallel combination:

$$\frac{1}{R_{TG}} = \frac{1}{R_{on,N}} + \frac{1}{R_{on,P}}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{TG} = \frac{R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}}{R_{on,N} + R_{on,P}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners love asking "Why does TG have a near-constant resistance across the input range?" Answer: As $V_{in}$ rises, $R_{on,N}$ rises but $R_{on,P}$ falls, and their parallel sum remains approximately constant.

**Applications of TG in VLSI:**
- **Multiplexers** (2:1, 4:1 MUX)
- **Flip-flops and latches** (master-slave storage)
- **XOR / XNOR gates** (compact full-adder implementation)
- **Register files and SRAM** (read/write access)
- **Sample-and-hold circuits** (analog switches)

### 2.3 Dynamic MOS Logic — Deep Analysis

**Phases of Operation:**

1. **Precharge Phase** ($\phi = 0$): PMOS precharge transistor turns ON, pulling the output node OUT to $V_{DD}$. All input signals must be stable *before* φ rises.
2. **Evaluate Phase** ($\phi = 1$): Precharge PMOS turns OFF. The NMOS pull-down network conditionally discharges OUT through the pass-transistor network based on inputs.

**Critical Non-Idealities:**

| Non-Ideal Effect | Cause | Mitigation |
|------------------|-------|------------|
| **Charge Sharing** | Internal node capacitances redistribute charge with $C_L$ | Precharge internal nodes, use **keeper PMOS** |
| **Clock Feedthrough** | Gate-drain overlap capacitance injects charge on clock edges | Use **dummy switch** or clock gating |
| **Leakage** | Subthreshold $I_{off}$ slowly discharges $C_L$ | Keeper transistor, **low-leakage** devices |
| **Race Condition** | Input transition during evaluate causes glitch | Strict timing — inputs must settle before φ rises |
| **Cascading Limitation** | Output of one stage valid only during evaluate | Use **domino logic** (static inverter added) |

**Domino Logic (Cascadable Dynamic Logic):**

Domino logic adds a **static CMOS inverter** at the output of a dynamic stage. The output of this inverter feeds the next dynamic stage. Since the inverter is inverting, the *precharged state* of one stage is the *non-conducting* state of the next, ensuring safe cascading.

**np-CMOS Logic (Dual-Rail Domino Alternative):**

- Uses **PMOS pull-up** network with $V_{DD}$ clock and **NMOS pull-down** with clock complement.
- Eliminates the *body effect* of precharge PMOS and provides **faster rising transitions**.
- Used in high-performance microprocessors (e.g., Intel Pentium floating-point units).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | Key Equation |
|--------|---------|:---:|---------------|
| $V_{Tn}$ | NMOS threshold voltage | **V** (typically **0.5–0.7 V**) | $V_{Tn} = V_{Tn0} + \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ |
| $V_{Tp}$ | PMOS threshold voltage | **V** (typically **−0.5 to −0.7 V**) | $V_{Tp} = V_{Tp0} - \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ |
| $R_{on,N}$ | NMOS on-resistance | $\Omega$ | $1 / [\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{GS} - V_{Tn} - V_{DS}/2)]$ |
| $R_{on,P}$ | PMOS on-resistance | $\Omega$ | $1 / [\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L) (\vert V_{GS} \vert - \vert V_{Tp} \vert - \vert V_{DS} \vert/2)]$ |
| $R_{TG}$ | TG parallel resistance | $\Omega$ | $(R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}) / (R_{on,N} + R_{on,P})$ |
| $C_L$ | Load capacitance | **F** | $C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ (intrinsic) |
| $t_{pd}$ | Propagation delay | **s** | $0.69 \cdot R_{TG} \cdot C_L$ (first-order) |
| $P_{dyn}$ | Dynamic power | **W** | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ |
| $P_{static}$ | Static power | **W** | $I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ |
| $t_{rise}$ | Rise time | **s** | $2.2 \cdot R_P \cdot C_L$ |
| $t_{fall}$ | Fall time | **s** | $2.2 \cdot R_N \cdot C_L$ |
| $\gamma$ | Body-effect coefficient | $\sqrt{V}$ | $t_{ox}/\epsilon_{ox} \cdot \sqrt{2q\epsilon_{si}N_A}$ |
| $\mu_n / \mu_p$ | Carrier mobility | $\text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ | $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$ (typical) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These logic styles directly influence **CPU datapath design**, **DRAM sense amplifiers**, **high-speed adders in GPUs**, and **low-power IoT processors**. A working knowledge of DPTL and TG is essential for **layout-aware digital design** roles in companies like Intel, Qualcomm, AMD, and Texas Instruments.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent On-Resistance of CMOS Transmission Gate

**Goal:** Show that the TG has approximately constant on-resistance over a wide input voltage range.

**Step 1: NMOS Resistance as a Function of Input Voltage**

For an NMOS with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ and $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ (when sourcing current to $V_{in}$):

$$R_{on,N}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N \left[(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \frac{V_{DD} - V_{in}}{2}\right]}$$

Simplify the bracketed term:

$$(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) - \frac{V_{DD}}{2} + \frac{V_{in}}{2} = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{Tn} + \frac{V_{in}}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{on,N}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N \left[\frac{V_{DD} - 2V_{Tn} + V_{in}}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 2: PMOS Resistance as a Function of Input Voltage**

For a PMOS with $V_{GS} = -V_{DD}$ and $V_{DS} = V_{in} - V_{DD}$:

$$R_{on,P}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P \left[(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert) - \frac{V_{DD} - V_{in}}{2}\right]}$$

After similar algebra:

$$R_{on,P}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P \left[\frac{V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{Tp} \vert + V_{in}}{2}\right]}$$

**Step 3: Parallel Combination**

The total TG resistance is the parallel sum:

$$R_{TG}(V_{in}) = \left[ \frac{1}{R_{on,N}(V_{in})} + \frac{1}{R_{on,P}(V_{in})} \right]^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Substitution with $\beta_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N$ and $\beta_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P$**

$$R_{TG}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\beta_n (V_{DD} - 2V_{Tn} + V_{in})/2 + \beta_p (V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{Tp} \vert + V_{in})/2}$$

**Step 5: Choosing Transistor Sizing for Constant Resistance**

If we choose $\beta_n = \beta_p = \beta$ (i.e., $\mu_n (W/L)_N = \mu_p (W/L)_P$), then:

$$R_{TG}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\beta \left[(V_{DD} - 2V_{Tn} + V_{in}) + (V_{DD} - 2\vert V_{Tp} \vert + V_{in})\right]/2}$$

If $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert = V_T$:

$$R_{TG}(V_{in}) = \frac{1}{\beta (V_{DD} - 2V_T + V_{in})}$$

This is **NOT** perfectly constant but varies with $V_{in}$. However, in practice:

- When $V_{in} = 0$: $R_{TG} \approx 1/[\beta(V_{DD} - 2V_T)]$ — dominated by PMOS (NMOS near cutoff)
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$: $R_{TG} \approx 1/[\beta(2V_{DD} - 2V_T)]$ — dominated by NMOS (PMOS near cutoff)
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$: $R_{TG}$ is **minimum** because both are strongly ON

**Step 6: Conclusion**

$$R_{TG} \approx \text{constant in the range } V_T \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - V_T$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Award:** Writing the parallel-resistance expression correctly with the right transconductance parameters is sufficient to score full marks in a derivation question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Charge Sharing in Dynamic Logic

**Setup:** A 2-input dynamic NAND gate. The output capacitance $C_L$ is precharged to $V_{DD}$. There is an internal node capacitance $C_x$ (e.g., from the bottom of the series NMOS stack). Assume inputs are A = 0, B = 1.

**Initial Condition:**
- $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (precharged)
- $V_x = 0$ (input 0 turned off the top NMOS; the node was discharged through previous operations)

**Charge Redistribution When φ Rises (Evaluate):**

When B = 1, the lower NMOS turns ON. The internal node $C_x$ and the load $C_L$ share charge.

Charge conservation:

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_x \cdot 0 = (C_L + C_x) \cdot V_{final}$$

Solving for $V_{final}$:

$$V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_x}$$

**Voltage Drop (Glitch):**

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_x}{C_L + C_x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Favorite Result:** If $C_x = C_L$, the output drops to $V_{DD}/2$ — this may be misinterpreted as a logic '0' by the next stage, **causing functional failure**. The standard remedy is to add a **bleeder PMOS (keeper)** that supplies leakage current to maintain the precharged level.

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy per Transition for Dynamic Node

The energy stored on $C_L$ at $V_{DD}$:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

The energy drawn from the supply during charging:

$$E_{supply} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

The energy dissipated as heat during charging:

$$E_{dissipated} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

The **remaining** half ($\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$) is stored on the capacitor. If the node is subsequently discharged to ground *without recycling*, an additional $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated in the NMOS pull-down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total per-cycle dissipation** for a dynamic gate with both precharge and discharge transitions:
> $$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$
> This confirms the standard $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ formula when $\alpha$ includes both transitions.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Guide for Logic Styles

| Application Requirement | Recommended Logic Style | Reason |
|--------------------------|:----------------------:|--------|
| Lowest power, high speed | **Dynamic CMOS / Domino** | No static current; small $C_L$ |
| Full-swing, low complexity, XOR-rich | **Transmission Gate** | Avoids $V_T$ drop; full-swing |
| Smallest area, full-swing | **DPTL** | Differential pairs eliminate static PTL problem |
| Mixed analog/digital signal routing | **Transmission Gate** | Bidirectional, low distortion |
| Noise-immune, ultra-low power | **Static CMOS** | High noise margin, zero static current |
| Wide fan-in OR / multiplexer | **Pseudo-NMOS / Dynamic** | Avoids the N-stack height problem |
| High-speed adder carry chain | **DPTL + TG** | Manchester carry chain |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Transmission Gate — Circuit Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode["VIN"] --- gateN["NMOS"]
    inNode --- gateP["PMOS"]
    gateN --- outNode["VOUT"]
    gateP --- outNode
    ctlIn["C"] --- gateN
    ctlInBar["C_BAR"] --- gateP
```

> **Reading the schematic:** The top transistor (PMOS) passes strong '1's; the bottom transistor (NMOS) passes strong '0's. Both share the input and output nodes. The gates are driven by complementary control signals C and $\bar{C}$.

### 4.2 DPTL Cell Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA["A"] --> netA["NMOS Network for F"]
    inABar["A_BAR"] --> netABar["NMOS Network for F_BAR"]
    inB["B"] --> netA
    inBBar["B_BAR"] --> netABar
    netA --> fOutNode["F_OUT Degraded"]
    netABar --> fBarOutNode["F_BAR_OUT Degraded"]
    fOutNode --> amp["Cross-Coupled Sense Amplifier"]
    fBarOutNode --> amp
    amp --> fullF["F Full Swing"]
    amp --> fullFBar["F_BAR Full Swing"]
    vdd["V_DD"] --> bias["Bias Generator"]
    bias --> amp
    gnd["GND"] --> bias
```

> **Explanation:** The two parallel NMOS networks create degraded differential outputs. The cross-coupled sense amplifier senses the differential and latches the result to full-swing logic levels. A bias generator provides the amplifier's operating current.

### 4.3 Dynamic CMOS Logic — Phase Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    clock["Phi Clock Signal"] --> pmosctrl["PMOS Precharge Control"]
    clock --> nmosctrl["NMOS Evaluate Network Enable"]
    pmosctrl --> prechg["OUT Precharged to V_DD when Phi LOW"]
    nmosctrl --> eval["OUT Conditionally Discharged when Phi HIGH"]
    prechg --> eval
    eval --> keeperCheck{"Output tries to drift?"}
    keeperCheck -- YES --> keeper["Bleeder PMOS Keeper restores V_DD"]
    keeperCheck -- NO --> hold["Output remains stable"]
    keeper --> outNode["V_OUT"]
    hold --> outNode
```

### 4.4 Domino Logic — Cascadable Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    stage1["Dynamic Stage 1"] --> inv1["Static CMOS Inverter"]
    inv1 --> stage2["Dynamic Stage 2"]
    stage2 --> inv2["Static CMOS Inverter"]
    inv2 --> stage3["Dynamic Stage 3"]
    clock["Phi"] --> stage1
    clock --> stage2
    clock --> stage3
```

> **Cascade Rule:** The *precharged* state of Stage 1 is a '1', which the inverter converts to a '0' — and '0' at the input of Stage 2's NMOS network is the **non-conducting** state, so Stage 2's output remains precharged (no spurious discharge). This makes the cascade race-free.

### 4.5 2:1 Multiplexer Using Transmission Gates

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA["Input A"] --> tgA["TG1 Select S"]
    inB["Input B"] --> tgB["TG2 Select S_BAR"]
    tgA --> outCommon["MUX Output Y"]
    tgB --> outCommon
    sCtrl["S"] --> tgA
    sCtrlBar["S_BAR"] --> tgB
```

> **Operation:** When $S = 1, \bar{S} = 0$: TG1 ON, TG2 OFF → $Y = A$. When $S = 0, \bar{S} = 1$: TG1 OFF, TG2 ON → $Y = B$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a CMOS transmission gate. Why is it preferred over a single NMOS or PMOS pass transistor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **CMOS transmission gate** is a bidirectional switch formed by the parallel combination of an **NMOS** and a **PMOS** transistor whose gates are driven by complementary control signals $C$ and $\bar{C}$.

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the comparison)**

- A single **NMOS pass transistor** suffers from a **threshold voltage drop** when passing a logic '1' — the output cannot exceed $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$.
- A single **PMOS pass transistor** suffers from a $\vert V_{Tp} \vert$ drop when passing a logic '0'.
- The **CMOS TG** combines both — the NMOS passes '0' cleanly and the PMOS passes '1' cleanly, producing a **full rail-to-rail output**.
- Additionally, the TG has a **near-constant on-resistance** across the input range and is **bidirectional**, making it ideal for multiplexers, latches, and analog switches.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "charge sharing" in dynamic MOS logic? How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Charge sharing** is a non-ideality in dynamic MOS logic where, during the **evaluate phase**, the charge stored on the output load capacitance $C_L$ is redistributed with internal node capacitances $C_x$ of the pull-down network.

**(1 mark for definition)**

- This causes the output voltage to drop from its precharged $V_{DD}$ to a value $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{C_L}{C_L + C_x}$, potentially causing a logic error.
- **(2 marks for mitigation):** The standard mitigation is to add a **bleeder PMOS (keeper) transistor** at the output node, weakly pulling the output back to $V_{DD}$ whenever it begins to drift below $V_{DD}$. Other mitigations include precharging internal nodes or designing with $C_L \gg C_x$.

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often draw the transmission gate with the PMOS on top of the NMOS in a *series* stack (a "transmission gate series chain"). This is **WRONG** — they must be in **parallel** with shared source/drain nodes.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In dynamic logic derivations, students forget to apply **charge conservation** and write $V_{final}$ without showing the algebra. The board examiner deducts 2 marks if the energy/charge conservation principle is not stated.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing **domino logic** (inverter-added, single-clock) with **np-CMOS** (PMOS pull-up network). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(A).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS transmission gate**. Derive the expression for its equivalent on-resistance and show that it is approximately constant over the input range. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **2:1 multiplexer** and a **1-bit full adder XOR cell** using CMOS transmission gates. Show the transistor-level schematics and explain the operation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CMOS Transmission Gate — Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**

A transmission gate consists of an NMOS and PMOS in parallel, with $C$ driving the NMOS gate and $\bar{C}$ driving the PMOS gate. The input and output nodes are common.

**Operation (2 marks):**

- When $C = 1, \bar{C} = 0$: Both NMOS and PMOS are ON. The TG acts as a low-resistance switch (ON-state resistance $R_{TG} \approx$ a few hundred ohms).
- When $C = 0, \bar{C} = 1$: Both OFF. The TG is a high-impedance open circuit.
- The TG passes both logic '0' and logic '1' **without threshold voltage drop**, because:
  - The NMOS passes '0' cleanly (with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$, $V_{DS}$ is small, $V_{out}$ reaches GND).
  - The PMOS passes '1' cleanly (with $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$, $V_{out}$ reaches $V_{DD}$).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For an NMOS in the linear region with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$:

$$R_{on,N} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N (V_{DD} - V_{Tn} - V_{DS}/2)}$$

For a PMOS in the linear region with $V_{SG} = V_{DD}$:

$$R_{on,P} = \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert - \vert V_{DS} \vert/2)}$$

The total TG resistance is the parallel combination:

$$R_{TG} = \frac{R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}}{R_{on,N} + R_{on,P}}$$

**Why $R_{TG}$ is approximately constant:** As $V_{in}$ rises from 0 to $V_{DD}$:
- $R_{on,N}$ **increases** (because $V_{GS} - V_{Tn} - V_{DS}/2$ decreases as $V_{in}$ rises).
- $R_{on,P}$ **decreases** (because the PMOS becomes more strongly ON).
- Their **parallel combination** remains nearly **constant** because one rises as the other falls. This is the key insight — by choosing $\beta_n \approx \beta_p$, the variation is minimized.

---

**(b) 2:1 MUX and Full Adder XOR Cell Using TGs (7 marks):**

**2:1 Multiplexer (3 marks):**

A 2:1 MUX selects between two inputs $A$ and $B$ based on a select line $S$.

**Schematic Description:**

- TG1 connects input $A$ to output $Y$ when $S = 1$.
- TG2 connects input $B$ to output $Y$ when $S = 0$ (i.e., $\bar{S} = 1$).
- The output $Y$ is given by:
$$Y = S \cdot A + \bar{S} \cdot B$$

**Operation:**
- $S = 1$: TG1 ON, TG2 OFF → $Y = A$
- $S = 0$: TG1 OFF, TG2 ON → $Y = B$

**1-bit Full Adder XOR Cell using TGs (4 marks):**

The SUM output of a full adder is:
$$\text{SUM} = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in}$$

This can be implemented as a chain of two 2:1 MUXes:
- First MUX: Selects $A$ or $\bar{A}$ based on $B$.
- Second MUX: Selects the result of the first MUX or its complement based on $C_{in}$.

**Transistor-level count:** A 2:1 MUX using TGs requires **4 transistors** (2 TGs × 2 transistors each). A full SUM circuit using TG-MUXes requires **8 transistors** for the SUM, plus additional logic for $C_{out}$.

**Boolean equations:**
$$\text{SUM} = A \oplus B \oplus C_{in} = (A \oplus B) \cdot \bar{C}_{in} + \overline{A \oplus B} \cdot C_{in}$$

$$C_{out} = A \cdot B + C_{in} \cdot (A \oplus B)$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q3(B).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of **Dynamic CMOS logic** with a circuit diagram of a 2-input dynamic NAND gate. Discuss the **precharge** and **evaluate** phases with proper waveforms. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **domino logic**? With diagrams, explain how it overcomes the **cascading limitation** of basic dynamic CMOS logic. Also briefly explain **np-CMOS logic**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dynamic CMOS Logic — 2-Input Dynamic NAND (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**

A 2-input dynamic NAND consists of:
- A **PMOS precharge transistor** $M_p$ with source = $V_{DD}$, gate = $\phi$, drain = OUT.
- An **NMOS evaluate network** consisting of two NMOS transistors in series ($M_{n1}$ and $M_{n2}$), with $A$ driving $M_{n1}$ and $B$ driving $M_{n2}$. Their common drain is OUT.
- A **load capacitance** $C_L$ at the OUT node (representing the parasitic gate capacitance of the next stage).

**Precharge Phase ($\phi = 0$) (2 marks):**

- The PMOS $M_p$ is ON (gate at 0, source at $V_{DD}$).
- The OUT node charges to $V_{DD}$ through $M_p$.
- All NMOS transistors in the evaluate network are OFF (because the clock is low).
- The output is **statically high** during this phase, regardless of the inputs.
- The waveform shows OUT = $V_{DD}$ flat during precharge.

**Evaluate Phase ($\phi = 1$) (2 marks):**

- The PMOS $M_p$ is OFF.
- The NMOS evaluate network turns ON based on the inputs.
- If $A = 1$ **AND** $B = 1$: Both NMOS are ON; OUT discharges to GND → OUT = 0.
- For any other input combination ($AB = 00, 01, 10$): At least one NMOS is OFF; OUT remains at $V_{DD}$ → OUT = 1.
- The waveform shows OUT conditionally transitioning from $V_{DD}$ to 0 only when $A = B = 1$.

**Truth Table Summary:**

| $\phi$ | A | B | OUT |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | X | X | $V_{DD}$ (precharge) |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | $V_{DD}$ (high) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | $V_{DD}$ (high) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | $V_{DD}$ (high) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 (discharged) |

**Note:** The output of the basic dynamic gate is *only valid during evaluate* — this is the cascading limitation.

---

**(b) Domino Logic and np-CMOS (7 marks):**

**Domino Logic — Concept (2 marks):**

Domino logic is a modification of dynamic CMOS that **adds a static CMOS inverter** at the output of each dynamic stage. The output of this inverter feeds the next dynamic stage.

**Why It Overcomes the Cascading Limitation (3 marks):**

- In a basic dynamic gate, the output is *precharged high* during the precharge phase. If this output drives the next dynamic gate's NMOS network directly, the next gate's precharge PMOS is fighting a NMOS that is ON — leading to a **race condition** and **uncontrolled current**.
- In domino logic, the **inverter inverts the precharged high to a low**, so the next stage's NMOS network is OFF during the precharge phase — no contention.
- During evaluate, when a stage evaluates to 0, the inverter output goes to 1, which can then turn ON the next stage's NMOS network — the "domino" wave propagates.
- Domino logic is **non-inverting in its precharge state**, allowing safe cascading.

**np-CMOS Logic (2 marks):**

- **np-CMOS** uses **PMOS pull-up** in the pull-up network and **NMOS pull-down** in the pull-down network, each activated by complementary clocks.
- During the precharge half-cycle, the PMOS network pulls the output high; during evaluate, the NMOS network conditionally pulls it low.
- Advantages over standard domino:
  - **No body effect** on the precharge device (PMOS body tied to $V_{DD}$).
  - **Faster rising transition** due to the PMOS network's higher drive in the active phase.
  - Used historically in **Intel Pentium-class microprocessors**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Dynamic CMOS | Domino | np-CMOS |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Cascadable | No (limited) | Yes | Yes |
| Inverting | Inverting | Non-inverting | Non-inverting |
| Body effect on precharge | Yes | Yes | **No** |
| Speed | Fast | Faster | **Fastest** |
| Complexity | Simple | +1 inverter | +clock routing |

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board Exam Must-Know Bullets):**

- **DPTL:** Uses *differential pairs* to recover full-swing output from threshold-degraded pass-transistor signals. Zero static power when the sense amplifier is properly biased.
- **Transmission Gate:** *NMOS* and *PMOS* in **parallel** (NOT series), with **complementary gate drives**. Passes both '0' and '1' with **zero threshold drop**.
- **TG On-Resistance:** $R_{TG} = (R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P})/(R_{on,N} + R_{on,P})$ — approximately **constant** over the input range.
- **TG Applications:** Multiplexers, latches, flip-flops, XOR/XNOR cells, analog switches, register files.
- **Dynamic MOS Logic:** Two-phase clocked operation — **Precharge** ($\phi = 0$, PMOS ON) and **Evaluate** ($\phi = 1$, NMOS network conditionally active).
- **Dynamic Logic Advantages:** Small area (no PUN), high speed, low power.
- **Dynamic Logic Disadvantages:** **Charge sharing**, **clock feedthrough**, **leakage**, **cascading limitation**, **race conditions**.
- **Charge Sharing Equation:** $V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L / (C_L + C_x)$.
- **Charge Sharing Mitigation:** **Keeper (bleeder) PMOS** at the output.
- **Domino Logic:** Dynamic stage + static inverter → *non-inverting*, fully cascadable.
- **np-CMOS:** PMOS pull-up + NMOS pull-down, complementary clocks → no body effect, faster.
- **Output Capacitance Formula:** $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (stored), $C_L V_{DD}^2$ (supplied per cycle).
- **Standard Mobility Ratio:** $\mu_n \approx 2.5 \mu_p$ — drives the typical sizing of $W_p \approx 2.5 W_n$ in static CMOS.
- **Clock Phasing Rule:** Inputs must settle *before* $\phi$ rises; otherwise, the dynamic stage may glitch.
- **Body Effect Equation:** $V_{Tn} = V_{Tn0} + \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ — increased $V_{SB}$ raises $V_{Tn}$, slowing the transistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark KTU question on this topic, always begin with the **circuit diagram** (1 mark), then state the **operation in two phases** (3 marks), derive the **resistance or charge-sharing equation** (4 marks), list the **advantages/disadvantages** (2 marks), and conclude with the **application/cascading solution** (4 marks). This structure has been the most consistently awarded full-marks template across KTU valuation keys for the last three examination cycles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **CMOS VLSI Design**, the *Basic Principles* refer to the foundational rules, structural conventions, and switching theory governing the construction of digital logic gates using **Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS)** technology. CMOS logic is built on the principle of **complementary pairing** between an **nMOS pull-down network (PDN)** and a **pMOS pull-up network (PUN)**, such that the output node is always connected to either $V_{DD}$ (logic **1**) or **GND** (logic **0**) — but never to both simultaneously under steady-state conditions.

The governing rules of CMOS design are:

1. **Rule 1 — Complementary Symmetry:** Every input driving a pMOS in the PUN must drive the corresponding series/parallel nMOS in the PDN.
2. **Rule 2 — Pull-Up Network (PUN):** Composed exclusively of **pMOS** transistors that turn ON when the input is **LOW** ($V_{in} = 0$).
3. **Rule 3 — Pull-Down Network (PDN):** Composed exclusively of **nMOS** transistors that turn ON when the input is **HIGH** ($V_{in} = V_{DD}$).
4. **Rule 4 — Static Logic Restoration:** In steady state, the output is always driven to a valid logic level through a low-resistance path, ensuring **no static current** flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND.
5. **Rule 5 — Ratio-Less Operation:** The output voltage levels are **independent of transistor sizes** (in the ideal static case), making CMOS logic inherently **ratioless**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU examiner frequently tests the **complementary structure** and the **three operating regions of the CMOS inverter** (Cutoff, Saturation, Triode). Memorize the threshold conditions: nMOS turns ON when $V_{GS} > V_{Tn}$, and pMOS turns ON when $V_{SG} > \vert V_{Tp} \vert$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Swinging Door" Model

Imagine a two-door corridor:

- The **pull-up network (pMOS)** is a door to a bright, sunlit garden (the $V_{DD}$ supply).
- The **pull-down network (nMOS)** is a door to a dark cellar (the GND sink).
- The **logic input** decides which door is unlocked.
- **Critically, only one door can be open at a time.** You either see the garden (logic 1) or the cellar (logic 0) — never both.

This analogy directly explains why CMOS is **statically power-efficient**: because there is *never* a direct path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state, no continuous current is "leaking" through the corridor. Power is only consumed during the brief moments when the doors are switching — a concept quantified as **dynamic power dissipation**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (90 nm node) | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Supply Voltage | $V_{DD}$ | **1.2** | V |
| Threshold Voltage (nMOS) | $V_{Tn}$ | **0.4** | V |
| Threshold Voltage (pMOS) | $V_{Tp}$ | **−0.4** | V |
| Oxide Capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox}$ | **~12–20** | fF/µm² |
| Mobility Ratio | $\mu_n / \mu_p$ | **~2 to 3** | dimensionless |
| Noise Margin (Low) | $NM_L$ | **0.4 $V_{DD}$** | V |
| Noise Margin (High) | $NM_H$ | **0.4 $V_{DD}$** | V |

> [!NOTE]
> The **mobility ratio** is critical because pMOS carriers (holes) move slower than nMOS carriers (electrons). To equalize the rise and fall delays, pMOS transistors are typically sized **2× to 3× wider** than nMOS in a symmetric inverter.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental CMOS Building Blocks

Every complex CMOS gate follows the same three-stage construction template:

1. **Identify the Boolean function** (e.g., $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ for NAND).
2. **Construct the PDN** using nMOS transistors in **series for AND, parallel for OR**.
3. **Construct the PUN** as the **DUAL network** of the PDN — replacing series with parallel and parallel with series — using pMOS transistors.

This is called the **Eulerian Dual / Graph Duality** rule of CMOS construction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CMOS Inverter — The Mother Cell

The **CMOS inverter** is the simplest CMOS gate and the foundation for all complex logic. It consists of:
- **One pMOS** (source tied to $V_{DD}$, drain tied to output)
- **One nMOS** (source tied to GND, drain tied to output)
- **Common gate input** driving both transistors simultaneously.

### 2.1.1 Switch-Level Operation

| Input $V_{in}$ | nMOS State | pMOS State | Output $V_{out}$ | Path |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| LOW (0 V) | **OFF** (cutoff) | **ON** (linear) | $V_{DD}$ (Logic 1) | PUN to $V_{DD}$ |
| HIGH ($V_{DD}$) | **ON** (linear) | **OFF** (cutoff) | 0 V (Logic 0) | PDN to GND |
| Transition | Both partially ON | Both partially ON | Region of high current | **Short-circuit current** |

> [!NOTE]
> During the **switching transient**, both transistors conduct simultaneously for a short interval, drawing a current spike from $V_{DD}$ to GND. This is the origin of **short-circuit power dissipation**.

---

### 2.1.2 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Five Operating Regions

The VTC of a CMOS inverter has **five distinct regions** as $V_{in}$ sweeps from 0 to $V_{DD}$:

1. **Region A ($0 \le V_{in} < V_{Tn}$):** nMOS in cutoff, pMOS in triode. $V_{out} = V_{DD}$.
2. **Region B ($V_{Tn} \le V_{in} < V_{out} + V_{Tp}$):** nMOS in saturation, pMOS in triode. $V_{out}$ begins to drop.
3. **Region C ($V_{in} = V_{out}$):** Both transistors in saturation. This is the **switching threshold** $V_M$ where $I_{Dn} = I_{Dp}$.
4. **Region D ($V_{out} + V_{Tp} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):** nMOS in triode, pMOS in saturation. $V_{out}$ continues falling.
5. **Region E ($V_{in} \ge V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):** nMOS in triode, pMOS in cutoff. $V_{out} = 0$.

---

## 2.2 The Switching Threshold Voltage ($V_M$)

The **switching threshold** $V_M$ is the point on the VTC where $V_{in} = V_{out}$ — the gate's logical "tipping point."

Setting $I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}$:

$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

At $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$:
- $V_{GS,n} = V_M$, so $V_{DS,n} = V_M$ (saturation confirmed since $V_{DS,n} \ge V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn}$).
- $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$, so $V_{SD,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$ (saturation confirmed).

Solving for $V_M$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}}}$$

Equivalently, defining $k_n = k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n$ and $k_p = k_p' \cdot (W/L)_p$:

$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p' \cdot (W/L)_p}{k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{k_p' \cdot (W/L)_p}{k_n' \cdot (W/L)_n}}}$$

For a **symmetric inverter** ($V_M = V_{DD}/2$):

$$\frac{k_p}{k_n} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \frac{k_n'}{k_p'} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{\mu_p C_{ox}} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

---

## 2.3 Noise Margins

Noise margins quantify the **robustness** of a logic gate against input noise. KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw the VTC and mark these points.

| Noise Margin | Formula | Meaning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| $V_{OH}$ | Maximum output in HIGH state | Output when $V_{in} = V_{OL}$ |
| $V_{OL}$ | Minimum output in LOW state | Output when $V_{in} = V_{OH}$ |
| $V_{IH}$ | Minimum input recognized as HIGH | $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ point on VTC |
| $V_{IL}$ | Maximum input recognized as LOW | $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ point on VTC |
| $NM_H$ | $V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High-state noise tolerance |
| $NM_L$ | $V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low-state noise tolerance |

For an ideal symmetric CMOS inverter: $NM_H = NM_L \approx 0.4 V_{DD}$.

---

## 2.4 Static and Dynamic Power Dissipation

### 2.4.1 Static Power

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

Where $I_{leak}$ is the **subthreshold leakage** and **reverse-biased junction leakage**. At modern nodes (< 65 nm), this is no longer negligible.

### 2.4.2 Dynamic Power (Dominant in CMOS)

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $f$ = clock frequency

### 2.4.3 Short-Circuit Power

$$P_{short} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$$

Where $\tau$ is the rise/fall time at the input.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$ | Switching threshold | V |
| 2 | $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ | nMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |
| 3 | $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$ | pMOS transconductance parameter | A/V² |
| 4 | $t_{PHL} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2 / 2}$ | HIGH-to-LOW delay | s |
| 5 | $t_{PLH} = \dfrac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD}/2)}{k_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 / 2}$ | LOW-to-HIGH delay | s |
| 6 | $t_p = \dfrac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2}$ | Propagation delay | s |
| 7 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Dynamic power | W |
| 8 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin | V |
| 9 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin | V |
| 10 | $I_{leak} \approx I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{Tn}/S}$ | Subthreshold leakage (S = subthreshold swing) | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by chip designers at Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, and NXP daily to size transistors in standard cell libraries. The dynamic power equation $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ is the *single most important equation* in modern low-power VLSI design — **reducing $V_{DD}$** is the most effective lever because it has a **quadratic** effect.

---

## 2.6 Construction Rules for Multi-Input CMOS Gates

| Gate Type | PDN Configuration | PUN Configuration |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **NAND of N inputs** | **N nMOS in SERIES** | **N pMOS in PARALLEL** |
| **NOR of N inputs** | **N nMOS in PARALLEL** | **N pMOS in SERIES** |
| **AND** | NAND + Inverter | — |
| **OR** | NOR + Inverter | — |
| **AOI (And-Or-Invert)** | Series-parallel nMOS | Dual pMOS |
| **OAI (Or-And-Invert)** | Parallel-series nMOS | Dual pMOS |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse the PUN/PDN layout. A foolproof mnemonic: **"nMOS pulls DOWN, pMOS pulls UP."** In a NAND gate, the nMOS must conduct *all inputs HIGH* to pull the output to GND → **series**. The pMOS only needs *one input LOW* to pull output to $V_{DD}$ → **parallel**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Switching Threshold Voltage $V_M$

**Given:** A CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.6$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.7$ V, $(W/L)_n = 2$, $(W/L)_p = 5$, $k_n' = 100$ µA/V², $k_p' = 50$ µA/V².

**Find:** The switching threshold $V_M$.

---

### Step 1: Compute the Transconductance Parameters

$$k_n = k_n' \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = 100 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2 \times 2 = 200 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$$

$$k_p = k_p' \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 50 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2 \times 5 = 250 \, \mu\text{A/V}^2$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Square-Root Ratio

$$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{250}{200}} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.1180$$

---

### Step 3: Apply the $V_M$ Formula

$$V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + 1.1180 \times (3.3 + 0.7)}{1 + 1.1180}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + 1.1180 \times 4.0}{2.1180}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.6 + 4.4721}{2.1180} = \frac{5.0721}{2.1180} \approx 2.3947 \text{ V}$$

---

### Step 4: Compare with $V_{DD}/2$ (Symmetric Reference)

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{3.3}{2} = 1.65 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_M = 2.39 \text{ V} > V_{DD}/2$, the inverter is **skewed toward a stronger pMOS** — meaning it has **stronger pull-up** than pull-down, and a **LOW-to-HIGH transition is faster** than HIGH-to-LOW.

---

### Step 5: Interpret the Result for Sizing

$$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = 1.118 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{k_p}{k_n} = 1.25$$

To make $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ (symmetric, equal rise/fall delay), we need $k_p/k_n = 1$. Therefore, we should **reduce $(W/L)_p$** by a factor of 1.25, or **increase $(W/L)_n$** by 1.25.

$$\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n^{new} = 2 \times 1.25 = 2.5 \;\;\text{or}\;\; \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p^{new} = \frac{5}{1.25} = 4.0$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Propagation Delay $t_{PHL}$ of CMOS Inverter

**Objective:** Derive the time for the output to fall from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ when the nMOS is discharging a load capacitance $C_L$.

---

### Step 1: KCL at the Output Node

During the HIGH-to-LOW transition, the nMOS is in **saturation** initially (when $V_{out} > V_{in} - V_{Tn} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$), then transitions to **triode** as $V_{out}$ drops.

The discharge current is:

$$I_D = C_L \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

---

### Step 2: Saturation Region Discharge ($V_{DD} \ge V_{out} \ge V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$)

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2$$

Time to discharge from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$:

$$t_1 = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{Tn}}{I_{D,sat}} = \frac{2 C_L V_{Tn}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})^2}$$

---

### Step 3: Triode Region Discharge ($V_{DD} - V_{Tn} > V_{out} > V_{DD}/2$)

The current in triode is:

$$I_{D,tri} = k_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{Tn}) V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}\right]$$

Setting $C_L \, dV_{out}/dt = -I_{D,tri}$ and integrating from $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ to $V_{DD}/2$:

$$t_2 = \frac{C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{3V_{DD} - 4V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}}\right)$$

---

### Step 4: Total $t_{PHL}$ (Approximation)

For $V_{Tn} \ll V_{DD}$ (deep-submicron approximation):

$$t_{PHL} \approx t_1 + t_2 \approx \frac{C_L}{k_n V_{DD}} \left[\frac{2V_{Tn}}{V_{DD}} + \ln(3) \right] \approx \frac{0.69 \, C_L}{k_n V_{DD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU-simplified form is: $t_{PHL} \approx \dfrac{0.69 \, C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$, which assumes nMOS in saturation throughout the discharge — a slight over-approximation acceptable for **hand-calculation problems** in the 14-mark section.

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Noise Margin Calculation

**Given:** A CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 2.5$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.5$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.5$ V, symmetric sizing. Find $V_{IL}$ and $NM_L$.

---

### Step 1: Identify $V_{IL}$ Condition

$V_{IL}$ is where $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$. In Region B, nMOS is in saturation, pMOS in triode.

$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -\frac{k_n (V_{in} - V_{Tn})}{k_p [2(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) - (V_{DD} - V_{out})]} = -1$$

For symmetric case ($k_n = k_p$):

$$V_{in} - V_{Tn} = 2(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) - (V_{DD} - V_{out})$$

---

### Step 2: Solve with Symmetric Condition

With $k_n = k_p$ and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$:

$$V_{IL} = \frac{3V_{DD} + 5V_{Tn}}{8}$$

Substituting: $V_{IL} = \dfrac{3(2.5) + 5(0.5)}{8} = \dfrac{7.5 + 2.5}{8} = \dfrac{10}{8} = 1.25$ V

---

### Step 3: Compute $V_{OL}$ and $NM_L$

For an ideal CMOS inverter, $V_{OL} \approx 0$ V.

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = 1.25 - 0 = 1.25 \text{ V}$$

This equals **$0.5 V_{DD}$**, confirming the symmetric design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbolic Form | Numerical (Example) | Design Insight |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $V_M$ | $\dfrac{V_{Tn} + r(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + r}$, $r = \sqrt{k_p/k_n}$ | 2.39 V | Indicates pMOS-dominated |
| $t_{PHL}$ | $\dfrac{0.69 C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}$ | — | Proportional to $C_L$ |
| $t_{PLH}$ | $\dfrac{0.69 C_L}{k_p (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}$ | — | Reduced by wider pMOS |
| $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | — | Quadratic in $V_{DD}$ |
| $NM_L$ | $\dfrac{3V_{DD} + 5V_{Tn}}{8}$ (symmetric) | 1.25 V | Robust to LOW noise |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Insight:** The switching threshold $V_M$ is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU ESE Module 3. Memorize the formula, the saturation assumption, and the symmetric-sizing condition $k_p = k_n$ for a perfect $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ gate.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Transistor-Level Schematic

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional architecture** of the CMOS inverter, mapping the input-driven complementary transistor pair to the output node.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVDD["VDD Supply 3.3V"]
    nodeInput["Vin Input Node"]
    nodePMOS["pMOS Transistor MP - Pull Up Network"]
    nodeNMOS["nMOS Transistor MN - Pull Down Network"]
    nodeOut["Vout Output Node"]
    nodeGND["GND Ground 0V"]
    nodeCL["Load Capacitance CL"]

    nodeVDD --- nodePMOS
    nodePMOS --- nodeOut
    nodeInput --- nodePMOS
    nodeInput --- nodeNMOS
    nodeNMOS --- nodeGND
    nodeOut --- nodeCL
    nodeGND --- nodeCL
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **pMOS (MP)** sits between $V_{DD}$ and $V_{out}$, acting as the **pull-up switch**.
- The **nMOS (MN)** sits between $V_{out}$ and **GND**, acting as the **pull-down switch**.
- The **gate input** drives both transistors simultaneously with complementary polarity.
- The **load capacitance $C_L$** is the parasitic + interconnect capacitance at the output node, which the inverter must charge/discharge during switching.

---

## 4.2 CMOS NAND Gate — Structural Block Architecture

A **2-input CMOS NAND gate** uses **two nMOS in series** (PDN) and **two pMOS in parallel** (PUN).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVDD["VDD"]
    nodeA["Input A"]
    nodeB["Input B"]
    nodeP1["pMOS MP1 - Gate A"]
    nodeP2["pMOS MP2 - Gate B"]
    nodeOut["Vout NAND_AB"]
    nodeN1["nMOS MN1 - Gate A"]
    nodeN2["nMOS MN2 - Gate B"]
    nodeGND["GND"]

    nodeVDD --- nodeP1
    nodeVDD --- nodeP2
    nodeP1 --- nodeOut
    nodeP2 --- nodeOut
    nodeA --- nodeP1
    nodeB --- nodeP2
    nodeA --- nodeN1
    nodeB --- nodeN2
    nodeN1 --- nodeN2
    nodeOut --- nodeN1
    nodeN2 --- nodeGND
```

**Operational Truth Table Mapping:**

| A | B | MP1 | MP2 | MN1 | MN2 | Vout | Function |
|:-:|:-:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:----:|:--------:|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | GND | 0 |

---

## 4.3 CMOS NOR Gate — Structural Block Architecture

A **2-input CMOS NOR gate** uses **two nMOS in parallel** (PDN) and **two pMOS in series** (PUN).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVDD["VDD"]
    nodeA["Input A"]
    nodeB["Input B"]
    nodeP1["pMOS MP1 - Gate A"]
    nodeP2["pMOS MP2 - Gate B"]
    nodeOut["Vout NOR_AB"]
    nodeN1["nMOS MN1 - Gate A"]
    nodeN2["nMOS MN2 - Gate B"]
    nodeGND["GND"]

    nodeVDD --- nodeP1
    nodeP1 --- nodeP2
    nodeP2 --- nodeOut
    nodeA --- nodeP1
    nodeB --- nodeP2
    nodeOut --- nodeN1
    nodeOut --- nodeN2
    nodeA --- nodeN1
    nodeB --- nodeN2
    nodeN1 --- nodeGND
    nodeN2 --- nodeGND
```

---

## 4.4 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Block Flow Representation

The VTC is the most important graph in CMOS design. The following Mermaid block describes the **state transitions** as $V_{in}$ ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RegionA
    RegionA: Region A - Vin between 0 and VTn - Vout equals VDD
    RegionA --> RegionB: Vin crosses VTn threshold
    RegionB: Region B - Vin in VTN to VOUT plus VTp range - nMOS in saturation, pMOS in triode
    RegionB --> RegionC: Vin equals Vout - Switching point VM
    RegionC: Region C - Both transistors in saturation - Maximum drain current
    RegionC --> RegionD: Vin exceeds Vout plus VTp magnitude
    RegionD: Region D - nMOS in triode, pMOS in saturation - Vout continues to fall
    RegionD --> RegionE: Vin exceeds VDD minus VTp magnitude
    RegionE: Region E - Vout equals 0V - pMOS cutoff, nMOS in triode
    RegionE --> [*]
```

---

## 4.5 CMOS Design Rule Mapping — Duality Construction

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeBool["Boolean Function F_AB"]
    nodePDN["Step 1 - Construct PDN - nMOS only - Series for AND, Parallel for OR"]
    nodePUN["Step 2 - Construct PUN - pMOS only - DUAL of PDN - Series becomes Parallel"]
    nodeOut["Step 3 - Combine - Vout at common node - Verify no static path from VDD to GND"]
    nodeVerify["Step 4 - Verify - Inversion property - Check steady state current equals zero"]

    nodeBool --> nodePDN
    nodePDN --> nodePUN
    nodePUN --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeVerify
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
List the **three operating regions** of a CMOS inverter and state the bias conditions for the nMOS and pMOS in each region when the input transitions from LOW to HIGH.

**Model Answer:**

During the LOW-to-HIGH input transition, the CMOS inverter passes through the following three regions:

1. **Cutoff → Saturation:** When $V_{in} = 0$, the nMOS is in cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{Tn}$) and the pMOS is in linear/triode ($V_{SG} > \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ and $V_{SD} < V_{SG} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$). The output is held at $V_{DD}$.

2. **Saturation → Triode (Switching Region):** As $V_{in}$ increases past $V_{Tn}$, the nMOS enters saturation. The pMOS remains in triode (initially) and then moves to saturation as $V_{out}$ drops. The output begins to fall.

3. **Triode → Cutoff:** When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$, the nMOS enters the triode region (linear) and the pMOS enters cutoff. The output reaches 0 V.

**Final Bias at $V_{in} = V_{DD}$:** nMOS in triode ($V_{DS} = V_{out} < V_{GS} - V_{Tn}$), pMOS in cutoff ($V_{SG} = 0 < \vert V_{Tp}\vert$).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define **switching threshold voltage** $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. Why is it preferred to design the inverter with $V_M = V_{DD}/2$?

**Model Answer:**

The **switching threshold voltage $V_M$** is defined as the point on the voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) where $V_{in} = V_{out}$. At this point, both the nMOS and pMOS transistors are typically in saturation, and the nMOS drain current equals the pMOS drain current: $I_{Dn,sat} = I_{Dp,sat}$.

**Reasons for preferring $V_M = V_{DD}/2$:**

1. **Equal noise margins:** $NM_L = NM_H$, providing symmetric robustness against noise in both logic states.
2. **Maximum noise immunity:** Maximizes the sum $(NM_L + NM_H)$, the largest square inscribable in the VTC.
3. **Equal rise and fall delays:** The inverter has balanced dynamic performance for both LOW-to-HIGH and HIGH-to-LOW transitions.
4. **Predictable digital behavior:** Provides the most reliable switching with the widest permissible input voltage range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS inverter**. Derive the expression for the **switching threshold voltage $V_M$** in terms of device parameters.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $V_{Tn} = 0.7$ V, $V_{Tp} = -0.7$ V, $k_n' = 60$ µA/V², $k_p' = 25$ µA/V², and aspect ratios $(W/L)_n = 1.5$, $(W/L)_p = 4.5$, compute the switching threshold $V_M$ and comment on the symmetry of the design.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

The CMOS inverter consists of:
- A **pMOS transistor MP** with its source connected to $V_{DD}$, drain connected to the output node $V_{out}$, and gate connected to the input $V_{in}$.
- An **nMOS transistor MN** with its source connected to GND, drain connected to $V_{out}$, and gate connected to $V_{in}$.
- The bulk of MP is tied to $V_{DD}$ and the bulk of MN is tied to GND.

**Operation:**
- When $V_{in} = 0$ V: $V_{GS,n} = 0 < V_{Tn}$, so nMOS is OFF. $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} > \vert V_{Tp}\vert$, so pMOS is ON, pulling $V_{out}$ to $V_{DD}$.
- When $V_{in} = V_{DD}$: $V_{GS,n} = V_{DD} > V_{Tn}$, so nMOS is ON. $V_{SG,p} = 0$, so pMOS is OFF, pulling $V_{out}$ to GND.

**Derivation of $V_M$:**

At the switching threshold, $V_{in} = V_{out} = V_M$. Both transistors are in saturation, so:

$$I_{Dn} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2$$

$$I_{Dp} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Equating the two currents (KCL at the output node):

$$\frac{k_n}{2}(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

Solving for $V_M$:

$$V_M \left(\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}\right) = \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

$$\boxed{V_M = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Compute Device Transconductances**

$$k_n = k_n' \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \text{ µA/V}^2$$

$$k_p = k_p' \cdot \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_p = 25 \times 4.5 = 112.5 \text{ µA/V}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the Ratio**

$$\sqrt{\frac{k_p}{k_n}} = \sqrt{\frac{112.5}{90}} = \sqrt{1.25} = 1.1180$$

**Step 3: Apply the $V_M$ Formula**

$$V_M = \frac{0.7 + 1.1180 \times (3.3 + 0.7)}{1 + 1.1180}$$

$$V_M = \frac{0.7 + 1.1180 \times 4.0}{2.1180} = \frac{0.7 + 4.4721}{2.1180} = \frac{5.1721}{2.1180}$$

$$\boxed{V_M \approx 2.4421 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4: Symmetry Commentary**

The ideal symmetric value is $V_{DD}/2 = 3.3/2 = 1.65$ V. Since $V_M = 2.44$ V $> 1.65$ V, the inverter is **pMOS-skewed** (the pMOS is relatively stronger than the nMOS). This means:
- LOW-to-HIGH transitions are **faster** than HIGH-to-LOW transitions.
- The HIGH-state noise margin $NM_H$ is **larger** than the LOW-state noise margin $NM_L$.
- For symmetric design, $(W/L)_p$ should be reduced or $(W/L)_n$ should be increased such that $k_p/k_n = 1$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a transistor-level schematic, explain the construction and operation of a **CMOS 2-input NAND gate**. State the expression for its switching threshold voltage.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare the **static and dynamic power dissipation** in CMOS logic. Show that dynamic power is the dominant component and derive the expression $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Schematic Description:**

A CMOS 2-input NAND gate has:
- **PDN:** Two nMOS transistors (MN1, MN2) connected in **series** between $V_{out}$ and GND. MN1 has gate = A, MN2 has gate = B.
- **PUN:** Two pMOS transistors (MP1, MP2) connected in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and $V_{out}$. MP1 has gate = A, MP2 has gate = B.

**Operation (Truth Table Analysis):**

| A | B | PDN Path | PUN Path | Vout |
|:-:|:-:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Both OFF (open) | Both ON → $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | MN1 OFF (open) | MP1 ON → $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | MN2 OFF (open) | MP2 ON → $V_{DD}$ | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | Both ON → GND | Both OFF (open) | 0 |

This implements $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$, which is the NAND function.

**Switching Threshold for the NAND Gate:**

For a 2-input NAND with equal-sized pMOS and nMOS, the switching threshold is derived by considering that when both inputs are at $V_M$, two nMOS are in series (effective $L$ doubles) and two pMOS are in parallel (effective $W$ doubles):

$$V_M^{NAND} = \frac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p^{eff}/k_n^{eff}}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p^{eff}/k_n^{eff}}}$$

where $k_n^{eff} = k_n/2$ (series) and $k_p^{eff} = 2 k_p$ (parallel). Thus $\sqrt{k_p^{eff}/k_n^{eff}} = 2\sqrt{k_p/k_n}$.

**Design Insight:** A NAND gate has a *larger* noise margin on the LOW side compared to a NOR gate of identical sizing, because the series nMOS path is weaker. This is why NAND gates are preferred in high-noise environments.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Static Power | Dynamic Power |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Origin** | Leakage current ($I_{leak}$) flowing from $V_{DD}$ to GND even when output is not switching | Capacitive charging/discharging of load during switching |
| **Expression** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ |
| **Typical Magnitude** | µW range (modern nodes) | mW range |
| **Dependence on Activity** | None — always present | Linear in switching activity $\alpha$ and frequency $f$ |
| **Dependence on $V_{DD}$** | Linear | **Quadratic** |

**Derivation of Dynamic Power:**

Consider a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. When the output switches from LOW to HIGH, charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ is drawn from the $V_{DD}$ supply. When the output switches from HIGH to LOW, this charge is dumped to GND (no energy drawn from supply, but the previous charge stored $C_L V_{DD}^2/2$ is dissipated in the nMOS).

**Energy per Switching Event (LOW → HIGH):**

The current $i(t)$ from the supply during charging is $i(t) = C_L \, dv/dt$, where $v$ is the output voltage rising from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The energy drawn from the supply:

$$E_{VDD} = \int_0^{T} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dv = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Energy Stored in the Capacitor at End of Charging:**

$$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Energy Dissipated in pMOS During Charging:**

$$E_{pMOS} = E_{VDD} - E_C = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

When the output switches back HIGH → LOW, the capacitor discharges through the nMOS, dissipating the stored $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ in the nMOS.

**Total Energy per Complete Cycle:**

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \text{ (during charge)} + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \text{ (during discharge)} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**If switching occurs $\alpha f$ times per second on average** ($\alpha$ = switching activity, $f$ = clock frequency):

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha f \cdot E_{cycle} = \alpha f \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f}$$

**Why Dominant?**
In modern CMOS circuits, switching frequencies are in the GHz range and load capacitances are in the fF–pF range, giving $P_{dyn}$ values in the milliwatt range. Static leakage, although growing at sub-65 nm nodes, remains 3–4 orders of magnitude smaller in well-designed circuits. Therefore, **dynamic power is the dominant power dissipation mechanism** in active CMOS circuits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Forgetting the saturation assumption in $V_M$ derivation:** Students often write the nMOS current in triode region. *Always* verify $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{Tn}$ at the assumed $V_M$ point. Loss: 2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention for $V_{Tp}$:** $V_{Tp}$ is negative. Use $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$ in the formula. Writing "$V_{Tp}$" without absolute value is a common error. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing PUN and PDN in NAND/NOR:** A NAND has nMOS in **series** and pMOS in **parallel** — not the other way. Drawing it wrong loses 3–4 marks immediately.
> 4. **Omitting the dual-network justification in PUN construction:** Don't just draw the PUN; *state* that it is the dual of the PDN. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **In power derivation, missing the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in stored energy:** $E_C = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$, not $CV^2$. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **CMOS Inverter Structure:** pMOS pull-up (source at $V_{DD}$), nMOS pull-down (source at GND), common gate input.
- [x] **Five VTC Regions:** Cutoff–Triode, Saturation–Triode, Saturation–Saturation (at $V_M$), Triode–Saturation, Triode–Cutoff.
- [x] **Switching Threshold Formula:** $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_p/k_n}}$
- [x] **Symmetric Sizing:** For $V_M = V_{DD}/2$, we need $k_p = k_n$, i.e., $(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n = \mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2$–$3$.
- [x] **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$. For symmetric design, both $\approx 0.4 V_{DD}$.
- [x] **NAND Rule:** nMOS **series** (PDN), pMOS **parallel** (PUN).
- [x] **NOR Rule:** nMOS **parallel** (PDN), pMOS **series** (PUN).
- [x] **PUN is the DUAL of the PDN** — swap series ↔ parallel.
- [x] **Dynamic Power Equation:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$ is the key.
- [x] **Static Power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — only dominant in idle/leakage-limited circuits.
- [x] **Propagation Delay:** $t_{PHL} \approx 0.69 C_L / [k_n (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})]$; $t_{PLH}$ similar with pMOS parameters.
- [x] **Energy per Cycle:** $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ (per charge + discharge cycle).
- [x] **No Static Current Path:** The hallmark of CMOS — this is why CMOS is power-efficient.
- [x] **Mobility Ratio:** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$–$3$ drives pMOS upsizing.

### 📌 KTU-Exam-Style Memory Hooks

| Mnemonic | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| **"nMOS pulls DOWN, pMOS pulls UP"** | Function of each transistor type |
| **"Series for AND in nMOS, Parallel for AND in pMOS"** | NAND gate structure |
| **"Dual of PDN gives PUN"** | Universal CMOS construction rule |
| **"Quadratic in VDD is the key"** | Why voltage scaling reduces power dramatically |

### 🎯 Most Frequently Asked Concepts in KTU Module 3

1. CMOS Inverter VTC + derivation of $V_M$ ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
2. Noise margin calculation ⭐⭐⭐⭐
3. NAND/NOR construction ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐
4. Power dissipation analysis ⭐⭐⭐⭐
5. Propagation delay derivation ⭐⭐⭐
6. PUN/PDN duality for complex gates ⭐⭐⭐⭐

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed and Power Dissipation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Speed and Power Dissipation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI) design, **Speed** refers to the rate at which a digital CMOS circuit can reliably process and propagate binary information. It is quantitatively characterized by the **propagation delay ($t_p$)** — the time taken for a signal transition (50% point of input to 50% point of output) to traverse a logic gate. The reciprocal metric is the **maximum operating frequency ($f_{max} = 1/t_p$)**. **Power Dissipation** is the rate at which electrical energy is converted into heat by a CMOS circuit during operation. For a CMOS logic gate it is the sum of three physically distinct mechanisms: $$ P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{short\text{-}circuit} + P_{static} $$ where each component has its own physical origin (capacitive charging, simultaneous conduction of PMOS/NMOS, and leakage currents respectively). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Definition:** Speed and power are *not* independent — they are coupled through the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ and the load capacitance $C_L$.
