The **Power-Delay Product (PDP)** and **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** are the canonical *figure-of-merit (FoM)* metrics used by KTU examiners to judge a design. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Think of a CMOS gate as a **water tap connected to a big overhead tank** (the supply rail at $V_{DD}$): | Hardware Element | Water Analogy | Engineering Quantity | |---|---|---| | Water tank height | Pressure of supply | Supply voltage **$V_{DD}$** | | Bucket at tap outlet | Capacitor to be filled | Load capacitance **$C_L$** | | Tap opening size | Transistor channel strength | W/L ratio, $\mu C_{ox}$ | | Time to fill bucket | How fast the gate works | Propagation delay **$t_p$** | | Water spilled per second | Energy wasted as heat | Power dissipation **$P$** | | Drip from closed tap | Trickle when "off" | Leakage current **$I_{leak}$** | - **Speed** = how fast the bucket fills. Bigger tap (wider transistor) → faster fill, but more water rushes per unit time → **higher power**. - **Power** = total water (energy) consumed per second, regardless of whether work was done. - A perfect VLSI design is a tap that **fills the bucket fast (high speed) using the minimum possible water (low power)**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes *low-power VLSI* because modern chips (mobile SoCs, IoT) operate near **~1 W** envelopes while containing billions of gates. Power dissipated as heat directly limits clock frequency — a chip that overheats automatically *throttles down*, killing speed. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants) - **$V_{DD}$**: Supply voltage (modern nodes: 0.7 V – 1.2 V) - **$C_L$**: Total load capacitance (gate + interconnect + diffusion) - **$f_{clk}$**: Clock frequency (Hz) - **$\alpha$**: Switching activity factor ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$) - **$t_{pHL}, t_{pLH}$**: High-to-Low and Low-to-High propagation delays - **$t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2$**: Average propagation delay - **PDP** (Power-Delay Product): **Joules**, energy per switching event - **EDP** (Energy-Delay Product): **Joule·seconds**, quality metric - **Thermal constant (silicon)**: $k_{th} \approx 1.5 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ — used in packaging constraints - **$V_T$ (threshold voltage)**: typically **0.3 – 0.5 V** at modern nodes - **MOS electron mobility $\mu_n \approx 1350 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$** and hole mobility $\mu_p \approx 480 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$** (standard room-temperature silicon values) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of CMOS Power Dissipation ### Pillar A — Dynamic (Switching) Power $P_{dyn}$ This is the **dominant** component in any well-designed CMOS gate (≈ 70–90% of $P_{total}$ in active operation). - **Physical origin:** Charging and discharging the load capacitor $C_L$ between $0$ and $V_{DD}$. - **Energy per transition:** $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ delivered by supply, but $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging. Net energy consumed per full cycle = $C_L V_{DD}^2$. - **Why it dominates:** It scales with $V_{DD}^2$ — reducing $V_{DD}$ from 5 V to 1 V cuts dynamic power by **25×**. ### Pillar B — Short-Circuit Power $P_{sc}$ - **Physical origin:** During the brief input-slew window when $V_{in}$ is between $V_{TN}$ and $V_{DD} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$, **both** NMOS and PMOS conduct simultaneously, creating a direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND. - **Typical magnitude:** 10–20% of dynamic power; vanishes if input rise/fall times are zero. - **Proportional to:** $P_{sc} \propto V_{DD}^3 \cdot f \cdot \tau_{in} / C_L$ where $\tau_{in}$ is the input rise/fall time. ### Pillar C — Static (Leakage) Power $P_{stat}$ In a **static CMOS inverter at steady state**, ideally no DC path exists. But at deep-submicron nodes, subthreshold leakage alone can dominate total power. $$ P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} $$ where $I_{leak}$ is the sum of: | Leakage Component | Symbol | Physical Cause | |---|---|---| | Subthreshold leakage | $I_{sub}$ | Weak inversion current when $V_{GS} < V_T$ | | Gate-oxide tunneling | $I_{gate}$ | Quantum tunneling through thin $\text{SiO}_2$ | | Reverse-biased junction leakage | $I_{jn}$ | Band-to-band tunneling at source/drain wells | ## 2.2 Speed Analysis — Propagation Delay of CMOS Inverter For a static CMOS inverter driving load $C_L$ with equivalent on-resistance $R_{eq}$ of the pull-down (NMOS) network, the first-order Elmore delay gives: $$ t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 \, R_n \, C_L \qquad t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 \, R_p \, C_L $$ $$ \boxed{\,t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} \approx \frac{0.69 \, C_L}{2}(R_n + R_p)\,} $$ Using the long-channel saturation current $I_{DSAT} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2$ with $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$: $$ t_{pHL} \approx \frac{C_L}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})}\left[\frac{V_{DD} - V_{TN}}{2} + \frac{V_{TN}}{2}\ln\!\left(3 - \frac{4V_{TN}}{V_{DD}}\right)\right] $$ **Simplified form** (used in KTU numericals): $$ \boxed{\,t_p \approx \frac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{2 \, I_{DSAT}} = \frac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}\,} $$ ## 2.3 Speed–Power Coupling — Figures of Merit ### Power-Delay Product (PDP) $$ PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p $$ For a CMOS gate switching every cycle ($\alpha = 1$): $$ PDP = C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n(V_{DD}-V_{TN})^2} = \frac{C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{k_n (V_{DD}-V_{TN})^2} $$ At high $V_{DD}$: $PDP \to C_L^2 V_{DD} / k_n$ — only weakly dependent on $V_{DD}$. ### Energy-Delay Product (EDP) $$ EDP = PDP \cdot t_p \quad [\text{Joule·seconds}] $$ EDP is the **true quality metric** — it penalizes designs that are either too slow *or* too power-hungry. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering use:** PDP tells the battery how much energy per operation is needed (crucial for smartphones). EDP is used by chip architects to compare competing microarchitectures (Intel vs AMD, ARM vs RISC-V). Both appear routinely in KTU 14-mark questions. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table completely — it covers ~85% of all KTU numerical questions on this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Dynamic power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f$ | W | Dominant in active CMOS | | 2 | Energy per switching cycle | $E_{cycle} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2$ | J | Independent of $f$ | | 3 | Static / leakage power | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | W | Dominant in sleep mode | | 4 | Short-circuit power | $P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD}-2V_T)^3 \tau f$ | W | Depends on input slew $\tau$ | | 5 | Total power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{sc} + P_{stat}$ | W | Always the sum | | 6 | Avg. prop. delay (inverter) | $t_p \approx C_L V_{DD} / [k_n(V_{DD}-V_T)^2]$ | s | Long-channel approximation | | 7 | RC delay | $t_{p} \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq} C_L$ | s | First-order | | 8 | PDP | $PDP = P_{avg} \cdot t_p$ | J | Energy per op | | 9 | EDP | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | J·s | Quality FoM | | 10 | Max clock freq | $f_{max} \approx 1/(2 t_p)$ | Hz | For pipelined logic | | 11 | Subthreshold current | $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS}-V_T)/(nV_T)} (1-e^{-V_{DS}/V_T})$ | A | Exponential in $V_T$ | | 12 | DIBL coefficient | $\Delta V_T = -\eta \cdot V_{DS}$ | V | Worsens leakage | | 13 | On-current (sat) | $I_{DSAT} = \frac{\mu C_{ox} W}{2L}(V_{GS}-V_T)^2$ | A | Square-law model | | 14 | Energy stored in cap | $E = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | J | At $V_{DD}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Watch out:** The vertical bar $\vert$ symbol has been replaced with `\vert` everywhere in the formula sheet to prevent markdown table breakage. KTU examiners expect students to write it in Roman style — do not omit the absolute value. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Dynamic Power $P_{dyn}$ from First Principles **Starting setup:** A CMOS inverter charging a load capacitor $C_L$ from $0 \to V_{DD}$, then discharging from $V_{DD} \to 0$. Repeat every clock period $T = 1/f$. **Step 1 — Charge delivered by the supply during the rising transition** When the output rises from 0 to $V_{DD}$, a charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ flows from the $V_{DD}$ rail. Energy drawn from the supply: $$ E_{supply} = \int_0^{T/2} V_{DD} \, i(t) \, dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor after charging** $$ E_{cap} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \, dQ = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \, C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging** By conservation of energy: $$ E_{dissipated,\,charging} = E_{supply} - E_{cap} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Energy dissipated during the falling transition** During discharge, the energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ stored in the capacitor is **completely** dissipated as heat in the NMOS network (no recovery path to supply in a standard CMOS inverter). $$ E_{dissipated,\,discharging} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Total energy per full clock cycle (0→1→0)** $$ E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Average dynamic power** The circuit completes $f$ cycles per second. If the switching activity (probability of transition) is $\alpha$: $$ \boxed{\,P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical insight:** The capacitor stores energy once, but loses it twice (once on charge, once on discharge). This is why the supply must deliver $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ of *unrecoverable* energy per transition. ## 3.2 Derivation: Propagation Delay of CMOS Inverter (Discharging Phase) Consider an NMOS pull-down transistor with $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ discharging $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ toward 0. **Step 1 — KCL at the output node** $$ C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{DS}(V_{out}) $$ **Step 2 — Use square-law saturation current (NMOS in saturation while $V_{out} > V_{DD} - V_{TN}$)** $$ I_{DS} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2 \quad \text{(constant during saturation)} $$ **Step 3 — Solve until $V_{out}$ reaches $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$** $$ \Delta t_1 = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{TN}}{I_{DS}} = \frac{2 C_L V_{TN}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2} $$ **Step 4 — After entering triode, integrate the full I-V equation** For $V_{out} < V_{DD} - V_{TN}$, NMOS is in triode: $$ I_{DS} = k_n\!\left[(V_{DD} - V_{TN})V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}\right] $$ Integrating from $V_{out} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ down to $V_{out} = V_{DD}/2$: $$ \Delta t_2 = \frac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{TN})} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\ln\!\left(3 - \frac{4V_{TN}}{V_{DD}}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Total high-to-low delay** $$ t_{pHL} = \Delta t_1 + \Delta t_2 = \frac{C_L}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{TN})}\left[\frac{V_{DD} - V_{TN}}{2} + \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\ln\!\left(3 - \frac{4V_{TN}}{V_{DD}}\right)\right] $$ **Step 6 — First-order approximation (ignoring the logarithmic correction)** For KTU numericals, the simpler **discharging-current-driven** form is used: $$ \boxed{\,t_{pHL} \approx \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \, I_{DSAT}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}\,} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Power-Delay Product (PDP) **Step 1 — Substitute $P_{dyn}$ and $t_p$** $$ PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p = \left(\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f\right)\left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Simplify** $$ \boxed{\,PDP = \frac{\alpha \, C_L^2 \, V_{DD}^3 \, f}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2}\,} $$ **Step 3 — Asymptotic analysis** - **At high $V_{DD}$** ($V_{DD} \gg V_{TN}$): $PDP \to \dfrac{\alpha C_L^2 V_{DD} f}{k_n}$ — linearly increasing with $V_{DD}$. - **At low $V_{DD}$** ($V_{DD} \to V_{TN}$): $PDP \to \infty$ — circuit cannot switch fast enough. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L = 50 \text{ fF}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$ with switching activity $\alpha = 0.2$ and clock $f = 1 \text{ GHz}$. NMOS has $k_n = 500 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $V_{TN} = 0.4 \text{ V}$. Compute $P_{dyn}$, $t_p$, PDP, and EDP. **Solution:** Step 1 — Dynamic power: $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f = (0.2)(50 \times 10^{-15})(1.2)^2(10^9) $$ $$ P_{dyn} = (0.2)(50)(1.44)(10^{-6}) = 14.4 \times 10^{-6} = \mathbf{14.4 \,\mu W} $$ Step 2 — Propagation delay: $$ t_p = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{k_n(V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-15})(1.2)}{(500 \times 10^{-6})(0.8)^2} $$ $$ t_p = \frac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{(500 \times 10^{-6})(0.64)} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-15}}{320 \times 10^{-6}} = 187.5 \times 10^{-12} = \mathbf{187.5 \, ps} $$ Step 3 — PDP: $$ PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p = (14.4 \times 10^{-6})(187.5 \times 10^{-12}) = \mathbf{2.7 \times 10^{-15} \, J = 2.7 \, fJ} $$ Step 4 — EDP: $$ EDP = PDP \cdot t_p = (2.7 \times 10^{-15})(187.5 \times 10^{-12}) = \mathbf{5.06 \times 10^{-25} \, J{\cdot}s} $$ ## 3.5 Trade-off Summary Table | Technique | Effect on $V_{DD}$ | Effect on Speed | Effect on Power | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---|---| | Reduce $V_{DD}$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ quadratically | $P \downarrow$ as $V^2$ | Speed loss | | Reduce $V_T$ | same | $\uparrow$ (faster) | Leakage $\uparrow$ exponentially | Static power | | Increase $W/L$ | same | $\uparrow$ (lower $R_{on}$) | $P_{dyn} \uparrow$ (larger $C_L$) | Area + cap | | Clock gating | same | same | $P_{dyn} \downarrow$ by $\alpha$ | Design complexity | | Multi-$V_{DD}$ | mixed | mixed | Mixed | Routing complexity | | Pipeline deeper | same | $\uparrow$ throughput | same per cycle, more cycles | Latency | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CMOS Inverter — Power Dissipation Anatomy This block diagram shows the three physical sources of power dissipation in a static CMOS inverter and where each energy loss occurs. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeVDD["VDD Supply Rail"] nodeIn["Vin Input Signal"] nodeInv["CMOS Inverter
PMOS plus NMOS"] nodeOut["Vout Output Node"] nodeCL["Load Capacitance CL"] nodeGND["GND Rail"] nodePdyn["Pdynamic
Charging Discharging CL
alpha CL VDD squared f"] nodePsc["Pshort circuit
Simultaneous PMOS NMOS conduction
during Vin transition"] nodePstat["Pstatic leakage
Subthreshold current
Gate oxide tunneling
Junction leakage"] nodeVDD --> nodeInv nodeIn --> nodeInv nodeInv --> nodeOut nodeOut --> nodeCL nodeCL --> nodeGND nodeInv --> nodeGND nodeVDD -.-> nodePdyn nodeInv -.-> nodePsc nodeVDD -.-> nodePstat ``` ## 4.2 Speed vs Power — The Design Trade-off Curve A high-level view of how the classic VLSI design knob (supply voltage $V_{DD}$) simultaneously moves speed and power. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeAxis["VDD axis left to right"] nodeLowVDD["Low VDD
e.g. 0.7 V"] nodeMidVDD["Mid VDD
e.g. 1.0 V"] nodeHighVDD["High VDD
e.g. 1.5 V"] nodeLowVDD --> nodelowPdyn["Pdyn LOW
scaled by V squared"] nodeLowVDD --> nodelowSpeed["Speed SLOW
long tp"] nodeLowVDD --> nodelowLeak["Leakage HIGH
low VT needed"] nodeMidVDD --> nodemidPdyn["Pdyn MODERATE"] nodeMidVDD --> nodemidSpeed["Speed MODERATE"] nodeMidVDD --> nodemidLeak["Leakage MODERATE"] nodeHighVDD --> nodehighPdyn["Pdyn HIGH
V squared term dominates"] nodeHighVDD --> nodehighSpeed["Speed FAST
short tp"] nodeHighVDD --> nodehighLeak["Leakage LOW
VT can be high"] ``` ## 4.3 CMOS Inverter — Switching Energy Flow Detailed block-level flow showing how energy moves from supply to capacitor to heat, derived in Section 3.1. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Clock edge: Vin toggles"] nodeRise["Output rising 0 to VDD"] nodeSupplyE["Energy from VDD rail
CsubL times VsubDD squared
total CsubL VsubDD squared"] nodeStoredE["Energy stored in CL
one half CsubL VsubDD squared"] nodePmosHeat["Heat in PMOS during charge
one half CsubL VsubDD squared"] nodeFall["Output falling VDD to 0"] nodeCapDischarge["Stored energy one half CsubL VsubDD squared
dumped to GND via NMOS"] nodeNmosHeat["Heat in NMOS during discharge
one half CsubL VsubDD squared"] nodeStart --> nodeRise nodeRise --> nodeSupplyE nodeSupplyE --> nodeStoredE nodeSupplyE --> nodePmosHeat nodeStart --> nodeFall nodeFall --> nodeCapDischarge nodeCapDischarge --> nodeNmosHeat nodeStoredE -. recoverable once .-> nodeFall ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The capacitor recovers its stored energy on the *next* falling edge, but that recovery is converted to heat in the NMOS — energy flows one-way from supply → capacitor → ground (as heat). This is the fundamental reason $P_{dyn}$ scales with $V_{DD}^2$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define propagation delay and average propagation delay of a CMOS inverter. How are they related to the maximum clock frequency of a synchronous digital system?** **Model Answer:** Propagation delay of a CMOS inverter is the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input waveform** and the **50% transition point of the corresponding output waveform**. Two specific delays are defined: - **$t_{pHL}$** (high-to-low delay): time for output to fall from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ when input goes low. - **$t_{pLH}$** (low-to-high delay): time for output to rise from 0 to $V_{DD}/2$ when input goes high. **Average propagation delay:** $$ t_p = \frac{t_{pHL} + t_{pLH}}{2} $$ **Relation to maximum clock frequency:** In a synchronous system, the clock period $T_{clk}$ must be long enough for the signal to propagate through the longest combinational path (critical path) within one cycle. The maximum operating frequency is therefore: $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk,\,min}} \approx \frac{1}{2 \, t_p} $$ The factor of 2 accounts for register setup + hold + clock-to-Q delay. A *faster* gate (smaller $t_p$) directly enables a *higher* clock frequency, increasing system throughput. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List the three components of power dissipation in a CMOS circuit. Which component dominates in (a) an active high-performance processor, and (b) a deep-sleep IoT sensor node?** **Model Answer:** The three components of power dissipation in CMOS are: 1. **Dynamic (switching) power** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — energy dissipated in charging/discharging load capacitances. 2. **Short-circuit power** $P_{sc}$ — direct-path current when both PMOS and NMOS conduct simultaneously during input transitions. 3. **Static (leakage) power** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — current flowing even when the gate is logically idle. **Dominant component:** | Scenario | Dominant Component | Reason | |---|---|---| | (a) Active high-performance processor | **Dynamic power $P_{dyn}$** | High $V_{DD}$, high $f$, large $\alpha$ — switching losses dominate (~70–90% of total) | | (b) Deep-sleep IoT sensor node | **Static leakage power $P_{stat}$** | Clock is gated off ($f \approx 0$), so $P_{dyn} \to 0$, but subthreshold leakage persists as long as $V_{DD}$ is on | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim "static power = 0 in CMOS". This is **false at deep-submicron nodes** (≤ 90 nm). At 28 nm, leakage can exceed dynamic power in low-activity blocks. Always state the *node technology* in your answer. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 scheme mandates internal choice in every Part B question. **Both Option A and Option B** are fully solved below. A student appearing for the exam must attempt exactly **one** of them. --- ### Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **Q. (a)** Derive the expression for **dynamic power dissipation** in a CMOS inverter. Hence obtain the average dynamic power per cycle in terms of $\alpha$, $C_L$, $V_{DD}$ and $f$. State clearly the physical origin of each term. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A CMOS inverter in a 65 nm process has the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.35$ V, $k_n = 600 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, load capacitance $C_L = 30$ fF, switching activity $\alpha = 0.15$, clock frequency $f = 500$ MHz. Compute: (i) the average propagation delay $t_p$, (ii) the dynamic power $P_{dyn}$, (iii) the Power-Delay Product (PDP), and (iv) comment on what the PDP value tells you about this design. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Energy supplied during charging:** A charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ flows from $V_{DD}$ to charge $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$. Energy drawn from supply: $$ E_{sup} = V_{DD} \cdot Q = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Energy stored in capacitor:** $$ E_{cap} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \, C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Heat dissipated in PMOS during charging** (conservation of energy): $$ E_{PMOS} = E_{sup} - E_{cap} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Heat dissipated in NMOS during discharging:** The $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ stored in $C_L$ is **fully** dissipated through the NMOS (no energy recovery path). $$ E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 5 — Total energy per full cycle (0→1→0):** $$ E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Average dynamic power** with switching activity $\alpha$ and frequency $f$: $$ \boxed{P_{dyn} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f} $$ **Physical interpretation of each term:** - **$\alpha$** — probability that a transition (0→1 or 1→0) occurs in a given clock cycle. Reduces effective switching. - **$C_L$** — physical capacitance being charged (gate + interconnect + diffusion). - **$V_{DD}^2$** — quadratic dependence is the *single most exploited knob* in low-power VLSI. - **$f$** — higher frequency = more cycles per second = more switching events. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] Given: $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $V_{TN} = 0.35$ V, $k_n = 600 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2 = 600 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V², $C_L = 30$ fF $= 30 \times 10^{-15}$ F, $\alpha = 0.15$, $f = 500$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^8$ Hz. **(i) Average propagation delay $t_p$:** $$ t_p \approx \frac{C_L \, V_{DD}}{k_n (V_{DD} - V_{TN})^2} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-15})(1.0)}{(600 \times 10^{-6})(0.65)^2} $$ $$ t_p = \frac{30 \times 10^{-15}}{(600 \times 10^{-6})(0.4225)} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-15}}{253.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 118.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ s} $$ $$ \boxed{t_p \approx 118.3 \text{ ps}} $$ **(ii) Dynamic power $P_{dyn}$:** $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f = (0.15)(30 \times 10^{-15})(1.0)^2(5 \times 10^8) $$ $$ P_{dyn} = (0.15)(30)(5) \times 10^{-15+8} = 22.5 \times 10^{-6} = \boxed{22.5 \,\mu\text{W}} $$ **(iii) Power-Delay Product (PDP):** $$ PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p = (22.5 \times 10^{-6})(118.3 \times 10^{-12}) $$ $$ \boxed{PDP = 2.66 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 2.66 \text{ fJ}} $$ **(iv) Design comment on PDP:** The PDP of **2.66 fJ** represents the **energy consumed per switching event** by this gate. This is the figure-of-merit for energy efficiency: - *Lower PDP* → less energy wasted per operation → better for battery-powered systems (mobile, IoT). - *PDP is independent of clock frequency* in its pure form, allowing fair comparison of designs at different speeds. - For a 65 nm inverter, this PDP is reasonable; cutting it further requires reducing $C_L$ (smaller transistors), lowering $V_{DD}$ (sacrificing speed), or using adiabatic/clocked logic styles. --- ### Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q. (a)** Explain the **sources of leakage current** in a deep-submicron CMOS transistor. Derive the expression for subthreshold leakage current and explain its exponential dependence on threshold voltage. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A mobile SoC chip operates at $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V with an average leakage current of $I_{leak} = 5 \,\mu\text{A}$ per million gates. The chip has 500 million gates, switching activity $\alpha = 0.1$, average load capacitance per gate $C_L = 1$ fF, and clock frequency $f = 1.2$ GHz. Compute: (i) total static power, (ii) total dynamic power, (iii) total power, and (iv) the ratio of static to dynamic power. Comment on which design strategy (reducing $V_{DD}$ or reducing $I_{leak}$) is more effective. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **The three main sources of leakage in deep-submicron CMOS:** 1. **Subthreshold leakage ($I_{sub}$):** Weak-inversion current flowing between source and drain when $V_{GS} < V_T$. Dominant in modern nodes. 2. **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage ($I_{gate}$):** Quantum-mechanical tunneling of electrons through the thin (≤ 2 nm) gate oxide. Grows exponentially as oxide scales down. 3. **Reverse-biased junction leakage ($I_{jn}$):** Band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) and thermal generation at the source/drain-to-well p-n junctions. Important at high doping. **Derivation of subthreshold leakage current:** When $V_{GS} < V_T$, the channel is in **weak inversion**. The drain current is dominated by carrier diffusion, analogous to a bipolar transistor: $$ I_{sub} = I_0 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_T}{n V_{th}}}\left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_{th}}}\right) $$ where: - $I_0 = \mu \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \, V_{th}^2 \, e^{1.8} \, e^{-\frac{V_T}{n V_{th}}}$ — the off-current scale. - $V_{th} = kT/q \approx 26$ mV at room temperature — thermal voltage. - $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ — subthreshold swing ideality factor (≈ 1.3–1.5). - $V_T$ is the threshold voltage. For $V_{DS} \gg V_{th}$ (typical case, $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$), the bracketed term $\to 1$. **Exponential dependence on $V_T$:** Setting $V_{GS} = 0$ (transistor nominally off): $$ I_{off} = I_0 \, e^{-V_T/(n V_{th})} $$ Reducing $V_T$ by **100 mV** multiplies $I_{off}$ by: $$ e^{0.1/(1.4 \times 0.026)} = e^{2.75} \approx 15.6 $$ → **a 100 mV drop in $V_T$ increases leakage by ~15×**, but only speeds the gate up by ~10–20%. This is the *speed-leakage trade-off* at the heart of modern low-power design. > [!TIP] > **Body-bias trick:** Applying **reverse body bias (RBB)** raises $V_T$ and exponentially suppresses leakage — a key knob in dynamic voltage scaling (DVS) systems. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] Given: $V_{DD} = 0.9$ V, $I_{leak} = 5 \,\mu\text{A} / 10^6$ gates, $N = 500 \times 10^6$ gates, $\alpha = 0.1$, $C_L = 1$ fF $= 10^{-15}$ F, $f = 1.2$ GHz $= 1.2 \times 10^9$ Hz. **(i) Total static power:** $$ P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak,\,total} = (0.9)\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^6}\right)(500 \times 10^6) $$ $$ P_{stat} = (0.9)(5 \times 10^{-6})(500) = (0.9)(2.5) = \boxed{2.25 \text{ W}} $$ **(ii) Total dynamic power:** Total load capacitance = $N \cdot C_L = (500 \times 10^6)(10^{-15}) = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ F. $$ P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_{L,tot} \, V_{DD}^2 \, f = (0.1)(5 \times 10^{-4})(0.81)(1.2 \times 10^9) $$ $$ P_{dyn} = (0.1)(5)(0.81)(1.2) \times 10^{-4+9} = 0.486 \times 10^{5} = \boxed{48.6 \text{ W}} $$ **(iii) Total power** (ignoring $P_{sc}$): $$ P_{tot} = P_{stat} + P_{dyn} = 2.25 + 48.6 = \boxed{50.85 \text{ W}} $$ **(iv) Ratio and design recommendation:** $$ \frac{P_{stat}}{P_{dyn}} = \frac{2.25}{48.6} \approx 0.046 \;\; (\text{or } 4.6\%) $$ **Comment:** Even though leakage contributes only ~4.6% in active mode, the absolute 2.25 W is a *floor* — it is paid 24/7 whenever the chip is powered, including in standby. To lower total power: - **Reducing $V_{DD}$** from 0.9 V to 0.7 V cuts $P_{dyn}$ by $(0.7/0.9)^2 = 0.605$ → saves ~19 W. - **Reducing $I_{leak}$** by 2× (e.g., high-$V_T$ transistors, power gating) saves only 1.1 W. → **Reducing $V_{DD}$ is far more effective** for active mode, but **leakage reduction** dominates in sleep/standby mode. A mobile SoC must use **both** — DVFS for active, power-gating for sleep. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\alpha$ factor in $P_{dyn}$.** Many students write $P_{dyn} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ without the switching activity. *Always include $\alpha$*. > 2. **Confusing $t_p$ and $t_{pHL}$.** $t_p$ is the *average* of the two; $t_{pHL}$ is one specific transition. KTU questions frequently swap notation. > 3. **Stating $P_{stat} = 0$ for CMOS.** Strictly false for ≤ 90 nm nodes. *Always mention the technology node*. > 4. **Ignoring short-circuit power.** In a complete answer, $P_{tot}$ should include $P_{sc}$ — at least mention it. > 5. **Unit errors in PDP/EDP.** PDP is in Joules (energy), EDP is in J·s. Writing "Watts" for PDP costs 1 mark. > 6. **Missing the factor of 0.69 in RC delay.** The first-order RC delay is $0.69 R_{eq} C_L$, not $R_{eq} C_L$ — this is a common board trap. > 7. **Not stating assumptions.** Always state the *square-law model* and *saturation-region approximation* in derivations. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 5-minute revision before the KTU exam — bullet-by-bullet mental scan.** - [x] CMOS has **three** power components: $P_{dyn}$, $P_{sc}$, $P_{stat}$ — total is their sum. - [x] $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — **dominant** in active digital circuits. - [x] Energy per transition in CMOS = $C_L V_{DD}^2$ — half dissipated on charge, half on discharge. - [x] $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$ → reducing $V_{DD}$ is the **single most powerful** power-reduction knob. - [x] $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — dominant in sleep mode and at sub-90 nm nodes. - [x] Three leakage sources: **subthreshold, gate-oxide tunneling, junction leakage**. - [x] Subthreshold current: $I_{sub} \propto e^{-V_T / (n V_{th})}$ — exponential in $V_T$. - [x] Propagation delay: $t_p = (t_{pHL} + t_{pLH})/2 \approx 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$. - [x] Inverter delay: $t_p \approx C_L V_{DD} / [k_n(V_{DD} - V_T)^2]$. - [x] **Higher $V_{DD}$ → faster gate but more power.** Higher $V_T$ → slower but less leakage. - [x] **PDP** = energy per switching event (Joules); **EDP** = PDP × delay (J·s). - [x] $f_{max} \approx 1/(2 t_p)$ for pipelined synchronous systems. - [x] Clock gating reduces $\alpha$ → reduces $P_{dyn}$ without changing $V_{DD}$ or speed. - [x] Power gating cuts $V_{DD}$ to idle blocks → eliminates **both** $P_{dyn}$ and $P_{stat}$. - [x] DVFS (Dynamic Voltage-Frequency Scaling) trades speed for power in real time. - [x] Always specify the **technology node** in numericals (affects leakage regime). - [x] Always state **assumptions** in derivations (square-law, saturation region, etc.). - [x] Units: $t_p$ in seconds, $P$ in Watts, PDP in Joules, EDP in J·s. - [x] Mermaid-safe: every diagram above uses alphanumeric node IDs and plain-text labels.

topics.title: Signal Integrity Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal Integrity Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Signal Integrity (SI)** in VLSI Design refers to the ability of an electrical signal to maintain its required electrical characteristics (voltage level, timing, and waveform shape) as it propagates through the on-chip interconnect network from the driver to the receiver, without undergoing unacceptable degradation due to physical phenomena arising in deep submicron (DSM) technologies.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for sub-100nm CMOS technologies, signal integrity issues emerge primarily because:
- Interconnect **resistance (R)** scales unfavorably with shrinking dimensions
- **Capacitive (Cc)** and **inductive (Ll, Mm)** coupling between adjacent wires becomes dominant
- The ratio of wire cross-section to length becomes very large, making wires behave as **distributed RLC transmission lines** rather than lumped capacitors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** As per the PEICT526 syllabus (Module 3), signal integrity is no longer an afterthought but a primary design constraint, classified into three pillars: **(a) Noise (crosstalk), (b) Timing degradation (delay), and (c) Power/Ground integrity (bounce)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Water Flowing Through Garden Hoses**
>
> Imagine water flowing through three parallel garden hoses lying on the ground:
> - **Hose 1 (Aggressor line)**: Water gushes through it rapidly.
> - **Hose 2 (Victim line)**: A thin, quiet hose next to it.
> - When the water in Hose 1 suddenly stops or starts, you can **see** Hose 2 shudder or vibrate — this is **crosstalk**.
> - The friction inside the long hoses is like **wire resistance** (R) causing voltage drop.
> - The water hammer effect when a valve slams shut is like **inductance** causing overshoot/undershoot (ringing).
> - Rust and scaling inside old narrow pipes is like **electromigration** — atoms physically moving due to high current density.

This is exactly what happens in nanoscale VLSI interconnects. The wires are so thin, so close, and so long relative to the signal rise time, that the electrical "water" (current/voltage) leaks, couples, and bounces in ways that distort the digital waveform.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Dielectric constant of SiO₂**: $\kappa_{ox} \approx 3.9$
- **Dielectric constant of low-k materials**: $\kappa \approx 2.0 - 2.7$ (e.g., Black Diamond, SiOC)
- **Permittivity of free space**: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Permeability of free space**: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- **Speed of light in vacuum**: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
- **Critical current density for electromigration (Al)**: $J_{max} \approx 10^{5}$ A/cm²
- **Characteristic impedance of typical on-chip line**: $Z_0 \approx 50 - 75\ \Omega$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Threshold Rule (Examiner's favorite):** A wire behaves as a **transmission line** when its physical length $L$ exceeds $\frac{t_r}{6 \cdot v_p}$, where $t_r$ is the signal rise time and $v_p$ is the propagation velocity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Signal Integrity

### Pillar 1: Interconnect Parasitics (Why Wires Are Not Ideal)

At deep submicron nodes, the **interconnect delay** exceeds the **gate delay**. The wire parasitics are modeled using the distributed π-model or RC ladder.

- **Resistance per unit length**: $r = \frac{\rho}{A}$ where $\rho$ is resistivity, $A$ is cross-section.
- **Capacitance per unit length** has three components:
  - **Ground capacitance (Cgnd)**: Between wire and ground plane.
  - **Coupling capacitance (Cc)**: Between adjacent signal wires.
  - **Fringing capacitance**: Sidewall contribution.
- **Inductance per unit length** becomes significant when $t_r < 100$ ps, due to high frequency content.

### Pillar 2: Crosstalk (The "Talking Wires" Problem)

**Crosstalk** is the undesired coupling of energy from one wire (the **aggressor**) to a neighboring wire (the **victim**) through parasitic capacitance and mutual inductance.

- **Capacitive Crosstalk**: Caused by the electric field coupling via $C_c$.
- **Inductive Crosstalk**: Caused by the magnetic field coupling via mutual inductance $M_m$.

#### Miller Coupling Effect

For two adjacent CMOS wires, the effective coupling capacitance is multiplied by the **Miller effect**:
- When aggressor switches **opposite** to victim → Effective $C_c$ is doubled ($2C_c$).
- When aggressor switches in the **same direction** as victim → Effective $C_c$ is zero ($0$).

### Pillar 3: Power and Ground Integrity (Supply Noise)

- **IR Drop**: Voltage drop across the resistive power distribution network (PDN).
- **Ground Bounce (or Supply Bounce)**: When output buffers switch simultaneously (e.g., a 32-bit bus), large transient currents ($di/dt$) flowing through the parasitic inductance of the bond wire and package pin cause the local ground reference to shift. This is also called **simultaneous switching noise (SSN)**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-yield summary of all equations a student must memorize for KTU exams on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Wire resistance | $R = \frac{\rho L}{A} = R_{\square} \cdot \frac{L}{W}$ | $R_{\square}$ = sheet resistance ($\Omega/\square$) |
| 2 | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \kappa_{ox} W L}{t_{ox}}$ | For wide wires over ground plane |
| 3 | Coupling capacitance ratio | $\frac{C_c}{C_{total}} \to 1$ as $S \to 0$ | In DSM, $C_c$ dominates over $C_{gnd}$ |
| 4 | Elmore delay (RC line) | $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \sum_{i} R_i \cdot C_i$ | Used for delay estimation in RC trees |
| 5 | Distributed RC delay | $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{tot} \cdot C_{tot}$ for lumped; $0.38 r c L^2$ for distributed | Lumped if $r L \cdot c L \ll t_r$ |
| 6 | Crosstalk voltage (static) | $V_{crosstalk} = \frac{C_c}{C_{gnd} + 2C_c} \cdot V_{dd}$ | For grounded victim, aggressor swings $V_{dd}$ |
| 7 | Miller factor | $M = \frac{C_{eff}}{C_c}$ | $M=2$ for opposite switching, $M=0$ for same |
| 8 | Ground bounce | $V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$ | $L_{eff}$ = effective package + bond wire inductance |
| 9 | IR drop | $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{grid}$ | From power pad to logic gate |
| 10 | Transmission line delay | $t_{pd} = \frac{L \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}{c}$ | Propagation delay for matched line |
| 11 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L_l}{C_l}}$ | $L_l$ and $C_l$ are per unit length |
| 12 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | At load termination |
| 13 | Electromigration limit | $J = \frac{I}{w \cdot t} < J_{max}$ | $J_{max} \approx 10^5$ A/cm² for Al |
| 14 | Antenna ratio | $A_R = \frac{\text{Area of metal connected to gate}}{t_{ox} \cdot W_{gate}}$ | Must be $< 300$ to $500$ to avoid antenna damage |

> [!TIP]
> **Boundary conditions for KTU exam:**
> - **Distributed RC line**: Use when $rL \cdot cL \gg t_r$ (slow rise time / long wire).
> - **Transmission line**: Use when $L > \frac{t_r}{6 v_p}$ (fast rise time / very long wire).
> - **Lumped C**: Use only for very short local wires.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Chip Design Houses (Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, Apple):** Use tools like Cadence Voltus/Synopsys PrimeTime SI to analyze signal integrity before tape-out.
- **PCB Design:** Same principles extend to board-level routing — controlled impedance traces ($Z_0 = 50\Omega$ for USB, $100\Omega$ differential for Ethernet).
- **Automotive & Aerospace Electronics:** Signal integrity is critical for EMI/EMC compliance and functional safety (ISO 26262).
- **High-Speed SerDes:** Multi-GHz serial links (PCIe Gen5, USB4) require pre-emphasis, de-emphasis, and adaptive equalization — all responses to SI issues.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Elmore Delay for a Distributed RC Line

The **Elmore delay** is the most common delay metric asked in KTU exams. Let us derive it for a uniform RC ladder.

### Setup

Consider a uniform wire modeled as a lumped RC ladder with $n$ segments. Each segment has resistance $rL/n$ and capacitance $cL/n$. The total resistance and capacitance are $R_{tot} = rL$ and $C_{tot} = cL$.

**Step 1: Express the transfer function.**

For a single RC stage, the transfer function is:
$$
H_1(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sR_1C_1}
$$

For $n$ cascaded stages, the open-circuit time constant at node $i$ is $\tau_i = R_i \cdot \sum_{j \geq i} C_j$.

**Step 2: Apply Elmore's approximation.**

Elmore showed that the dominant-pole approximation gives the 50% propagation delay as:
$$
t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot \sum_{i} R_{i,i} \cdot C_{i,tot}
$$

where $R_{i,i}$ is the common resistance from input to node $i$, and $C_{i,tot}$ is the total capacitance downstream.

**Step 3: Compute the sum for a uniform line.**

For a uniform RC line (continuous model), we convert the sum to an integral:
$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \int_0^L R_{in}(x) \cdot c \, dx
$$

The resistance from the input (x=0) to a point at distance $x$ is:
$$
R_{in}(x) = r \cdot x
$$

The downstream capacitance is $c \cdot (L - x)$ only if we measure from the point $x$ to the output, but the total capacitance seen is the entire $cL$. The standard Elmore result for a distributed RC line is:
$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot \int_0^L x \, dx = 0.69 \cdot r \cdot c \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}
$$

**Step 4: Final closed-form expression.**
$$
t_{pd} = 0.38 \cdot r c L^2
$$

where the **0.38** arises from $0.69 \times 0.5$ (Elmore's 0.69 factor times the integration constant 0.5).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Notice the delay scales **quadratically** with length $L$ — this is why long global interconnects are the bottleneck, and why repeaters are inserted.

## 3.2 Derivation: Crosstalk Voltage for a Floating Victim Line

Consider two parallel wires of length $L$, separated by distance $S$ and surrounded by a ground plane at height $H$ (3-wire model). Let aggressor line $A$ switch with step voltage $V_{dd}$ while victim line $V$ is floating (high impedance at both ends).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the capacitance network.**

The victim line is coupled to the aggressor through $C_c$ and to ground through $C_{gnd}$. Since the victim is floating, the charge injected by $C_c$ is redistributed between $C_c$ and $C_{gnd}$.

**Step 2: Apply charge conservation.**

The total charge injected onto the victim node by the aggressor is:
$$
\Delta Q = C_c \cdot V_{dd}
$$

This charge is shared between the victim-to-ground capacitance $C_{gnd}$ and the back-coupling capacitance $C_c$ to the aggressor (which itself changed voltage).

**Step 3: Write the voltage equation.**

By voltage division (capacitive divider) between $C_c$ and $C_{gnd}$:
$$
V_{victim} = \frac{C_c}{C_{gnd} + 2C_c} \cdot V_{dd}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

- If $C_{gnd} \to 0$ (e.g., victim is surrounded by other active wires), $V_{victim} \to \frac{V_{dd}}{2}$, a **huge noise pulse**.
- This is the classic **floating-line crosstalk**, and it can flip the state of a memory cell.

> [!TIP]
> **For grounded victim (one end tied to known logic):** $V_{victim} = \frac{C_c}{C_c + C_{gnd}} \cdot V_{dd}$, which is smaller.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ground Bounce Voltage

When $N$ output drivers of a bus switch simultaneously, the total current drawn from the supply/ground pin is:
$$
I_{total} = N \cdot I_{driver}
$$

The rate of change of this current through the effective inductance $L_{eff}$ of the bond wire + package pin + ground rail produces an induced voltage:
$$
V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}
$$

**Step 1: Express $di/dt$ in terms of bus parameters.**

For a CMOS inverter, the output current during transition is approximately:
$$
I \approx C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{t_r}
$$

For $N$ parallel drivers switching in the same direction:
$$
\frac{di}{dt} = N \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{t_r^2}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute.**
$$
V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot N \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{t_r^2}
$$

**Step 3: Analysis.**
- $V_{gb}$ grows **quadratically** with shorter rise times $t_r$.
- $V_{gb}$ grows **linearly** with bus width $N$ and load $C_L$.
- **Mitigation**: Use multiple power/ground pins, on-chip decoupling capacitors, and controlled slew rate drivers.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Phenomenon | Primary Cause | Frequency Domain | Time Domain Symptom | Fix |
|------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|-----|
| **Capacitive Crosstalk** | $C_c$ between wires | High $dV/dt$ | Glitch on victim | Wire spacing, shielding |
| **Inductive Crosstalk** | Mutual $M_m$ | High $di/dt$ | Ringing on both lines | Ground shielding, return path |
| **Reflection** | $Z_L \neq Z_0$ | All freq. | Overshoot, undershoot | Termination resistor |
| **IR Drop** | $R_{grid}$ | DC | Voltage level shift | Wider power rails, more vias |
| **Ground Bounce** | $L_{eff}$, $di/dt$ | Switching transients | Logic error at receiver | Decoupling caps, more V/G pins |
| **Electromigration** | High $J$ | DC and AC | Open circuit over time | Wire widening, redundancy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Exams:** Students often confuse the Miller factor direction. Remember: **Opposite switching ⇒ +2× coupling; Same direction switching ⇒ 0× coupling (no glitch, but added delay).**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Signal Integrity Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the workflow a VLSI engineer follows when addressing signal integrity issues during physical design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Netlist and SDF] --> B[Extract RC and RLC Parasitics]
    B --> C1{Coupling Capacitance High}
    B --> C2{Wire Length Long}
    B --> C3{Multiple Drivers Switching}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Crosstalk Analysis]
    C1 -- No --> E[Nominal SI Pass]
    C2 -- Yes --> D2[Transmission Line Analysis]
    C2 -- No --> D2b[Lumped RC Analysis]
    C3 -- Yes --> D3[SSN Ground Bounce Analysis]
    C3 -- No --> D3b[Single Driver Analysis]
    D1 --> F[Static and Dynamic Noise Check]
    D2 --> G[Reflection and Ringing Check]
    D3 --> H[IR Drop and Decoupling Check]
    F --> I[Fix Spacing Shielding]
    G --> J[Add Termination Resistor]
    H --> K[Add Decoupling Caps and Vias]
    I --> L[Signoff SI Verification]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    D2b --> L
    D3b --> L
    E --> L
    L --> M[Tapeout Ready GDSII]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Crosstalk Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DriverA[Aggressor Driver] --> WireA[Aggressor Wire]
    DriverV[Victim Driver] --> WireV[Victim Wire]
    WireA ---|Cc Coupling Capacitance| WireV
    WireA ---|Mm Mutual Inductance| WireV
    WireV --> RecV[Victim Receiver]
    WireA --> RecA[Aggressor Receiver]
    WireA ---|Cgnd| Gnd1[Ground Plane]
    WireV ---|Cgnd| Gnd2[Ground Plane]
```

## 4.3 Power Distribution Network Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Pad1[VDD Pad] --> R1[Parasitic Resistance Rgrid]
    R1 --> Node1[Internal Node]
    Node1 --> L1[Package Inductance Lpkg]
    L1 --> Gate1[Logic Gate]
    Node1 --> Cap1[On-chip Decoupling Cap]
    Node1 --> Gate2[Logic Gate]
    Node2[GND Pad] --> R2[Parasitic Resistance Rgnd]
    R2 --> Node3[Ground Rail]
    Node3 --> L2[Ground Bond Inductance]
    L2 --> Gate1
    L2 --> Gate2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The inductance $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the principal sources of ground bounce. The decoupling capacitor $C_{ap1}$ acts as a local charge reservoir to suppress $di/dt$ transients.

## 4.4 Crosstalk Waveform Schematic (Text-Based)

```
   Aggressor (V_A):       ___
                         |   |
                       __|   |__
                      |         | 
   ───────────────────┘         └─────────────────

   Victim (V_V):              ___
                              |   |   ← Glitch
   ───────────────────────────┘   └─────────────────
                          ↑
                     Crosstalk Pulse
                  (height = Vdd * Cc / (Cgnd + 2Cc))
```

The victim line is **static LOW** but exhibits a **positive glitch** when the aggressor rises, and a **negative glitch** when the aggressor falls. If the glitch exceeds the noise margin, the receiver misinterprets the logic level.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on KTU University Examination pattern for PEICT526 (VLSI Design), Module 3. Marks and CO mapping follow the 2024 Scheme regulations.

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]
**Define signal integrity. List the four major signal integrity issues encountered in deep submicron VLSI design.**

**Model Answer:**

**Signal Integrity** is the measure of the quality of an electrical signal as it travels through the on-chip interconnect, ensuring that the signal reaches the receiver with sufficient voltage margin and timing accuracy.

The four major signal integrity issues are:
1. **Crosstalk** — Unwanted coupling between adjacent wires via parasitic capacitance and mutual inductance.
2. **Reflection and Ringing** — Caused by impedance mismatch in transmission line interconnects.
3. **Ground Bounce (SSN)** — Voltage fluctuation on the ground rail due to $L \cdot di/dt$.
4. **IR Drop** — Voltage drop in the power distribution network due to wire resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Word limit:** Stick to 4–5 lines. Examiners award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per issue listed (up to 2 marks for explanation).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Differentiate between lumped RC model and distributed RC model for on-chip interconnects. State the condition for using each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lumped RC Model | Distributed RC Model |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| Representation | Single R, single C | RC ladder or π-network |
| Applicable when | $rL \cdot cL \ll t_r$ | $rL \cdot cC \geq t_r$ |
| Delay | $0.69 R_{tot} C_{tot}$ | $0.38 rc L^2$ |
| Frequency | Low | Moderate to high |
| Typical use | Short local wires | Long semi-global and global wires |

**Condition for use:**
- Lumped model: when the interconnect's RC time constant is much less than the signal rise time.
- Distributed model: when wire length is significant and resistance of the wire cannot be ignored.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **crosstalk phenomenon** between two adjacent interconnect lines. Derive the expression for the crosstalk voltage on a floating victim line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **ground bounce** in CMOS VLSI circuits. Derive the expression for ground bounce voltage. Discuss two design techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Crosstalk

**Definition:** Crosstalk is the undesirable coupling of a signal from one wire (aggressor) to a neighboring wire (victim) through parasitic capacitance $C_c$ and mutual inductance $M_m$.

**Diagram:** (Two parallel lines over a ground plane, with $C_c$ between them and $C_{gnd}$ to ground. The aggressor is driven by a step input; the victim is left floating.)

**Derivation for floating victim line:**

Let aggressor line switch from $0$ to $V_{dd}$ with a step input, and victim line be floating (no DC path to ground).

Capacitance network:
- $C_c$: coupling capacitance between aggressor and victim.
- $C_{gnd}$: victim to ground.

Charge injected onto victim by aggressor:
$$
\Delta Q = C_c \cdot V_{dd}
$$

This charge redistributes between $C_{gnd}$ and the back-coupling path. By voltage division (capacitive divider):
$$
V_{noise} = V_{victim} = \frac{C_c}{C_{gnd} + 2 C_c} \cdot V_{dd}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $C_{gnd} \ll C_c$ (deep submicron, where spacing $S$ is very small), then $V_{noise} \to \frac{V_{dd}}{2}$.
- This large noise pulse can flip the state of a memory cell or cause a setup/hold violation.

**Miller Effect Summary:**
- Aggressor and victim switch in **opposite** directions → Effective coupling = $2C_c$.
- Aggressor and victim switch in **same** direction → Effective coupling = $0$ (no glitch, but added delay).

**Design Fixes:** Wire spacing, ground shielding, twisted routing, buffer insertion.

#### Part (b) — Ground Bounce

**Definition:** Ground bounce (or supply bounce) is the phenomenon where the on-chip ground potential temporarily shifts due to transient currents flowing through the parasitic inductance of the package pin and bond wire during simultaneous output switching.

**Derivation:**

For $N$ output drivers switching simultaneously, each driving a load $C_L$:
$$
\frac{di}{dt} = N \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{t_r^2}
$$

The induced ground bounce voltage is:
$$
V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = L_{eff} \cdot N \cdot C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}}{t_r^2}
$$

where $L_{eff}$ is the effective inductance of the bond wire, package pin, and internal ground rail.

**Design Techniques to Mitigate:**
1. **Multiple V/G pins and bonding wires:** Reduces effective $L_{eff}$ by parallel combination of inductances.
2. **On-chip decoupling capacitors:** Placed near the output drivers, they supply the instantaneous current and isolate the package inductance from the switching transients.
3. **Controlled slew rate drivers:** Increase $t_r$ to reduce $di/dt$.
4. **Separate power/ground pins for I/O and core** to isolate noisy I/O from sensitive core logic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to forget the factor of $N$ (number of switching drivers) in the ground bounce equation. Many students write $V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$ without specifying the $N \cdot C_L$ substitution. This costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **distributed RC model** of an on-chip interconnect. Derive the Elmore delay for a uniform distributed RC line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **electromigration**? Explain the mechanism and the conditions under which it occurs. Discuss two design rules to prevent electromigration failure. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Distributed RC Model and Elmore Delay

**Introduction:**
A long on-chip interconnect cannot be modeled as a single lumped R and C because the resistance of the wire itself is comparable to the driver's ON-resistance. The wire is therefore modeled as a chain of infinitesimal RC segments, giving a distributed RC line.

**Mathematical Setup:**
Let $r$ = resistance per unit length ($\Omega$/µm), $c$ = capacitance per unit length (fF/µm), and $L$ = total wire length. Then $R_{tot} = rL$ and $C_{tot} = cL$.

The wire is divided into $n$ identical segments, each with $R_i = rL/n$ and $C_i = cL/n$.

**Elmore Delay Derivation:**

For a single RC stage, the transfer function is:
$$
H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

The Elmore delay is the first moment of the impulse response (open-circuit time constant):
$$
t_d = \sum_i R_{ii} \cdot C_{i,tot}
$$

For a uniform line, the common resistance from input to a point at distance $x$ is $R_{in}(x) = rx$. The total downstream capacitance is $cL$. Converting the sum to an integral:
$$
t_d = \int_0^L r x \cdot c \, dx = rc \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}
$$

Applying the 50% delay factor (0.69) and the symmetry correction:
$$
t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot \frac{rcL^2}{2} = 0.38 \cdot r c L^2
$$

**Key Conclusion:** The delay scales **quadratically** with length $L$, motivating the use of repeaters and better interconnect materials (copper, low-k).

#### Part (b) — Electromigration

**Definition:**
Electromigration is the physical movement of metal atoms in an interconnect due to momentum transfer from conducting electrons, leading to voids (open circuits) or hillocks (short circuits) over time.

**Mechanism:**
- High current density ($J > 10^5$ A/cm² for aluminum) causes electron wind.
- Electrons transfer momentum to metal ions, pushing them in the direction of electron flow.
- Atoms accumulate at the cathode end (hillocks) and deplete at the anode end (voids).
- Failure occurs as gradual open or short, accelerated by elevated temperature.

**Conditions for Occurrence:**
1. High current density $J = I / (w \cdot t)$.
2. Elevated temperature.
3. AC vs DC — DC electromigration is worse (no recovery).
4. Grain boundaries and material defects.

**Design Rules to Prevent Failure:**
1. **Wire Widening:** Ensure $J = I / (w \cdot t) < J_{max}$ by choosing $w$ large enough. Power rails are typically much wider than signal lines.
2. **Material Selection:** Replace aluminum with copper, which has higher electromigration resistance ($J_{max} \approx 10^6$ A/cm²).
3. **Redundant Vias:** Use multiple vias between metal layers to share current and reduce via failure.
4. **Current Density Rule (Bayer's Rule):** Enforce $J < 1$ mA/µm width in layout design rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often describe electromigration as "wire melting." It is **not melting** — it is a gradual atomic transport phenomenon. Examiners deduct marks for this conceptual error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Signal Integrity** = ability of signal to propagate without distortion (noise, timing, power).
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Noise (crosstalk), Timing (delay), Power (IR drop, ground bounce).
- ✅ **Crosstalk formula (floating victim):** $V_{noise} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_{gnd} + 2C_c} \cdot V_{dd}$.
- ✅ **Miller Factor:** $M = 2$ (opposite switching), $M = 0$ (same direction).
- ✅ **Elmore delay (distributed RC line):** $t_{pd} = 0.38 \cdot r c L^2$.
- ✅ **Lumped RC delay:** $t_{pd} = 0.69 \cdot R_{tot} \cdot C_{tot}$.
- ✅ **Ground bounce:** $V_{gb} = L_{eff} \cdot N \cdot C_L \cdot \dfrac{V_{dd}}{t_r^2}$.
- ✅ **IR drop:** $V_{drop} = I \cdot R_{grid}$.
- ✅ **Reflection coefficient:** $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$.
- ✅ **Transmission line condition:** $L > \dfrac{t_r}{6 v_p}$.
- ✅ **Electromigration limit:** $J_{max} \approx 10^5$ A/cm² (Al), $10^6$ A/cm² (Cu).
- ✅ **Antenna effect:** Metal area connected to gate must satisfy $A_R < 300-500$.
- ✅ **Dielectric constants:** $\kappa_{SiO_2} = 3.9$; low-k materials $\kappa = 2.0-2.7$.
- ✅ **Design fixes for SI:** Wire spacing, shielding, repeaters, decoupling caps, low-k dielectrics, copper interconnects.
- ✅ **Quadratic scaling of delay with length** is the *single most important* fact in interconnect design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When a question asks "explain signal integrity issues," always categorize your answer into the three pillars (Crosstalk, Delay, Power Integrity) and back up each with the relevant formula. This is the structure KTU examiners look for in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequential Logic Circuits: Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sequential Logic Circuits: Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for VLSI Design (PEICT526), Module 4, **Timing Metrics for Sequential Circuits** is formally defined as the set of quantitative parameters that characterize the temporal behavior of clocked (synchronous) sequential CMOS circuits, ensuring correct data propagation, capture, and stability across combinational logic blocks and sequential storage elements (flip-flops and registers) under specified operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEICT526, Module 4):**
> Timing metrics define the *contract* between combinational logic and sequential elements. They guarantee that every flip-flop reliably captures the correct logical value at every active clock edge. Violation of any timing metric results in **metastability**, functional failure, or reduced yield in fabricated VLSI chips.

The principal timing metrics covered in this module are:

- **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** — the maximum time from an input change to a stable output change.
- **Contamination Delay ($t_{cd}$)** — the minimum time from an input change to the *first* output change.
- **Clock-to-Q Propagation Delay ($t_{pcq}$)** — the maximum delay from the active clock edge to a stable flip-flop output.
- **Clock-to-Q Contamination Delay ($t_{ccq}$)** — the minimum delay from the active clock edge to the *first* flip-flop output change.
- **Setup Time ($t_{setup}$)** — the minimum time that data must be stable *before* the active clock edge.
- **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$)** — the minimum time that data must remain stable *after* the active clock edge.
- **Clock Period ($T_c$)** — the interval between two consecutive active clock edges.
- **Clock Skew ($t_{skew}$)** — the spatial difference in clock arrival times between two sequentially adjacent flip-flops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bottling plant conveyor belt** with two robotic arms controlled by a master clock:

- The **first arm** (flip-flop $FF_1$) places a bottle on the belt at the *tick* of the clock.
- The **conveyor belt** (combinational logic) carries the bottle to a filling station, which takes some *variable time* (logic delay).
- The **second arm** (flip-flop $FF_2$) must grab the bottle *exactly* at the next tick of the clock.

For the second arm to succeed, the bottle must arrive:
- **Not too early** (the arm would try to grab empty space) → governed by the **hold time**.
- **Not too late** (the arm would grab the *next* bottle) → governed by the **setup time**.
- **At a known, bounded speed** (the conveyor must finish) → governed by the **propagation delay** and the **clock period**.

Just as the plant manager (the VLSI designer) sets the conveyor speed (clock frequency) so that the slowest bottle clears the belt before the next grab, the VLSI engineer must size the clock period such that the **worst-case combinational delay plus setup time** fits inside one clock cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> Timing metrics are *worst-case bounds*. The clock period is chosen using the **longest** (maximum) delays, while hold-time violations are checked using the **shortest** (minimum) delays. This asymmetry is the heart of synchronous timing analysis.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The standard metrics, all measured in **seconds** (typically **nanoseconds, ns**, or **picoseconds, ps** in modern VLSI), are summarized as:

| Metric | Symbol | Nature | Typical Range (28 nm CMOS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propagation delay | $t_{pd}$ | Maximum (worst-case) | 50 – 500 ps |
| Contamination delay | $t_{cd}$ | Minimum (best-case) | 20 – 200 ps |
| Clock-to-Q (prop) | $t_{pcq}$ | Maximum | 30 – 100 ps |
| Clock-to-Q (cont) | $t_{ccq}$ | Minimum | 10 – 50 ps |
| Setup time | $t_{setup}$ | Required minimum | 20 – 80 ps |
| Hold time | $t_{hold}$ | Required minimum | 10 – 40 ps |
| Clock skew | $t_{skew}$ | Spatial difference | 0 – 100 ps |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Sequential Timing Model

A canonical sequential circuit consists of two registers ($FF_1$ and $FF_2$) separated by a block of **combinational logic**. The clock signal `CLK` is distributed to both registers. We assume a **rising-edge-triggered**, **positive-edge** D flip-flop for all derivations.

The data flow per clock cycle is:

1. At the **rising edge** of clock cycle $n$, $FF_1$ launches a new value $D_1$ onto its output $Q_1$.
2. This new value propagates through the combinational logic block, taking some delay $t_{pd,\text{logic}}$ (worst case) and $t_{cd,\text{logic}}$ (best case).
3. The signal arrives at the $D$ input of $FF_2$ as $D_2$.
4. At the **rising edge** of clock cycle $n+1$, $FF_2$ must capture $D_2$ correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two different delays (max and min)?**
> In a real CMOS gate, the output does not switch instantaneously. The first transition (contamination) may occur quickly because of fast pull-down or pull-up paths, but the signal must then settle to its final value (propagation). For **setup checks**, we use the maximum (slowest) delays. For **hold checks**, we use the minimum (fastest) delays.

### 2.2 The Two Fundamental Timing Constraints

#### 2.2.1 Setup Time Constraint

The data launched by $FF_1$ at clock edge $n$ must arrive at $FF_2$ **early enough** to satisfy the setup time requirement before clock edge $n+1$. This produces the **Setup Time Constraint**:

$$t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} \le T_c$$

Where:
- $t_{pcq}$ = clock-to-Q propagation delay of $FF_1$ (max).
- $t_{pd,\text{logic}}$ = worst-case combinational propagation delay.
- $t_{setup}$ = setup time requirement of $FF_2$ (max).
- $T_c$ = clock period.

Solving for the **minimum allowable clock period**:

$$T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}$$

#### 2.2.2 Hold Time Constraint

The data launched by $FF_1$ at clock edge $n$ must **not arrive too quickly** at $FF_2$, otherwise it could overwrite the data from the *previous* cycle. This produces the **Hold Time Constraint**:

$$t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold}$$

Where:
- $t_{ccq}$ = clock-to-Q contamination delay of $FF_1$ (min).
- $t_{cd,\text{logic}}$ = minimum combinational contamination delay.
- $t_{hold}$ = hold time requirement of $FF_2$ (min).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation (KTU Examiner's Favorite):**
> The **hold time constraint does NOT depend on the clock period**. It is a function only of the minimum contamination delays. Hold time violations are caused by *too-fast* combinational logic or *excessive* clock skew, and they cannot be fixed by slowing down the clock.

### 2.3 Effect of Clock Skew

In real chips, the clock signal arrives at $FF_1$ and $FF_2$ at slightly different times due to wire delay, buffer delay, and load mismatch. The **clock skew** is defined as:

$$t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$$

Where $t_{c1}$ and $t_{c2}$ are the absolute clock arrival times at $FF_1$ and $FF_2$ respectively. With skew, the constraints become:

**Setup Time Constraint (with skew):**
$$T_c \ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$

**Hold Time Constraint (with skew):**
$$t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold} + t_{skew}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Skew Sign Convention (KTU 2024):**
> If $t_{skew} > 0$ (clock at $FF_2$ arrives *later* than at $FF_1$), the setup constraint becomes *harder* (needs more time), but the hold constraint becomes *easier* (more time to violate). If $t_{skew} < 0$, the opposite occurs. Students frequently lose marks by mis-assigning the sign of $t_{skew}$.

### 2.4 The Concept of Slack

**Slack** is the *margin* by which a timing constraint is satisfied. Positive slack means the design is safe; negative slack means a violation.

- **Setup Slack:**
$$Slack_{setup} = T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew})$$

- **Hold Slack:**
$$Slack_{hold} = (t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}) - (t_{hold} + t_{skew})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Static Timing Analysis (STA) tools such as **Synopsys PrimeTime** or **Cadence Tempus** compute these slacks for every timing path in a billion-transistor chip. The minimum slack across the chip determines the **achievable clock frequency** and is the most important number in the entire physical-design flow.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}$ | Minimum clock period (no skew) | All delays in **ns** or **ps** |
| 2 | $T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$ | Minimum clock period (with skew) | $t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$ |
| 3 | $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold}$ | Hold time constraint (no skew) | All values are **min** |
| 4 | $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold} + t_{skew}$ | Hold time constraint (with skew) | Skew makes it stricter if $t_{skew} < 0$ |
| 5 | $Slack_{setup} = T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew})$ | Setup margin | Must be $\ge 0$ |
| 6 | $Slack_{hold} = (t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}) - (t_{hold} + t_{skew})$ | Hold margin | Must be $\ge 0$ |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{c,\min}}$ | Maximum operating frequency | In **Hz** |
| 8 | $t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$ | Definition of clock skew | Spatial difference in clock arrival |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Rule of Thumb for KTU Boards:**
> *For setup: use MAX delays. For hold: use MIN delays. The clock period is independent of hold-time checks.*

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern System-on-Chip (SoC) design, timing metrics drive the **synthesis, place-and-route, and clock-tree synthesis (CTS)** stages:

- **Synthesis** uses setup-time constraints to choose gate sizes and buffer insertions that meet $T_c$.
- **CTS** balances the clock tree to minimize $t_{skew}$ so that neither setup nor hold is violated.
- **Hold-fixing** is performed by adding *delay buffers* on the data path — the only technique available because hold violations are independent of clock frequency.
- **Sign-off STA** reports the **Worst Negative Slack (WNS)** and **Total Negative Slack (TNS)** for the entire chip.

These metrics are universally applied in industry-standard tools and form the foundation of **Static Timing Analysis (STA)**, which is the de-facto verification methodology for all digital ICs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Setup Time Constraint

We consider two adjacent flip-flops $FF_1$ and $FF_2$ separated by combinational logic. The clock period is $T_c$. Let $t=0$ be the rising edge of the launching clock at $FF_1$.

**Step 1: Time at which $FF_1$ output is valid (latest).**
The output $Q_1$ becomes valid (worst case) at time:

$$t_{Q_1,\text{valid}} = 0 + t_{pcq} = t_{pcq}$$

This is because the clock-to-Q propagation delay is the maximum time from the active clock edge to a stable $Q$ output.

**Step 2: Time at which $D_2$ input becomes valid (latest).**
The signal must propagate through the combinational logic. The latest it can become valid is:

$$t_{D_2,\text{valid}} = t_{Q_1,\text{valid}} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}}$$

**Step 3: Time at which $D_2$ must be stable for $FF_2$.**
$FF_2$ samples its $D$ input at the next rising clock edge, which occurs at time $T_c$. By definition of setup time, the data must be stable at least $t_{setup}$ *before* this edge. Therefore, the latest *required* arrival time of $D_2$ is:

$$t_{D_2,\text{required}} = T_c - t_{setup}$$

**Step 4: Equating the latest arrival with the latest requirement.**
For correct operation, the data must arrive no later than the required time:

$$t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} \le T_c - t_{setup}$$

**Step 5: Rearranging for the minimum clock period.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &\ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} \\
\therefore \quad T_{c,\min} &= t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental setup-time constraint** for synchronous sequential circuits.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hold Time Constraint

The hold time constraint ensures that the data launched by $FF_1$ does not arrive at $FF_2$ *too early* and corrupt the data that $FF_2$ is supposed to capture in the *current* cycle.

**Step 1: Earliest time at which $Q_1$ changes.**
The first transition at $Q_1$ occurs at the minimum clock-to-Q contamination delay:

$$t_{Q_1,\text{first}} = 0 + t_{ccq} = t_{ccq}$$

**Step 2: Earliest time at which $D_2$ changes.**
The contamination delay of the combinational logic determines the earliest possible change at $D_2$:

$$t_{D_2,\text{first}} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}$$

**Step 3: Earliest *forbidden* change at $D_2$.**
The $D$ input of $FF_2$ must remain stable for at least $t_{hold}$ *after* the active clock edge of $FF_2$ (which also occurs at $t=0$ since both FFs share the ideal clock). Therefore, the earliest *allowed* change is at time $t_{hold}$:

$$t_{D_2,\text{first}} \ge t_{hold}$$

**Step 4: Combining the inequalities.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} &\ge t_{hold}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **fundamental hold-time constraint**. Notice that the clock period $T_c$ has disappeared from the equation — confirming that hold time is independent of clock frequency.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Setup and Hold Constraints with Clock Skew

Now assume the clock at $FF_2$ arrives $\Delta t = t_{skew}$ *later* than the clock at $FF_1$ (positive skew in our convention).

**Setup constraint with skew:**

The launching edge at $FF_1$ still occurs at $t=0$, but the capturing edge at $FF_2$ now occurs at $t = T_c + t_{skew}$. The data must still arrive $t_{setup}$ before this *shifted* edge. Thus:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} &\le (T_c + t_{skew}) - t_{setup} \\
T_c &\ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} - t_{skew} \\
\therefore \quad T_{c,\min} &= t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}
\end{aligned}$$

**Hold constraint with skew:**

The $D_2$ signal must remain stable until time $t_{hold}$ *after* the capturing clock edge at $FF_2$, which now occurs at $t = t_{skew}$. The earliest change at $D_2$ is still $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}$, so:

$$t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold} + t_{skew}$$

For *positive* skew, the hold constraint is *easier* (more time available). For *negative* skew (clock at $FF_2$ arrives *earlier*), the hold constraint becomes *harder* and can fail.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A sequential circuit has the following parameters: $t_{pcq} = 100$ ps, $t_{ccq} = 60$ ps, $t_{pd,\text{logic}} = 350$ ps, $t_{cd,\text{logic}} = 120$ ps, $t_{setup} = 80$ ps, $t_{hold} = 50$ ps, and $t_{skew} = 30$ ps. Compute (a) the minimum clock period, (b) the maximum clock frequency, and (c) verify the hold-time constraint.

**Part (a): Minimum Clock Period**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{c,\min} &= t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew} \\
&= 100 + 350 + 80 + 30 \\
&= 560 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b): Maximum Clock Frequency**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{c,\min}} = \frac{1}{560 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 1.7857 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \\
&\approx 1.79 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c): Hold Time Verification**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHS} &= t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} = 60 + 120 = 180 \text{ ps} \\
\text{RHS} &= t_{hold} + t_{skew} = 50 + 30 = 80 \text{ ps} \\
\text{Slack}_{hold} &= 180 - 80 = 100 \text{ ps} \ge 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Since LHS $\ge$ RHS, the hold-time constraint is satisfied with 100 ps of positive slack.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Scenario Comparison

| Scenario | $t_{pcq}$ (ps) | $t_{pd,\text{logic}}$ (ps) | $t_{setup}$ (ps) | $t_{skew}$ (ps) | $T_{c,\min}$ (ps) | $f_{max}$ (GHz) | Hold OK? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1: Baseline (no skew) | 100 | 350 | 80 | 0 | 530 | 1.89 | $60+120=180 \ge 50$ ✓ |
| 2: Positive skew | 100 | 350 | 80 | +30 | 560 | 1.79 | $180 \ge 80$ ✓ |
| 3: Negative skew | 100 | 350 | 80 | −30 | 500 | 2.00 | $180 \ge 20$ ✓ |
| 4: Aggressive logic | 100 | 200 | 80 | 0 | 380 | 2.63 | $60+90=150 \ge 50$ ✓ |
| 5: Hold violation | 60 | 30 | 50 | −100 | 240 | 4.17 | $90 \ge -50$ ✓ (still OK) |
| 6: Hold fails | 60 | 30 | 80 | −100 | 230 | 4.35 | $90 \ge -20$ ✓ (still OK) |
| 7: Severe hold fail | 30 | 20 | 80 | −50 | 230 | 4.35 | $50 \ge 30$ ✓ |
| 8: Critical hold | 20 | 10 | 100 | −30 | 200 | 5.00 | $30 \ge 70$ ✗ FAIL |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Synchronous Sequential Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLK_DIST["Clock Distribution Network"]
        CLK_SRC[/"Global CLK\nSource"/]
    end

    subgraph FF1_BANK["Launching Flip-Flop FF1"]
        D1[/"D1 input"/]
        FF1_BOX["D-FF\nStorage Cell"]
        Q1[/"Q1 output"/]
    end

    subgraph COMBO["Combinational Logic Cloud"]
        INV1["Inverter / Gate 1"]
        NAND1["NAND Gate"]
        NOR1["NOR Gate"]
        BUF1["Buffer"]
    end

    subgraph FF2_BANK["Capturing Flip-Flop FF2"]
        D2[/"D2 input"/]
        FF2_BOX["D-FF\nStorage Cell"]
        Q2[/"Q2 output"/]
    end

    D1 --> FF1_BOX --> Q1
    Q1 --> INV1 --> NAND1 --> NOR1 --> BUF1 --> D2
    D2 --> FF2_BOX --> Q2

    CLK_SRC -- "t_c1 arrival" --> FF1_BOX
    CLK_SRC -- "t_c2 arrival\n(positive skew)" --> FF2_BOX

    classDef launch fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef combo fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef capture fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef clock fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

    class D1,FF1_BOX,Q1 launch
    class INV1,NAND1,NOR1,BUF1 combo
    class D2,FF2_BOX,Q2 capture
    class CLK_SRC clock
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The clock source fans out to both flip-flops but with different arrival times ($t_{c1}$ and $t_{c2}$). The combinational cloud between them contains arbitrary gates; its *worst-case* delay $t_{pd,\text{logic}}$ determines the minimum clock period, while its *minimum* delay $t_{cd,\text{logic}}$ determines whether the hold-time constraint is satisfied.

### 4.2 Timing Waveform Diagram — Setup and Hold Windows

```mermaid
gantt
    title Sequential Circuit Timing — Setup and Hold Windows
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Clock CLK
    RisingEdge_FF1 :a1, 0, 0
    FallingEdge   :a2, 500, 0
    RisingEdge_FF2 :a3, 1000, 0

    section FF1 Output Q1
    Stable_Pre     :b1, 0, 100
    Transition     :b2, 100, 0
    Stable_New     :b3, 100, 900

    section D2 Input Signal
    Hold_Window_FF2 :c1, 1000, 80
    Setup_Window_FF2 :c2, 920, 80
    Stable_Window   :c3, 280, 640

    section Status
    Capture_OK :milestone, 1000, 0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the Gantt Chart:**
> - The **Hold Window** begins at the capturing clock edge (1000 ps) and extends *backward* in time by $t_{hold} = 80$ ps. The data $D_2$ must NOT change during this window.
> - The **Setup Window** ends at the capturing clock edge and extends *backward* by $t_{setup} = 80$ ps. The data MUST be stable during this window.
> - The **Stable Window** of $D_2$ (from 280 ps to 920 ps) is the time during which the signal is guaranteed not to change.

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Hold-Time Violation Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Identify Hold-Time Slack\n(Slack_hold = t_ccq + t_cd_logic - t_hold - t_skew)"]

    CHECK{"Is Slack_hold\n>= 0?"}

    OK_PATH["Timing Path is SAFE\nNo fix required"]
    FIX_PATH["Hold-Time VIOLATION detected\nSlack_hold < 0"]

    FIX_DECISION["Choose a Fix Strategy"]
    OPT_A["Add delay buffers\non data path\n(increases t_cd_logic)"]
    OPT_B["Re-balance clock tree\n(reduces negative skew)"]
    OPT_C["Insert lockup latch\n(only between clock domains)"]
    OPT_D["Use slower drive-strength\ncells on data path"]

    RE_VERIFY["Re-run Static Timing\nAnalysis (STA)"]
    DONE["Sign-off: Timing Closure\nAchieved"]

    START --> CHECK
    CHECK -- Yes --> OK_PATH
    CHECK -- No --> FIX_PATH
    FIX_PATH --> FIX_DECISION
    FIX_DECISION --> OPT_A
    FIX_DECISION --> OPT_B
    FIX_DECISION --> OPT_C
    FIX_DECISION --> OPT_D
    OPT_A --> RE_VERIFY
    OPT_B --> RE_VERIFY
    OPT_C --> RE_VERIFY
    OPT_D --> RE_VERIFY
    RE_VERIFY --> CHECK

    classDef safe fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef action fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    class OK_PATH safe
    class FIX_PATH,CHECK fail
    class FIX_DECISION,OPT_A,OPT_B,OPT_C,OPT_D action
    class START,RE_VERIFY,DONE result
```

### 4.4 Setup-Time vs Hold-Time Constraint Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SETUP_DOMAIN["Setup-Time Domain (uses MAX delays)"]
        S1["t_pcq (max)"]
        S2["t_pd_logic (max)"]
        S3["t_setup (max)"]
        S4["t_skew (sign-aware)"]
        S_OUT["Constraint:\nT_c >= S1+S2+S3+S4"]
    end

    subgraph HOLD_DOMAIN["Hold-Time Domain (uses MIN delays)"]
        H1["t_ccq (min)"]
        H2["t_cd_logic (min)"]
        H3["t_hold (min)"]
        H4["t_skew (sign-aware)"]
        H_OUT["Constraint:\nH1+H2 >= H3+H4"]
    end

    S1 --> S_OUT
    S2 --> S_OUT
    S3 --> S_OUT
    S4 --> S_OUT

    H1 --> H_OUT
    H2 --> H_OUT
    H3 --> H_OUT
    H4 --> H_OUT

    SETUP_DOMAIN -. "Independent of\nclock period" .-> HOLD_DOMAIN

    classDef sDomain fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef hDomain fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000

```
