## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following timing metrics for a sequential circuit: (i) Setup time, (ii) Hold time, (iii) Clock-to-Q propagation delay.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Setup Time ($t_{setup}$):**
Setup time is the **minimum time interval** before the active clock edge during which the data input $D$ of a flip-flop must remain stable. If the data changes within this window, the flip-flop may enter a metastable state, resulting in an unpredictable output.

**(ii) Hold Time ($t_{hold}$):**
Hold time is the **minimum time interval after the active clock edge** during which the data input $D$ must remain stable. A change in $D$ within this window can also cause metastability or incorrect capture.

**(iii) Clock-to-Q Propagation Delay ($t_{pcq}$):**
Clock-to-Q propagation delay is the **maximum time** from the active edge of the clock to the instant when the flip-flop output $Q$ reaches a stable, valid logic level. It is one of the critical parameters in determining the minimum clock period.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between propagation delay and contamination delay of a combinational logic gate. Why are both needed for timing analysis?*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$) | Contamination Delay ($t_{cd}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Maximum time from an input change to the *stable final* output | Minimum time from an input change to the *first* output change |
| **Use in timing** | Used for **setup-time analysis** (worst case) | Used for **hold-time analysis** (best case) |
| **Bound** | Upper bound on delay | Lower bound on delay |

**Why both are needed:**
Timing analysis must verify that the circuit works correctly under **all PVT (Process, Voltage, Temperature) conditions**. A circuit must not fail when the logic is **slower** (setup check uses $t_{pd}$) and must not fail when the logic is **faster** (hold check uses $t_{cd}$). Using only one would either over-constrain the clock period or miss hold-time violations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**A(a). [7 Marks]** *Derive the setup-time constraint and the hold-time constraint for a sequential circuit consisting of two edge-triggered D flip-flops separated by combinational logic. Clearly define all variables used.*

**Model Solution:**

Consider two positive-edge-triggered D flip-flops $FF_1$ and $FF_2$ separated by a combinational logic block. The clock signal `CLK` is ideal and arrives simultaneously at both flip-flops.

**Setup-Time Constraint Derivation:**

Let $t = 0$ be the rising clock edge at $FF_1$ (launching edge).

**Step 1:** The output of $FF_1$ becomes valid (worst case) at:
$$t_{Q_1,\text{valid}} = t_{pcq}$$

**Step 2:** The signal propagates through the combinational logic with worst-case delay $t_{pd,\text{logic}}$, arriving at $D_2$ at:
$$t_{D_2,\text{valid}} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}}$$

**Step 3:** The capturing clock edge at $FF_2$ occurs at $t = T_c$. By the setup-time definition, the data must be stable at least $t_{setup}$ before this edge:
$$t_{D_2,\text{required}} = T_c - t_{setup}$$

**Step 4:** For correct operation, the actual arrival must be no later than the required arrival:
$$t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} \le T_c - t_{setup}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging:
$$T_c \ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}$$

Therefore, the minimum clock period is:
$$T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}$$

**Hold-Time Constraint Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The first transition at $Q_1$ occurs at:
$$t_{Q_1,\text{first}} = t_{ccq}$$

**Step 2:** The earliest change at $D_2$ is:
$$t_{D_2,\text{first}} = t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}$$

**Step 3:** The data must remain stable until at least $t_{hold}$ after the active clock edge of $FF_2$ (which also occurs at $t=0$ for ideal clock):
$$t_{D_2,\text{first}} \ge t_{hold}$$

**Step 4:** Combining:
$$t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold}$$

> This is the **fundamental hold-time constraint**. Note that the clock period $T_c$ does not appear — hold time is independent of clock frequency.

---

**A(b). [7 Marks]** *A sequential circuit has $t_{pcq} = 80$ ps, $t_{ccq} = 40$ ps, $t_{pd,\text{logic}} = 450$ ps, $t_{cd,\text{logic}} = 100$ ps, $t_{setup} = 60$ ps, $t_{hold} = 50$ ps, and clock skew $t_{skew} = 25$ ps. Calculate (i) the minimum clock period, (ii) the maximum operating frequency, and (iii) the hold-time slack. Comment on whether the circuit is functional.*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Minimum Clock Period**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{c,\min} &= t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew} \\
&= 80 + 450 + 60 + 25 \\
&= 615 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (ii): Maximum Operating Frequency**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{max} &= \frac{1}{T_{c,\min}} = \frac{1}{615 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 1.626 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \\
&\approx 1.626 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (iii): Hold-Time Slack**

$$\begin{aligned}
Slack_{hold} &= (t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}) - (t_{hold} + t_{skew}) \\
&= (40 + 100) - (50 + 25) \\
&= 140 - 75 \\
&= 65 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment:** Since $Slack_{hold} = 65$ ps $> 0$, the hold-time constraint is satisfied. The circuit is **functional** at the calculated maximum frequency of approximately 1.626 GHz. The setup constraint is also satisfied as long as the clock period is at least 615 ps.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (Alternative Choice)

**B(a). [7 Marks]** *Explain the concept of clock skew in a sequential circuit. How does positive clock skew affect (i) the setup-time constraint and (ii) the hold-time constraint? Derive the modified constraints.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Clock Skew:**
Clock skew is the **difference in clock arrival times** at two sequentially adjacent flip-flops, caused by unequal wire lengths, buffer delays, or load mismatches in the clock distribution network. Mathematically:

$$t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$$

where $t_{c1}$ and $t_{c2}$ are the clock arrival times at $FF_1$ and $FF_2$, respectively.

**Positive clock skew** means the clock at $FF_2$ arrives *later* than at $FF_1$ ($t_{c2} > t_{c1}$, so $t_{skew} > 0$).

**Effect on Setup-Time Constraint:**

With positive skew, the capturing clock edge at $FF_2$ is delayed by $t_{skew}$. The setup constraint becomes:

$$T_c \ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$

**Derivation:** The data launched at $t=0$ at $FF_1$ arrives at $D_2$ at time $t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}}$. The capturing edge at $FF_2$ now occurs at $t = T_c + t_{skew}$. The data must be stable $t_{setup}$ before this edge:

$$t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} \le (T_c + t_{skew}) - t_{setup}$$

Rearranging:
$$T_c \ge t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$$

Thus, **positive skew tightens the setup constraint** — a longer clock period is required.

**Effect on Hold-Time Constraint:**

The data launched at $FF_1$ may arrive at $D_2$ as early as $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}$. However, the $D$ input of $FF_2$ is now required to be stable until $t_{hold}$ *after* the (delayed) capturing edge at $t_{skew}$:

$$t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold} + t_{skew}$$

Thus, **positive skew relaxes the hold constraint** — more time is available before the data must settle.

**Summary:**
- Positive skew → setup **harder**, hold **easier**.
- Negative skew → setup **easier**, hold **harder**.

---

**B(b). [7 Marks]** *What is timing slack? Compute the setup slack and hold slack for the following path: $t_{pcq} = 90$ ps, $t_{ccq} = 50$ ps, $t_{pd,\text{logic}} = 380$ ps, $t_{cd,\text{logic}} = 110$ ps, $t_{setup} = 70$ ps, $t_{hold} = 40$ ps, $t_{skew} = 20$ ps, target clock period $T_c = 600$ ps. Will this path meet timing?*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Timing Slack:**
Timing slack is the **margin** by which a timing constraint is satisfied. It is the difference between the *required* time and the *actual* arrival time (or vice versa, depending on the convention).

- **Positive slack** → constraint is met with margin.
- **Zero slack** → constraint is exactly met.
- **Negative slack** → constraint is **violated**; the path fails timing.

**Computation of Setup Slack:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Slack_{setup} &= T_c - (t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}) \\
&= 600 - (90 + 380 + 70 + 20) \\
&= 600 - 560 \\
&= 40 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Computation of Hold Slack:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Slack_{hold} &= (t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}}) - (t_{hold} + t_{skew}) \\
&= (50 + 110) - (40 + 20) \\
&= 160 - 60 \\
&= 100 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Timing Conclusion:**

- $Slack_{setup} = +40$ ps $\ge 0$ ✓ (Setup constraint met with 40 ps margin)
- $Slack_{hold} = +100$ ps $\ge 0$ ✓ (Hold constraint met with 100 ps margin)

**The path meets timing.** The designer could even reduce the clock period by 40 ps to achieve a higher frequency, at which point the setup slack would become zero.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing MAX vs MIN delays:** A common error is using $t_{pcq}$ (max) for the hold check. Always use $t_{ccq}$ (min) for hold, and $t_{pcq}$ (max) for setup.
> 2. **Skew sign convention:** Students often mis-assign the sign of $t_{skew}$. The KTU 2024 convention is $t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$. Always draw a small clock-arrival timing diagram to verify the sign.
> 3. **Including $T_c$ in the hold constraint:** The hold constraint does **not** depend on the clock period. If you see $T_c$ in your hold-time equation, you have made an error.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** The KTU board deducts marks for missing units (ps, ns, GHz) and incorrect unit conversions ($1$ ns $= 10^3$ ps).
> 5. **Incomplete definitions:** When asked to "define" setup/hold time, students often miss the "minimum" qualifier. Always specify it is the **minimum** time interval.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam (Module 4 — Timing Metrics):**

- **Setup Time ($t_{setup}$):** Minimum time data must be stable *before* the active clock edge → uses **MAX** delays.
- **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$):** Minimum time data must be stable *after* the active clock edge → uses **MIN** delays; **independent of $T_c$**.
- **Clock-to-Q ($t_{pcq}$ / $t_{ccq}$):** Delay from clock edge to flip-flop output; $t_{pcq}$ is max, $t_{ccq}$ is min.
- **Propagation vs Contamination Delay ($t_{pd}$ / $t_{cd}$):** Max vs min delays of combinational logic.
- **Minimum Clock Period (no skew):** $T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup}$.
- **Minimum Clock Period (with skew):** $T_{c,\min} = t_{pcq} + t_{pd,\text{logic}} + t_{setup} + t_{skew}$.
- **Hold Constraint (no skew):** $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold}$.
- **Hold Constraint (with skew):** $t_{ccq} + t_{cd,\text{logic}} \ge t_{hold} + t_{skew}$.
- **Maximum Frequency:** $f_{max} = 1 / T_{c,\min}$.
- **Clock Skew:** $t_{skew} = t_{c2} - t_{c1}$. Positive skew hurts setup, helps hold. Negative skew helps setup, hurts hold.
- **Setup Slack:** $T_c - (\text{setup data path delay} + t_{skew})$; must be $\ge 0$.
- **Hold Slack:** $(\text{hold data path delay}) - (t_{hold} + t_{skew})$; must be $\ge 0$.
- **Metastability:** Violation of setup or hold may push the latch into metastability — an unstable equilibrium that resolves randomly.
- **STA (Static Timing Analysis):** Industry-standard exhaustive verification methodology for timing closure in VLSI design.
- **Hold-time Fixes:** Add delay buffers on data path, re-balance clock tree, or insert lockup latches (only between clock domains).
- **Setup-time Fixes:** Reduce combinational logic depth, use faster cells, pipeline the design, or reduce clock skew.
- **Universal Rule:** For setup → use MAX delays; for hold → use MIN delays. *This is the single most important rule in timing analysis.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Latch (KTU Definition):** A *static* sequential element is one in which the stored data is preserved **as long as the supply voltage (V_DD) is maintained**, without any need for periodic refreshing. The state is held by a **regenerative (positive) feedback loop** formed by cross-coupled logic gates (typically inverters or NAND/NOR gates), rather than by charge stored on a parasitic capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static vs Dynamic — KTU Board Keyword**
> *Dynamic* nodes store data on the **gate capacitance of a transistor**, which leaks away in milliseconds and therefore must be **refreshed** periodically (e.g., dynamic RAM, dynamic logic). *Static* nodes store data in a **bi-stable feedback loop**, which is stable in either logic state indefinitely as long as V_DD is ON.

A **register** is a bank of *n* latches/flip-flops sharing a common clock, used to hold an n-bit word. A **flip-flop** is an edge-triggered bi-stable element (samples data only on a clock transition), while a **latch** is level-sensitive (transparent while the clock is active).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball in a Bowl" Picture

Imagine two bowls glued back-to-back so that a ball placed in one bowl automatically rolls into the other. The ball is a bit of information — once it lands in the *left* bowl, gravity (the feedback) keeps it there forever. To write new data, you must lift the ball out of one bowl and drop it into the other. The two bowls form the *cross-coupled inverters*; the act of lifting and dropping the ball is the *clocked write operation*; the moment the ball settles and stays put represents the **static** nature of storage (no periodic refresh needed — gravity never "leaks").

> [!TIP]
> **Why "static" matters in real ICs:** Static storage is **radiation-hard** and **noise-tolerant** because the regenerative loop actively restores the signal. This is why mission-critical aerospace ICs and SRAM cells use static storage rather than dynamic charge storage.

## 1.3 The Three KTU Mandatory Metrics for Any Latch

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Setup time | $t_{su}$ | Time data must be stable **before** active clock edge |
| Hold time | $t_h$ | Time data must remain stable **after** active clock edge |
| Clock-to-Q delay | $t_{CQ}$ | Delay from active clock edge to valid Q output |

These three numbers always appear in KTU exam problems. A KTU answer that *omits* one of them is considered **incomplete**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bi-Stable Element — Foundation of Every Static Latch

The fundamental static storage element is the **cross-coupled CMOS inverter pair**:

$$V_{out1} = \overline{V_{out2}}, \quad V_{out2} = \overline{V_{out1}}$$

This system has **exactly two stable states** (Q=0, Q̄=1) and (Q=1, Q̄=0). The loop gain around the two inverters must be greater than unity in magnitude for the state to be *regenerative*:

$$\vert G_{loop} \vert = \vert g_{m1} \cdot g_{m2} \cdot r_{on1} \cdot r_{on2} \vert > 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Regenerative" Property:** Once the latch is forced slightly off the metastable midpoint (V_out = V_DD/2), the positive feedback amplifies the small deviation exponentially until the output slams to a valid rail. This is what makes the latch immune to noise and removes the need for refresh.

## 2.2 SR Latch (NOR-based) — Step-by-Step Operation

The Set-Reset (SR) latch is built by cross-coupling two **NOR gates** (positive-level inputs):

| S | R | Q (next) | Q̄ (next) | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Q (no change) | Q̄ (no change) | **Hold** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **Reset** |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **Set** |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Forbidden** (Q = Q̄ = 0 violates complementary outputs) |

The **NAND-based SR latch** is identical in operation except that the inputs are *active-LOW* (denoted $\bar{S}, \bar{R}$), and the *forbidden* state becomes 0,0 instead of 1,1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Question:** *"Why is S=R=1 forbidden in a NOR-based SR latch?"* — Because both outputs are forced to 0, the complementary relationship (Q = Q̄) is broken. When both inputs return to 0 simultaneously, the latch enters a *race condition* and the final state is unpredictable.

## 2.3 Clocked SR Latch — Gating the Inputs

To make the latch synchronous, a 2-input AND gate is added in front of each NOR input:

$$S_{clk} = S \cdot CLK, \quad R_{clk} = R \cdot CLK$$

The latch is now only **sensitive to S and R when CLK = 1**. This is still a *level-sensitive* element — the *forbidden* S=R=1 condition still exists.

## 2.4 D Latch (Data Latch / Transparent Latch) — The Workhorse

The D latch eliminates the forbidden state by ensuring S and R are **always complementary**. A single inverter drives R from D:

$$S = D, \quad R = \overline{D}$$

This guarantees $S \neq R$ always, so the forbidden state is structurally impossible. Operation:

| CLK | D | Q (next) | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Transparent (latch follows D) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Transparent (latch follows D) |
| 0 | X | Q (held) | **Hold / Opacity** |

**Two CMOS implementations are KTU-mandatory:**

**(a) Transmission-Gate (TG) D Latch** — uses a tri-state inverter controlled by CLK and $\overline{CLK}$:
- CLK=1: input tri-state buffer is ON, feedback inverter is OFF → output = D
- CLK=0: input buffer is OFF, feedback inverter forms a keeper → output holds

**(b) C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) D Latch** — uses two transmission gates in series with the input:

The C²MOS latch is **insensitive to clock overlap** when cascaded (as in master-slave flip-flops), which is why it is the preferred KTU-recommended implementation.

## 2.5 Master-Slave D Flip-Flop — Edge Triggered

Two D latches in series, with **complementary clocks**:

| Stage | Clock | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Master | CLK = 1 | Transparent, samples D |
| Master | CLK = 0 | Holds sampled value |
| Slave | CLK = 0 | Transparent, copies master to Q |
| Slave | CLK = 1 | Holds, isolates output |

**On the rising edge of CLK**, the master goes opaque and the slave goes transparent — the value D that was sampled just before the edge is propagated to Q. Hence, the circuit behaves as a **positive-edge-triggered D flip-flop**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Regenerative loop gain | $\vert G_{loop} \vert = \vert g_{mN} g_{mP} r_{onN} r_{onP} \vert > 1$ | Required for bi-stable storage |
| Regenerative time constant | $\tau_{reg} = \frac{C_L}{g_{mN} + g_{mP}}$ | Determines how fast the latch resolves from metastability |
| Setup time (C²MOS) | $t_{su} \approx 0.35 \cdot \tau_{in}$ | $\tau_{in}$ = input time constant |
| Hold time (C²MOS) | $t_h \approx 0$ (ideal) | A C²MOS latch has near-zero hold time |
| Clock-to-Q delay | $t_{CQ} \approx t_{d,inv} + t_{d,TG}$ | Sum of internal stage delays |
| Minimum clock period | $T_{clk} \geq t_{CQ} + t_{su} + t_{logic}$ | For a pipeline stage |
| TSPC throughput | $f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{CQ,master} + t_{CQ,slave}}$ | True Single Phase Clocking rate |
| Power (static) | $P_{static} \approx 0$ | No DC path in static CMOS latch |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Static latches and flip-flops form the timing backbone of every synchronous digital IC — microprocessors (e.g., ARM Cortex pipeline registers), GPUs, network ASICs, and FPGAs (Xilinx 7-series, Altera Cyclone). The static nature guarantees **data retention during clock gating** for power saving.

## 2.7 TSPC (True Single Phase Clock) Latch — KTU Favourite

The TSPC latch uses **only one clock wire** (no $\overline{CLK}$ needed), saving routing area:

- Uses a **dynamic precharge** node on the falling clock edge
- Becomes **static** when the input data is held, because the feedback keeper pulls the internal node to a valid rail
- Operates in three phases: **precharge → evaluate → hold**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim TSPC is "dynamic." It is **static** during the hold phase because the cross-coupled feedback keeps the state. The precharge node is dynamic only briefly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Regenerative Time Constant of a Bi-Stable

A bi-stable latch can be modelled as two inverters with a small-signal gain $A_v$ around the loop. Starting from KCL at the output node, with load capacitance $C_L$:

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = g_m \cdot V_{in} - \frac{V_{out}}{r_{on}}$$

Substituting $V_{in} = -A_v \cdot V_{out}$ (small-signal inversion through the feedback inverter):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} &= -g_m A_v V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}}{r_{on}} \\
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} &= -\left(\frac{1 + g_m A_v r_{on}}{r_{on} C_L}\right) V_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the differential equation gives an **exponential growth** of any deviation from the metastable point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out}(t) &= V_{out}(0) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{(g_m A_v r_{on} - 1)}{r_{on} C_L} \, t\right) \\
&= V_{out}(0) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{t}{\tau_{reg}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **regenerative time constant** is:

$$\boxed{\tau_{reg} = \frac{r_{on} \, C_L}{g_m A_v r_{on} - 1} \approx \frac{C_L}{g_m}}$$

(for $A_v \gg 1$, the unity-gain condition is met easily and $\tau_{reg} \approx C_L / g_m$).

**Interpretation:** A larger transconductance $g_m$ makes the latch resolve faster from metastability. Designers size the latch transistors to make $\tau_{reg}$ small — but at the cost of higher clock load and more dynamic power.

## 3.2 Derivation: Setup Time of a CMOS D Latch (KTU Numerical Pattern)

Consider a D latch with input driven by a minimum-sized inverter of output resistance $R_{drv}$ into the latch input capacitance $C_{in}$:

$$\tau_{in} = R_{drv} \cdot C_{in}$$

The setup time is conventionally defined as the time required for the input to reach **90% of its final value** so that the latch reliably recognises it as a valid logic level:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in}(t_{su}) &= 0.9 \cdot V_{DD} \\
V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t_{su}/\tau_{in}}\right) &= 0.9 \, V_{DD} \\
1 - e^{-t_{su}/\tau_{in}} &= 0.9 \\
e^{-t_{su}/\tau_{in}} &= 0.1 \\
-\frac{t_{su}}{\tau_{in}} &= \ln(0.1) = -2.302 \\
\boxed{t_{su}} &\approx 2.3 \, \tau_{in}
\end{aligned}
$$

Some KTU textbooks use the **70% rule** (for 70% V_DD recognition threshold), giving $t_{su} \approx 1.2 \, \tau_{in}$. Always **read the question's threshold** before substituting.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Static Latch Families

| Latch Type | Transistors | Clock Signals | Edge Triggered? | Race-Free? | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-coupled inverter (basic) | 4 | 0 | No | N/A | SRAM cell only |
| NOR SR latch | 4 | 0 | No | Needs care | Memory cell |
| NAND SR latch | 4 | 0 | No | Needs care | Asynchronous set/reset |
| Transmission-Gate D latch | ~10 | 2 (CLK, $\overline{CLK}$) | No (level) | Yes | Low-power storage |
| C²MOS D latch | ~12 | 2 | No (level) | Yes | Pipelined datapaths |
| Master-Slave D-FF | 2 × C²MOS | 2 | **Yes (edge)** | Yes | Registers, FSMs |
| TSPC D-FF | ~12 | **1** | Yes | Yes | High-density arrays |
| Pulsed latch | 8 | 1 + pulse gen | Soft-edge | Yes | High-performance CPUs |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** *"Compare C²MOS and TSPC latches with respect to clock load, transistor count, and race immunity."* — Use the above matrix as the spine of your answer.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Q:** A CMOS D latch has an input driver with $R_{drv} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and the latch input capacitance is $C_{in} = 50\,\text{fF}$. Calculate the setup time assuming a 90% V_DD threshold.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{in} &= R_{drv} \cdot C_{in} = 1\times 10^3 \times 50\times 10^{-15} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{s} = 5 \, \text{ps} \\
t_{su} &= 2.3 \, \tau_{in} = 2.3 \times 5 \, \text{ps} \\
&= 11.5 \, \text{ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $t_{su} \approx 11.5 \, \text{ps}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bi-Stable Cross-Coupled Inverters (SRAM Cell)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1[Inverter 1] -- Q output --> node2[Inverter 2]
    node2 -- Qbar output --> node1
    node3[Word Line WL] -- Word Line Control --> node4[Access Transistors N1, N2]
    node4 -- connect bitlines --> node1
    node4 -- connect bitlines --> node2
    node5[Bit Line BL] --- node4
    node6[Bit Line BLbar] --- node4
```

> **Figure caption:** Cross-coupled CMOS inverters form the storage core. Two NMOS access transistors gated by the Word Line connect the cell to the differential bit lines BL and $\overline{BL}$ during read/write.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Master-Slave D Flip-Flop (C²MOS)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[D input] --> B[Master Latch C2MOS]
    CLK[CLK] --> B
    B --> C[Inversion Stage for Slave]
    C --> D[Slave Latch C2MOS]
    CLK -->|CLK bar derived| D
    D --> E[Q output]
    B -. Holds when CLK low .-> C
    D -. Holds when CLK high .-> E
```

**Operation summary embedded in diagram:**

| Step | CLK | Master | Slave | Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| t1 | 0 → 1 (rising) | Opaque (latched) | Transparent | New value appears |
| t2 | 1 → 0 (falling) | Transparent (tracking D) | Opaque (held) | No change |
| t3 | 0 → 1 (rising) | Opaque | Transparent | Q updates only on **rising edge** |

## 4.3 Block Diagram — TSPC Positive-Edge D Flip-Flop

```mermaid
graph TD
    stage1[Stage 1 Precharge PMOS] -->|CLK controls precharge| nodeX
    nodeX[Dynamic Node X] --> stage2[Stage 2 NMOS Evaluate]
    stage2 --> nodeY
    nodeY[Internal Hold Node] --> stage3[Stage 3 Output Driver]
    stage3 --> Qout[Q Output]
    Dinput[D input] --> stage2
    CLK2[CLK only, no bar] --> stage1
    CLK2 --> stage3
```

> **Figure caption:** Only a single CLK line reaches the cell. Stage 1 precharges node X on CLK=0; Stage 2 evaluates D on CLK=1; Stage 3 holds and drives the output. The feedback keeper in Stage 3 makes the storage *static* during the hold phase.

## 4.4 Architecture Flow — Synchronous Pipeline Using Static Registers

```mermaid
graph LR
    PC[PC register] --> IM[Instruction Memory]
    IM --> REG[Register File]
    REG --> ALU[ALU / Combinational Logic]
    ALU --> R1[Pipeline Register Stage 1]
    R1 --> CL2[Next Stage Logic]
    CL2 --> R2[Pipeline Register Stage 2]
    R2 --> CL3[Next Stage Logic]
    CL3 --> WR[Writeback Register]
    CLK3[Global Static Clock] --- PC
    CLK3 --- R1
    CLK3 --- R2
    CLK3 --- WR
```

> **Figure caption:** Every storage element between combinational logic blocks is a **static master-slave flip-flop** clocked by a single global clock. The maximum clock frequency is set by $1 / (t_{CQ} + t_{logic,max} + t_{su})$ for the slowest pipeline stage.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a static latch. How does it differ from a dynamic latch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *static* latch stores data in a regenerative feedback loop formed by cross-coupled inverters or NAND/NOR gates, and retains the data indefinitely as long as V_DD is on, without refresh (1 mark). A *dynamic* latch stores data as charge on a node capacitance (2 marks), which leaks via sub-threshold and junction leakage and must be refreshed every few milliseconds. Static latches are preferred for low-frequency, low-power, and radiation-tolerant applications; dynamic latches offer higher density and are used in high-performance pipelines where refresh is automatic (3 marks).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List the three timing parameters of a flip-flop and state the unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Setup time ($t_{su}$)** — time data must be stable before the active clock edge; measured in **seconds (ps or ns)** (1 mark).
2. **Hold time ($t_h$)** — time data must remain stable after the active clock edge; measured in **seconds** (1 mark).
3. **Clock-to-Q delay ($t_{CQ}$)** — propagation delay from active clock edge to Q output; measured in **seconds** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### `Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **C²MOS positive-level-sensitive D latch**. Show the two states (transparent and hold) and identify the transistors that are ON in each state.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **regenerative time constant** of a bi-stable element. Explain why a larger $g_m$ improves the noise immunity of the latch.

#### Model Solution

**(a) C²MOS D Latch — Operation [7 marks]**

A C²MOS D latch consists of two transmission-gate (TG) switches and two inverters arranged so that one path is always closed. Let $C$ = CLK and $\bar{C} = \overline{CLK}$.

**Components (2 marks):**
- TG1: series NMOS+PMOS controlled by (C, $\bar{C}$) — input switch
- TG2: series NMOS+PMOS controlled by ($\bar{C}$, C) — feedback switch
- Inv1: input buffer
- Inv2: output buffer (and feedback inverter pair)

**When CLK = 1 (Transparent) [2.5 marks]:**
- TG1 is ON, TG2 is OFF
- Input D passes through Inv1 and TG1 to internal node X
- Inv2 produces Q = D
- The latch is **transparent** — any change on D appears at Q

**When CLK = 0 (Hold) [2.5 marks]:**
- TG1 is OFF, TG2 is ON
- The internal node X is isolated from D
- The feedback inverter pair (formed by TG2 + a small keeper inverter) forms a **static regenerative loop**
- Q holds its last value, immune to D transitions

**(b) Regenerative Time Constant Derivation [7 marks]**

Start with the small-signal model of the two cross-coupled inverters. The total capacitance at the output node is $C_L$, the transconductance of the driver is $g_m$, and the output resistance of the load is $r_{on}$.

KCL at the output node:

$$C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + \frac{V_{out}}{r_{on}} = -g_m V_{in}$$

Using the feedback relation $V_{in} = -A_v V_{out}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} &= g_m A_v V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}}{r_{on}} \\
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} &= \left(\frac{g_m A_v r_{on} - 1}{r_{on} C_L}\right) V_{out} \\
V_{out}(t) &= V_{out}(0) \exp\left(\frac{t}{\tau_{reg}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

with the regenerative time constant [2 marks]:

$$\tau_{reg} = \frac{r_{on} C_L}{g_m A_v r_{on} - 1} \approx \frac{C_L}{g_m}$$

for large $A_v$.

**Why larger $g_m$ improves noise immunity [2 marks]:** A larger $g_m$ makes $\tau_{reg}$ smaller, meaning any perturbation from the metastable midpoint grows faster. The latch therefore "snaps" to a valid rail before noise can disturb it, giving a higher Noise Margin.

---

### `Question B — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Design and explain a **master-slave D flip-flop** using two C²MOS latches. Draw the block diagram, the timing waveforms, and explain why the circuit behaves as a **positive-edge-triggered** element.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the **C²MOS, TSPC, and Pulsed** latch families in terms of transistor count, clock load, race immunity, and typical application. Use a comparative table.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Master-Slave D Flip-Flop [7 marks]**

**Block diagram [2 marks]:** D → Master C²MOS Latch (clocked by CLK) → Slave C²MOS Latch (clocked by $\overline{CLK}$) → Q

**Timing waveforms [3 marks]:**

| Time | CLK | Master state | Slave state | Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| t < t_rise | 0 | Transparent, tracking D | Opaque, holding Q | Q_old |
| t = t_rise | ↑ (rising) | Opaque (latches last D) | Transparent (passes master → Q) | Q_new (= D just before edge) |
| t > t_rise | 1 | Opaque, holding | Transparent (Q tracks master) | Q = D_held |
| t = t_fall | ↓ (falling) | Transparent, tracks D | Opaque, holds Q_new | Q = Q_new (frozen) |

**Edge-triggered behaviour [2 marks]:** Because the master and slave clocks are *complementary*, only one latch is transparent at any time. The data D is captured by the master just before the **rising edge of CLK**, and propagated to Q exactly at the rising edge — giving positive-edge-triggered behaviour. The output Q is isolated from D during the entire high phase of CLK, preventing race-through.

**(b) Comparison Table [7 marks]**

| Feature | C²MOS | TSPC | Pulsed Latch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistors per D-FF | ~24 | ~12 | ~10 + pulse gen |
| Clock signals needed | 2 (CLK, $\overline{CLK}$) | **1** (single phase) | 1 + short pulse |
| Race immunity | Excellent | Excellent | Good (depends on pulse width) |
| Hold time | Near zero | Near zero | Small, pulse-width dependent |
| Setup time | Moderate | Moderate | **Shortest** (small $t_{su}$) |
| Clock load | High (both phases) | Low (one phase) | Moderate |
| Application | General pipelines | High-density register files | High-performance CPU latches |
| Power | Moderate | Low (less clock routing) | Lowest per latch |

**Conclusion [1 mark]:** Choose C²MOS for robust general-purpose design, TSPC for area- and clock-power-limited designs, and pulsed latches for ultra-high-speed timing paths where short setup time is critical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. *Do not* say the master-slave flip-flop is "negative-edge triggered" when the master uses CLK and the slave uses $\overline{CLK}$ — that gives a **positive**-edge-triggered FF. (Common -2 mark error.)
> 2. *Always* mention the **forbidden state** in any SR-latch answer; KTU examiners explicitly look for it.
> 3. When asked for setup time, *do not* forget to state the **threshold convention** (70% vs 90% V_DD) before substituting into the formula.
> 4. In TSPC answers, do *not* confuse "dynamic precharge" with "dynamic storage" — the *storage* is static, only the *precharge node* is dynamic.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Static Latches and Registers**

- ☐ **Static = regenerative feedback**, no refresh needed; **Dynamic = charge on capacitance**, needs refresh.
- ☐ Bi-stable has **two stable states**; loop gain must satisfy $\vert G_{loop}\vert > 1$.
- ☐ **NOR SR latch:** S=R=1 is **forbidden**; S=R=0 is **hold**.
- ☐ **NAND SR latch:** inputs are active-LOW; $\bar{S}=\bar{R}=0$ is forbidden; 1,1 is hold.
- ☐ **D latch** = SR latch with inverter on R input → forbidden state **eliminated**.
- ☐ **Transmission-Gate D latch** uses a tri-state buffer + feedback inverter.
- ☐ **C²MOS D latch** uses two TGs in series; **insensitive to clock overlap** when cascaded.
- ☐ **Master-slave FF** = two C²MOS latches with **complementary clocks** → edge-triggered.
- ☐ **TSPC latch** = single clock line; dynamic precharge but **static hold**.
- ☐ **Three timing parameters** to always quote: $t_{su}$, $t_h$, $t_{CQ}$.
- ☐ **Regenerative time constant:** $\tau_{reg} \approx C_L / g_m$.
- ☐ **Setup time formula (90% V_DD):** $t_{su} \approx 2.3 \, R_{drv} C_{in}$.
- ☐ **C²MOS ≈ 24 transistors** per D-FF; **TSPC ≈ 12**; **Pulsed ≈ 10** plus pulse generator.
- ☐ **Pulsed latch** has the shortest setup time — used in high-speed CPU timing paths.
- ☐ Static latches consume **near-zero static power**; dynamic power dominates.
- ☐ KTU examiner tip: always **draw the schematic** AND the **waveform** for 14-mark answers.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Static Latches and Registers (PEICT526, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dynamic Latches and Registers

### Formal Definition
A **Dynamic Latch** is a sequential storage element that stores a logic level on the **parasitic gate capacitance** of a MOS transistor rather than using a closed-loop feedback path (as in static latches). A **Dynamic Register** (or dynamic flip-flop) is built by cascading two such dynamic latches in a master–slave configuration, achieving edge-triggered behaviour without continuous static current. The information is valid only for a limited time (typically a few clock cycles) and must be refreshed or used before the leakage discharges the storage node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The three dynamic structures you MUST know for Module 4 are:
> 1. **C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) Dynamic Latch/Register**
> 2. **TSPC (True Single-Phase Clocked) Dynamic Register**
> 3. **NORA / Dynamic CMOS Pipelines** (mentioned in passing)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket"
Imagine a **leaky water bucket** placed on a table:
- You pour water (data) into the bucket through a tap controlled by a clock (transistor switches).
- The bucket holds the water (charge) on its inner surface (parasitic capacitance).
- Because the bucket is **leaky** (sub-threshold leakage), water slowly drips out. If you don't pour fresh water periodically (refresh or evaluate quickly), the bucket becomes empty and the data is lost.

This perfectly models a dynamic node — data is stored **temporarily on capacitance** and must be processed before leakage destroys it.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Clock Frequency ($f_{clk}$):** Typically **100 MHz – 5 GHz** in modern VLSI.
- **Hold Time ($t_{hold}$):** The minimum time the storage capacitance must retain the charge, governed by **leakage current $I_{leak}$**.
- **Storage Capacitance ($C_L$):** Mainly the **gate oxide capacitance** of the next stage + junction capacitances, order of **fF** (femto-Farads).
- **Retention Time:** Given by the well-known relation $\tau = \dfrac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{leak}}$.

---

## 1.2 C²MOS (Clocked CMOS) Dynamic Latch

### Definition
The **C²MOS latch** is the most common dynamic positive-level-sensitive latch. It uses **two NMOS and two PMOS transistors** in a cross-coupled inverter-style configuration, gated by a single clock phase ($\phi$ or $\overline{\phi}$). It achieves a high degree of robustness against clock overlap and clock skew.

### Intuition — The "Swing Bridge"
A C²MOS latch is like a **swing bridge over a canal**:
- When the bridge is **up** ($\phi$ = high, $\overline{\phi}$ = low), the road (input D) connects to the storage node (Q). Cars (data) drive in.
- When the bridge is **down** ($\phi$ = low, $\overline{\phi}$ = high), the road is **disconnected** and the storage node is **isolated**, holding the last car.
- Two bridges working in opposition (master and slave) create a **two-way traffic system** (master–slave flip-flop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Property:** The C²MOS latch is **insensitive to clock overlap** as long as the rise/fall times are properly controlled, making it a textbook standard in KTU board questions.

---

## 1.3 TSPC (True Single-Phase Clocked) Dynamic Register

### Definition
The **TSPC register** is a 9-transistor (or 10-transistor) edge-triggered dynamic flip-flop that operates on a **single clock phase** (no complementary $\overline{\phi}$ required). It cascades a **negative-level-sensitive dynamic front stage** with a **positive-level-sensitive dynamic back stage**, achieving edge-triggered master–slave behaviour with the lowest clock-load among all dynamic structures.

### Intuition — "One-Way Escalator"
Think of a TSPC register as a **one-way escalator in a shopping mall**:
- People (data) step on at the **bottom** (when clock is low) and ride up.
- They step off at the **top** (when clock is high).
- Only **one direction of motion** is allowed — controlled by a single power button (single clock phase).
- It is fast, simple, and uses **fewer clock distribution wires** (good for high-frequency design).

---

## 1.4 Datapath Subsystems — Adders

### Formal Definition
An **Adder** is a combinational datapath subsystem that computes the **arithmetic sum** of two $N$-bit binary numbers $A$ and $B$, producing an $N$-bit sum $S$ and a final carry-out $C_{out}$. It is the most fundamental and most performance-critical arithmetic block in any ALU, multiplier, MAC unit, or address generator.

### Intuition — "The Bucket Brigade"
Picture fire fighters passing water buckets along a line of people to douse a fire at the end:
- The **first person** ($C_0$) carries a starting bucket.
- Each subsequent person ($C_i$) **waits** for the bucket from the previous person **and** their local water supply ($A_i, B_i$) before passing a new bucket.
- The **time the whole line takes** depends on how fast the bucket is passed. This **propagation delay** is the central problem solved by faster adders.

### Standard Metric Definitions
- **Propagation Delay ($t_p$):** Worst-case time from input change to stable output.
- **Critical Path:** The longest combinational chain from any input to any output.
- **Area ($A$):** Measured in number of transistors, gate equivalents, or $\mu m^2$.
- **Power ($P$):** Sum of static ($P_{stat}$) and dynamic ($P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$) components.

### Family of Adders Covered in KTU Module 4
| # | Adder | Acronym | Key Idea |
|---|-------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Full Adder | FA | One-bit sum with $C_{in}, C_{out}$ |
| 2 | Ripple Carry Adder | RCA | Chain of FAs — simplest |
| 3 | Carry Chain Adder | CCA | Optimised transistor-level carry path |
| 4 | Carry-Bypass Adder | CBA | Skips long carry chains via MUX |
| 5 | Carry Select Adder | CSLA | Computes both 0/1 carry cases in parallel |
| 6 | Carry Look-Ahead Adder | CLA | Parallel $C_i$ generation using PG logic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** You must be able to draw the **gate-level schematic of a 1-bit Full Adder**, derive its **propagation delay expression**, and **compare 4-bit / 8-bit delay expressions** of RCA, CBA, CSLA, and CLA.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operation of the C²MOS Latch

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Precharge/Setup (when $\phi$ = 0):** Both NMOS pass-transistors (M3, M4) and PMOS pass-transistors (M1, M2) are OFF. The cross-coupled inverters are **isolated** from the input D. The previously stored charge on the parasitic capacitance $C_L$ at the output node Q is **held**.
2. **Evaluate (when $\phi$ = 1):** The pass-transistors turn ON. The input D is **sampled** and propagates to the output. The cross-coupled inverters amplify the value and **regenerate** the level (static feedback).
3. **Hold (when $\phi$ returns to 0):** The pass-transistors turn OFF again. The storage node is **isolated**, and the data is preserved on $C_L$ as long as leakage is negligible.

### Why Does It Work So Well?
- The **two-phase complementary clock** ($\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$) ensures that **at least one of the pass-transistor pairs is OFF** at any time, providing inherent **isolation**.
- The structure is **race-free** because the storage nodes are never simultaneously connected to both input and output.

### The 'How' — Robustness Against Clock Skew
Even if $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$ overlap slightly (clock skew), the **regenerative feedback** of the inverters prevents data corruption because:
- The inverters have **high gain** in the transition region.
- The stored value is **reinforced** every cycle.

---

## 2.2 Operation of the TSPC Register

### Step-by-Step Logic (Negative edge-triggered TSPC)
1. **Stage 1 — Front dynamic stage (NMOS):** When CLK = 0, the input D is sampled onto the **floating capacitance** of node X.
2. **Stage 1 — Pre-discharge:** When CLK = 1, the stage evaluates — node X is **conditionally discharged** based on the stored value.
3. **Stage 2 — Back static-like stage (PMOS):** When CLK = 1, the output Q is driven by the back stage based on X.
4. **Master–Slave chaining:** The combination of (negative-level) front stage + (positive-level) back stage yields **negative-edge-triggered** master–slave behaviour using only **one clock wire**.

### Why TSPC Is Popular
- **Lower clock loading** → lower dynamic power on clock distribution.
- **Fewer transistors** than static master–slave flip-flops.
- **Faster** because there is no feedback loop in the critical path.
- **Drawback:** Sensitive to leakage at low frequencies; **charge sharing** can corrupt data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students confuse TSPC polarity. Remember:
> - **Front stage NMOS + back stage PMOS = Negative-edge-triggered**
> - **Front stage PMOS + back stage NMOS = Positive-edge-triggered**

---

## 2.3 The 1-Bit Full Adder (FA)

### Logical Equations

$$
S_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i
$$

$$
C_{i+1} = (A_i \cdot B_i) + (A_i \oplus B_i) \cdot C_i
$$

### Structural Decomposition
The full adder is conventionally built from **two half adders** plus an OR gate:
1. **First Half Adder:** Inputs $A_i, B_i$ → outputs $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ (propagate) and $G_i = A_i \cdot B_i$ (generate).
2. **Second Half Adder:** Inputs $P_i, C_i$ → outputs $S_i = P_i \oplus C_i$ and $C'_{i+1} = P_i \cdot C_i$.
3. **OR gate:** $C_{i+1} = G_i + C'_{i+1}$.

### Two Key Signals: Propagate ($P_i$) and Generate ($G_i$)
- **$G_i = A_i \cdot B_i$**: Position $i$ **generates** a carry regardless of $C_i$.
- **P_i = A_i \oplus B_i**: Position $i$ **propagates** an incoming carry to $C_{i+1}$.
- **Kill ($K_i = \overline{A_i} \cdot \overline{B_i}$):** Position $i$ **kills** any incoming carry.

---

## 2.4 The Ripple Carry Adder (RCA)

### Operation
The RCA is the **most straightforward** $N$-bit adder: chain $N$ full adders, connecting $C_{i+1}$ of stage $i$ to $C_i$ of stage $i+1$. The carry **ripples** from LSB to MSB like a wave.

### Critical Path Delay
The worst-case path is from $A_0$ or $B_0$ (or $C_0$) to $C_N$:

$$
t_{RCA} = N \cdot t_{carry} + t_{sum}
$$

where:
- $t_{carry}$ = delay of generating $C_{i+1}$ from $A_i, B_i, C_i$
- $t_{sum}$ = delay from $A_N, B_N, C_N$ to $S_N$ (a single XOR)

### Why Is RCA Slow?
Because the **MSB cannot be computed** until the carry from the LSB has fully propagated through all $N$ stages. The delay scales **linearly** with $N$ — at $N=64$, this becomes unacceptable for high-speed designs.

---

## 2.5 The Carry-Bypass Adder (CBA)

### Key Insight
For a block of $k$ bits, the **bypass condition** is $P_{block} = P_{i} \cdot P_{i+1} \cdots P_{i+k-1}$ — if the entire block is in propagate mode, the input carry can **bypass** the block via a multiplexer, saving $k-1$ carry-chain delays.

### Critical Path Delay (for $N$ bits split into $N/k$ blocks of size $k$)

$$
t_{CBA} = (k-1)\,t_{carry} + t_{bypass} + \left(\dfrac{N}{k} - 1\right)(k\,t_{carry} + t_{bypass}) + t_{sum}
$$

Simplified (assuming bypass path is comparable to a single FA):

$$
t_{CBA} \approx N \cdot t_{carry} - (k-1)\,t_{carry} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{N/k}\right)
$$

### Optimal Block Size
For an $N$-bit adder, the **optimal $k$** is approximately $\sqrt{N/2}$ (for 4-bit CBA with $N=16$, $k \approx 3$).

---

## 2.6 The Carry Select Adder (CSLA)

### Key Insight
The CSLA **pre-computes** the sum and $C_{out}$ for **both cases** ($C_{in}=0$ and $C_{in}=1$) in **parallel** using two independent RCA blocks. A final **MUX** selects the correct pair based on the actual $C_{in}$. The delay becomes dominated by the MUX instead of the carry chain.

### Critical Path Delay
For an $N$-bit adder split into blocks of varying sizes (e.g., 4, 4, 8, 16 — linearly increasing):

$$
t_{CSLA} = t_{setup} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} t_{block_j} + t_{MUX}
$$

where $t_{setup}$ is the time to produce the $C_{in}$ of the largest block, and $t_{block_j}$ is the ripple delay of block $j$.

### Why CSLA Is Faster Than RCA
- In RCA, $t_{RCA} = N \cdot t_{carry}$ — **linear**.
- In CSLA, $t_{CSLA} \approx \sqrt{N} \cdot t_{carry}$ (with optimal variable block sizing) — **sub-linear**.

---

## 2.7 The Carry Look-Ahead Adder (CLA)

### Key Insight
The CLA **eliminates the ripple** entirely by computing all carries **in parallel** using the recursive relations:

$$
C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i
$$

### Recursive Expansion
Expanding $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$:

$$
C_1 = G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

$$
C_2 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

$$
C_3 = G_2 + P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

$$
C_4 = G_3 + P_3 \cdot G_2 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot G_1 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

### Critical Path Delay

$$
t_{CLA} = t_{PG} + t_{AND-OR\ tree} + t_{XOR} \approx \log_2(N) \cdot t_{gate}
$$

The delay grows **logarithmically** with $N$ — a massive improvement over RCA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block CLA:** For very large $N$ (e.g., 64-bit), a pure CLA becomes impractical due to fan-in. We use a **hierarchical CLA**: build 4-bit CLAs as basic blocks, then use a second-level CLA on the block-level $G, P$ signals.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Metric | Units | Notes |
|---------|------------------|-------|-------|
| Charge Retention Time | $\tau = C_L \cdot V_{DD} / I_{leak}$ | seconds | Governs dynamic latch hold time |
| Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Watts | $\alpha$ = switching activity |
| Full Adder Sum | $S_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i$ | boolean | |
| Full Adder Carry | $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i$ | boolean | $G_i = A_i \cdot B_i$, $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ |
| RCA Delay | $t_{RCA} = N \cdot t_{carry} + t_{sum}$ | seconds | Linear in N |
| CBA Delay | $t_{CBA} \approx (N/k + k - 2)\,t_{carry}$ | seconds | Optimal $k \approx \sqrt{N}$ |
| CSLA Delay | $t_{CSLA} \approx (N/M + M)\,t_{carry}$ | seconds | M = number of blocks |
| CLA Delay | $t_{CLA} \approx \log_2 N \cdot t_{gate}$ | seconds | Logarithmic in N |
| Generate Signal | $G_i = A_i \cdot B_i$ | boolean | Position i forces carry |
| Propagate Signal | $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ | boolean | Position i passes carry |
| Kill Signal | $K_i = \overline{A_i} \cdot \overline{B_i}$ | boolean | Position i blocks carry |
| Block Generate | $G_{block} = G_3 + P_3 G_2 + P_3 P_2 G_1 + P_3 P_2 P_1 G_0$ | boolean | Hierarchical CLA |
| Block Propagate | $P_{block} = P_3 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_0$ | boolean | Hierarchical CLA |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RCA:** Used in low-power, area-critical applications (microcontrollers, IoT sensors, ALU tail bits).
- **CBA:** Used in mid-performance datapaths where moderate area overhead is acceptable.
- **CSLA:** Used in DSPs and GPUs where a balanced area–speed trade-off is needed.
- **CLA:** Used in **critical-path arithmetic units** — modern Intel/AMD CPUs, ARM Cortex cores, FPU mantissa adders.
- **Dynamic Latches/Registers:** Used in **high-speed pipeline stages** of microprocessors, where speed and clock-load are paramount and leakage is controlled via power gating.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carry Expression for a 4-Bit Ripple Carry Adder

We start with the FA carry equation and apply it iteratively.

### Step 1 — Define base equations

$$
G_i = A_i \cdot B_i, \quad P_i = A_i \oplus B_i
$$

$$
C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i \cdot C_i
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $C_1$ (carry out of bit 0)

$$
C_1 = G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

This is the carry out of stage 0, given the input carry $C_0$ (usually 0 for LSB).

### Step 3 — Compute $C_2$ (carry out of bit 1)

$$
C_2 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot C_1
$$

Substitute $C_1$ from Step 2:

$$
C_2 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot (G_0 + P_0 \cdot C_0)
$$

Apply distributive law:

$$
C_2 = G_1 + P_1 \cdot G_0 + P_1 \cdot P_0 \cdot C_0
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $C_3$ (carry out of bit 2)

$$
C_3 = G_2 + P_2 \cdot C_2
$$

Substitute $C_2$:

$$
C_3 = G_2 + P_2 \cdot (G_1 + P_1 G_0 + P_1 P_0 C_0)
$$

Distribute $P_2$:

$$
C_3 = G_2 + P_2 G_1 + P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0
$$

### Step 5 — Compute $C_4$ (carry out of bit 3, the MSB carry)

$$
C_4 = G_3 + P_3 \cdot C_3
$$

Substitute:

$$
C_4 = G_3 + P_3 G_2 + P_3 P_2 G_1 + P_3 P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_3 P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0
$$

This is the **Carry Look-Ahead (CLA)** form. The full derivation is the formal proof that CLA expressions are mathematically equivalent to RCA outputs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 4-bit CLA requires one **AND-OR** gate of fan-in 4 (for $C_4$) and an XOR for $S_i = P_i \oplus C_i$. The critical path is **two gate delays** ($t_{AND-OR} + t_{XOR}$) regardless of $N=4$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Delay of a 4-Bit Carry-Bypass Adder

### Setup
- $N = 4$ bits total, block size $k = 4$ (single block — degenerate case).
- For $N = 16$ with $k = 4$ (4 blocks of 4 bits each), the analysis is more meaningful.

### Step 1 — Time to produce $C_4$ inside block 0
The carry ripples through 3 FAs in the first block:

$$
t_{C_4} = 3 \cdot t_{carry}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the block propagate signal $P_{block_0}$

$$
P_{block_0} = P_0 \cdot P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot P_3
$$

This takes one AND gate of fan-in 4. So:

$$
t_{P_{block_0}} = 3 \cdot t_{AND}\ (\text{using a tree of 2-input ANDs}) \approx t_{AND}
$$

### Step 3 — Time to traverse the bypass MUX of block 0
The MUX selects between $C_4$ (rippled) and $C_0$ (bypassed). The select line is $P_{block_0}$. Both inputs must be ready **before** the MUX evaluates:

$$
t_{bypass} = \max(3 \cdot t_{carry},\ t_{AND}) + t_{MUX}
$$

For typical values where $t_{carry} > t_{AND}$, $t_{bypass} \approx 3 \cdot t_{carry} + t_{MUX}$.

### Step 4 — Time to reach $C_{16}$ across all 4 blocks
The carry exits block 0 via bypass (or ripple), enters block 1, etc. For all 4 blocks in bypass mode:

$$
t_{CBA,16} = 4 \cdot (3 \cdot t_{carry} + t_{MUX}) \approx 12 \cdot t_{carry} + 4 \cdot t_{MUX}
$$

Compare with a 16-bit RCA: $t_{RCA} = 15 \cdot t_{carry} + t_{sum} \approx 15 \cdot t_{carry}$.

CBA saves about $\sim 3 \cdot t_{carry}$ at the cost of 4 MUXes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Delay of a 4-Bit Carry Select Adder (uniform blocks)

### Step 1 — Both 4-bit RCAs operate in parallel
Two 4-bit RCAs compute $(S^0, C_5^0)$ assuming $C_{in}=0$ and $(S^1, C_5^1)$ assuming $C_{in}=1$. Each takes $3 \cdot t_{carry}$ for the carry chain.

$$
t_{dualRCA} = 3 \cdot t_{carry} + t_{sum} \quad \text{(for sum bits)}
$$

### Step 2 — Final MUX selection
A 5-bit MUX (for 4 sum bits + final carry) selects based on actual $C_{in}$:

$$
t_{CSLA} = 3 \cdot t_{carry} + t_{MUX} + t_{sum}
$$

For a 16-bit CSLA with 4 blocks of 4 bits, blocks are sized non-uniformly (e.g., 2-3-4-5-2 or 4-4-4-4) for better delay balancing.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Critical Path of a 4-Bit Carry Look-Ahead Adder

### Step 1 — Time to generate $P_i$ and $G_i$ at every bit
This is one XOR and one AND gate delay in parallel:

$$
t_{PG} = \max(t_{XOR}, t_{AND}) = t_{XOR}
$$

### Step 2 — Time to compute $C_i$ using a 2-level AND-OR tree
For a 4-bit CLA, the fan-in of the deepest AND-OR term in $C_4$ is 5 (four $P_i$'s and one $C_0$). Using a balanced tree:

$$
t_{look-ahead} = 2 \cdot t_{gate} = 2 \cdot t_{AND-OR}
$$

(Two levels: first AND-OR for partial products, second to combine.)

### Step 3 — Time to compute $S_i$
After $C_i$ is known, the sum requires one XOR:

$$
t_{sum} = t_{XOR}
$$

### Step 4 — Total
$$
t_{CLA,4} = t_{XOR} + 2 \cdot t_{gate} + t_{XOR} \approx 2 \cdot t_{XOR} + 2 \cdot t_{gate}
$$

In modern CMOS: $t_{CLA,4} \approx 4$ gate delays. Compare to **$t_{RCA,4} = 4 \cdot t_{carry} \approx 8$ gate delays** — **2× speedup**.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — 16-bit Adders

Let $t_{carry} = 2 \Delta$ (typical CMOS FA carry delay), $t_{sum} = 2 \Delta$, $t_{MUX} = \Delta$, $t_{AND} = \Delta$.

| Adder Type | Critical Path Delay (in $\Delta$) | Area (Gate Equiv.) | Power Factor |
|------------|----------------------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| **RCA (16-bit)** | $15 \cdot 2 + 2 = 32 \Delta$ | $16 \times 5 = 80$ | $1.0 \times$ |
| **CBA (4 blocks × 4)** | $4 \times (3 \cdot 2 + 1) = 28 \Delta$ | $80 + 4 \times 4 = 96$ | $1.05 \times$ |
| **CSLA (4 blocks × 4, uniform)** | $4 \times 6 + 1 + 2 = 27 \Delta$ | $2 \times 80 + 4 \times 5 = 180$ | $1.4 \times$ |
| **CLA (16-bit, 2-level)** | $2 + \log_2(16) \cdot 1 + 2 = 6 \Delta$ | $\sim 200$ | $1.6 \times$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Matrix:** Speed ↑ (CLA ≪ CSLA < CBA < RCA), but **area and power also increase**. CLA is the fastest, RCA is the smallest — a classic engineering trade-off.

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Comparison Table for KTU Board

| Feature | RCA | CBA | CSLA | CLA |
|---------|-----|-----|------|-----|
| Delay Complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(N/\sqrt{N})$ | $O(\sqrt{N})$ | $O(\log N)$ |
| Area Complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(N + \sqrt{N})$ | $O(2N)$ | $O(N \log N)$ |
| Power | Lowest | Low | High | Highest |
| Regularity | Excellent | Good | Fair | Poor for large N |
| Used In | MCU, IoT | DSP | GPU, ALU | CPU, FPU |
