```mermaid
    b2b --> m2
    m2 --> cout2["C12"]
    cout2 --> b3a["RCA Block 3<br/>assume Cin=0"]
    cout2 --> b3b["RCA Block 3<br/>assume Cin=1"]
    b3a --> m3["MUX 3<br/>select = C12"]
    b3b --> m3
    m3 --> cout3["C16 final"]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Architecture — 4-Bit Carry Look-Ahead Adder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    a0["A0"] --> g0["G0 = A0 AND B0"]
    b0["B0"] --> g0
    a0 --> p0["P0 = A0 XOR B0"]
    b0 --> p0
    a1["A1"] --> g1["G1 = A1 AND B1"]
    b1["B1"] --> g1
    a1 --> p1["P1 = A1 XOR B1"]
    b1 --> p1
    a2["A2"] --> g2["G2 = A2 AND B2"]
    b2["B2"] --> g2
    a2 --> p2["P2 = A2 XOR B2"]
    b2 --> p2
    a3["A3"] --> g3["G3 = A3 AND B3"]
    b3["B3"] --> g3
    a3 --> p3["P3 = A3 XOR B3"]
    b3 --> p3
    g0 --> la["Look-ahead Logic<br/>AND-OR Tree"]
    p0 --> la
    g1 --> la
    p1 --> la
    g2 --> la
    p2 --> la
    g3 --> la
    p3 --> la
    c0["C0"] --> la
    la --> c1["C1"]
    la --> c2["C2"]
    la --> c3["C3"]
    la --> c4["C4"]
    p0 --> sx0["S0 = P0 XOR C0"]
    c0 --> sx0
    p1 --> sx1["S1 = P1 XOR C1"]
    c1 --> sx1
    p2 --> sx2["S2 = P2 XOR C2"]
    c2 --> sx2
    p3 --> sx3["S3 = P3 XOR C3"]
    c3 --> sx3
```

## 4.7 Mermaid Flow — Dynamic Latch Charge Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["State 1: Precharge<br/>phi = 0, phi_bar = 1<br/>pass transistors OFF"] -->|clock rises| s2["State 2: Evaluate<br/>phi = 1, phi_bar = 0<br/>input D sampled onto Q"]
    s2 -->|clock falls| s3["State 3: Hold<br/>phi = 0, phi_bar = 1<br/>charge trapped on parasitic C"]
    s3 -->|leakage over time| s4["State 4: Degraded<br/>voltage droops, data at risk"]
    s4 -->|must refresh| s1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a dynamic latch. Why is a refresh mechanism not required within a single clock cycle for typical high-speed operation?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A **dynamic latch** is a sequential storage element that temporarily stores a logic level on the **parasitic gate capacitance** of a MOS transistor, rather than using a closed-loop static feedback path. The data is held as **charge** on this node, which is isolated from the input during the hold phase by clocked pass-transistors.

Within a single clock cycle (typically 100 ps to 10 ns at modern VLSI frequencies), the **leakage current** ($I_{leak}$ in nA) is too small to discharge the storage capacitance ($C_L$ in fF) by a significant fraction. Specifically, the voltage droop $\Delta V = I_{leak} \cdot \Delta t / C_L$ remains well below the noise margin for the duration of one cycle. Hence, no active refresh is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> However, if the latch is held in storage for **many cycles** (e.g., clock gating or low-frequency operation), the charge leaks away and the data is lost — this is why dynamic logic is **incompatible with long idle periods**.

---

### Question 2: Compare the propagation delay scaling of a Ripple Carry Adder and a Carry Look-Ahead Adder. Which is preferred in modern CPUs and why?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
| Adder | Delay Scaling | Reason |
|-------|---------------|--------|
| RCA | $O(N)$ — linear in N | Carry must ripple through every stage |
| CLA | $O(\log N)$ — logarithmic in N | Carries generated in parallel via PG tree |

**Derivation sketch:** For an $N$-bit adder with per-stage carry delay $t_{carry}$:
- $t_{RCA} = N \cdot t_{carry} + t_{sum}$
- $t_{CLA} = t_{PG} + (\log_2 N) \cdot t_{gate} + t_{XOR}$

For $N = 64$: $t_{RCA} \approx 64 \cdot t_{carry}$ vs $t_{CLA} \approx 6 \cdot t_{gate}$ — a **~10× speedup**.

Modern CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Zen, Apple M-series) use **CLA or its hybrid variants (Kogge–Stone, Han–Carlson)** in their integer ALUs because the integer adder is on the **critical path of every instruction** (address calculation, branch resolution, cache indexing). Even a few hundred picoseconds of delay per cycle matters for GHz-class operation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Dynamic Latches and Full Adder Design**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

#### (a) Draw the circuit diagram of a C²MOS dynamic master–slave register and explain its operation. Show that it is robust against clock overlap. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A C²MOS master–slave register consists of **two C²MOS latches** in cascade. The master latch is driven by clock $\phi$ and the slave by complementary clock $\overline{\phi}$.

Each C²MOS latch is a **tristate inverter** followed by a **static inverter**:
- Tristate stage: Two NMOS (M3, M4) in series with the data path, controlled by $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$, plus two PMOS (M1, M2) controlled by $\overline{\phi}$ and $\phi$ respectively.
- Output inverter: Standard CMOS pair (M5 NMOS, M6 PMOS) for signal restoration.

**Step 2 — Operation:**

| Phase | $\phi$ | $\overline{\phi}$ | Master Behaviour | Slave Behaviour |
|-------|--------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Phase 1 | 0 | 1 | Isolated (holds Q_M) | Sampling D_M |
| Phase 2 | 1 | 0 | Sampling D | Isolated (holds Q) |

**Step 3 — Clock Overlap Robustness:**
When $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$ overlap (both briefly equal 1 or both 0), the master and slave are **never simultaneously in the evaluate mode**. The two phases are mutually exclusive. Additionally, the **regenerative feedback** of the static inverter inside each stage reinforces the stored value, preventing accidental data overwrite.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The C²MOS register achieves **edge-triggered** master–slave behaviour with **single-phase clocking** (two wires, $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$) and inherent robustness against skew.

---

#### (b) Design a 1-bit CMOS Full Adder schematic using transmission gates and derive the worst-case propagation delay for a 16-bit Ripple Carry Adder built from these FAs. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Transistor-Level Full Adder Block Diagram:**

The full adder is partitioned into three sub-blocks:
1. **XOR block** (for $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$): 4 transistors (transmission gate XOR).
2. **Carry block** (for $C_{i+1} = A_i B_i + (A_i \oplus B_i) C_i$): 6 transistors (AOI/OAI logic).
3. **Sum block** (for $S_i = P_i \oplus C_i$): 4 transistors.

Total: ~**20–24 transistors** per FA cell.

**Step 2 — Delay Derivation:**

Let:
- $t_{XOR}$ = delay of XOR gate (typical value: 2$\Delta$, where $\Delta$ = inverter delay).
- $t_{AND-OR}$ = delay of the carry generation logic (typical: 2$\Delta$).
- $t_{carry}$ = delay from $(A_i, B_i, C_i)$ to $C_{i+1}$:
  $$t_{carry} = t_{XOR}(A_i, B_i) + t_{AND-OR} = 2\Delta + 2\Delta = 4\Delta$$

**Step 3 — 16-bit RCA Delay:**

The worst-case path is from the LSB carry-in $C_0$ to the MSB carry-out $C_{16}$:

$$
t_{RCA,16} = 16 \cdot t_{carry} - \Delta_{\text{overlap}}
$$

A more accurate count is **15 carry stages** (FA 0 → FA 15):

$$
t_{RCA,16} = 15 \cdot 4\Delta + t_{sum} = 60\Delta + 2\Delta = 62\Delta
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity Check with Practical Values:**
For a 0.18 µm CMOS process, $\Delta \approx 50$ ps:

$$
t_{RCA,16} \approx 62 \times 50\ \text{ps} = 3.1\ \text{ns}
$$

This limits the maximum clock frequency to $f_{max} \approx 1/3.1\ \text{ns} \approx 322\ \text{MHz}$ — clearly too slow for modern GHz processors, motivating the use of **CLA or CSLA**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Carry Look-Ahead and Carry Select Adders**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

#### (a) Derive the carry equations for a 4-bit Carry Look-Ahead Adder from the base relation $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i C_i$. Show that the critical path is independent of the bit-width. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define generate and propagate:**

$$
G_i = A_i \cdot B_i, \qquad P_i = A_i \oplus B_i
$$

**Step 2 — Recursive expansion of $C_1$ through $C_4$:**

$$
C_1 = G_0 + P_0 C_0
$$

$$
C_2 = G_1 + P_1 G_0 + P_1 P_0 C_0
$$

$$
C_3 = G_2 + P_2 G_1 + P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0
$$

$$
C_4 = G_3 + P_3 G_2 + P_3 P_2 G_1 + P_3 P_2 P_1 G_0 + P_3 P_2 P_1 P_0 C_0
$$

**Step 3 — Critical Path Analysis:**

The longest term in $C_4$ has fan-in 5 (four $P_i$'s and $C_0$). Using a **balanced 2-level AND-OR tree**:
- Level 1: AND-OR of partial products → delay $t_{g1}$.
- Level 2: Final OR combination → delay $t_{g2}$.
- Total look-ahead delay: $t_{LA} = t_{g1} + t_{g2} = 2 t_{gate}$.

The sum bit $S_i = P_i \oplus C_i$ requires **one more XOR**: $t_{sum} = t_{XOR}$.

$$
t_{CLA,4} = t_{PG} + 2 t_{gate} + t_{XOR}
$$

**Step 4 — Independence from N:**

The 2-level AND-OR structure delivers $C_1, C_2, C_3, C_4$ **simultaneously** after a fixed number of gate delays — the **same delay** as for a 2-bit CLA, just with **wider gates**. For larger $N$ (e.g., 16-bit), a **block hierarchical CLA** is used where 4-bit CLAs are combined via a second-level CLA on block $G$ and $P$, yielding:

$$
t_{CLA,16} = t_{PG} + (\log_4 16) \cdot t_{LA} = t_{PG} + 2 \cdot t_{LA} = \text{constant-level} \cdot t_{gate}
$$

This logarithmic scaling is the key advantage over RCA.

---

#### (b) Explain the architecture of a 16-bit Carry Select Adder with uniform 4-bit blocks. Derive its worst-case delay and compare it to a 16-bit RCA. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture:**

A 16-bit CSLA is built from **4 identical blocks**, each 4 bits wide. Each block contains **two parallel 4-bit RCAs**: one assuming $C_{in}=0$, the other assuming $C_{in}=1$. A **5-bit MUX** (4 sum bits + 1 carry) selects the correct output based on the actual $C_{in}$.

**Step 2 — Per-Block Delay:**

Each 4-bit RCA produces $C_{out}$ in:

$$
t_{block} = 3 \cdot t_{carry} = 3 \cdot 4\Delta = 12\Delta
$$

(Three internal carry stages, $C_0$ to $C_4$.)

**Step 3 — Total CSLA Delay:**

The four blocks are chained. The carry from block 0 selects the MUX of block 1, and so on. Worst-case path:

$$
t_{CSLA,16} = 4 \cdot t_{block} + 4 \cdot t_{MUX} + t_{sum}
$$

Assuming $t_{MUX} = 2\Delta$:

$$
t_{CSLA,16} = 4 \cdot 12\Delta + 4 \cdot 2\Delta + 2\Delta = 48\Delta + 8\Delta + 2\Delta = 58\Delta
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with RCA:**

For a 16-bit RCA:

$$
t_{RCA,16} = 15 \cdot 4\Delta + 2\Delta = 62\Delta
$$

CSLA achieves:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{62\Delta}{58\Delta} \approx 1.07\times
$$

**Step 5 — Why the Speedup Is Modest:**

With **uniform** 4-bit blocks, the speedup is small. The **real benefit** of CSLA appears with **non-uniform** (linearly increasing) block sizes — e.g., 2-3-4-5-2 or 4-6-6-8 — where the critical path is balanced.

For 16-bit CSLA with optimal 2-3-4-5 blocks:

$$
t_{CSLA,16,opt} \approx 3 \cdot t_{block} + 3 \cdot t_{MUX} \approx 36\Delta + 6\Delta = 42\Delta
$$

This gives a speedup of $\frac{62}{42} \approx 1.48\times$ over RCA, with a 2× area penalty (two RCAs per block).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not forget** to mention the **MUX selection delay** in CSLA — many students compute only the ripple delay of the dual RCAs and miss the MUX term, losing ~2 marks.
> - In CLA derivations, **always** show the recursive expansion step by step. Skipping directly to the final $C_4$ equation will cost you 2–3 marks.
> - When drawing the C²MOS register, **label both clock phases** ($\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$) and indicate the **master/slave boundary** clearly. Examiners specifically look for this.
> - For dynamic latches, **never claim "no leakage"** — always qualify that the charge is **temporarily** held and the design is valid only for the relevant clock period.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **C²MOS latch** uses 6 transistors per stage; relies on complementary clocks $\phi$ and $\overline{\phi}$; race-free and skew-tolerant.
- [x] **TSPC register** uses a single clock phase; the front stage samples during $\phi=0$ (or $\phi=1$ depending on polarity) and the back stage evaluates during the opposite phase.
- [x] **Master–slave dynamic configuration** is what gives edge-triggered behaviour from two level-sensitive latches.
- [x] **Storage in dynamic circuits** is on the **parasitic gate capacitance**; leakage sets a hard limit on hold time.
- [x] **Full adder equations** to memorize: $S_i = A_i \oplus B_i \oplus C_i$ and $C_{i+1} = A_i B_i + (A_i \oplus B_i) C_i$.
- [x] **Generate $G_i = A_i B_i$** forces a carry regardless of $C_i$; **Propagate $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$** passes $C_i$ through.
- [x] **RCA delay** scales **linearly** $O(N)$ with bit-width — slow for large $N$.
- [x] **CBA** uses MUX-based bypass when $P_{block}=1$ across a group of bits; optimal block size $k \approx \sqrt{N}$.
- [x] **CSLA** computes **both** $C_{in}=0$ and $C_{in}=1$ cases in parallel; final MUX selects; area roughly **doubles**.
- [x] **CLA** computes all carries in parallel using AND-OR trees; delay is **$O(\log N)$** but area grows.
- [x] **Hierarchical / Block CLA** is used for wide adders (32, 64-bit) to limit fan-in.
- [x] **Trade-off triangle:** Speed ↔ Area ↔ Power — pick the adder that fits the design budget.
- [x] **Modern CPU integer units** use CLA/Kogge–Stone variants; **GPUs** often use CSLA for area-efficient speed; **MCUs/IoT** use RCA for power.
- [x] For **TSPC** — remember: front NMOS + back PMOS = **negative-edge** triggered; the opposite combination = **positive-edge** triggered.
- [x] **Clock skew robustness** in C²MOS comes from the **regenerative feedback** of the static inverter inside the tristate stage.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** In the 14-mark question, **always** spend ~2 minutes drawing the circuit / architecture cleanly, label all signals, and **write the delay equation explicitly**. Most marks in KTU valuation are awarded for the **derivation steps**, not just the final answer. Practice drawing C²MOS and TSPC schematics from memory — they appear almost every exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplier - Array Multiplier - Carry Save Multiplier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Multiplier - Array Multiplier - Carry Save Multiplier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Multiplier:** A combinational digital circuit that performs the arithmetic multiplication of two unsigned (or signed) binary numbers by generating **partial products** and subsequently **summing** them using a network of **full adders (FA)** and **half adders (HA)**.

In VLSI Design (PEICT526 – KTU 2024 Scheme), hardware multipliers are classified based on:
1. **Architecture** — *Array* (systolic/regular) vs. *Tree* (irregular but fast).
2. **Data representation** — *Unsigned* vs. *Signed* (Baugh–Wooley, Modified Booth).
3. **Acceleration technique** — *Carry Save*, *Carry Lookahead*, *Wallace Tree*, *Dadda Tree*.

The two architectures specified in your Module 4 syllabus are:

| Architecture | Topology | Primary Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Array Multiplier** | Regular 2-D mesh of AND + FA cells | Predictable layout, easy VLSI realization |
| **Carry Save Multiplier** | Column compression using CSA + CPA | Reduced critical path delay |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Plain-English Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Hand-Multiplication" Picture**
> Think of multiplying two 4-digit numbers the way you did in primary school:
> - You write one number above the other.
> - For **each** digit of the bottom number, you produce a **partial product row** (a shifted, digit-by-digit multiplication).
> - You then **stack** all these rows and **add them up diagonally** to get the final result.

In hardware:
- Each row of partial products is generated by an **array of AND gates**.
- The "diagonal addition" is performed by a **mesh of full adders** — one per bit-column.
- The final result is read out from the bottom and right edges of the mesh.

For an **N × N multiplier**, you have **N² partial products** and **N rows of accumulation** — exactly mirroring the manual long-multiplication algorithm.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Transistor count per Full Adder (static CMOS):** ≈ **24–28 transistors**.
- **Full-adder gate delay ($T_{FA}$):** typically **2 gate delays** ($T_{XOR} + T_{XOR}$).
- **Half-adder gate delay ($T_{HA}$):** typically **2 gate delays** ($T_{XOR} + T_{AND}$).
- **AND-gate delay ($T_{AND}$):** typically **1 gate delay**.
- **Standard cell area unit:** usually normalized per **2-input NAND gate ($\lambda^2$)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — Binary Long Multiplication

Let $A = a_{N-1}\,a_{N-2}\dots a_1\,a_0$ and $B = b_{N-1}\,b_{N-2}\dots b_1\,b_0$ be two unsigned N-bit numbers. Their product $P = A \times B$ is a 2N-bit number.

The product is expanded as:

$$P = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} (a_i \cdot b_j)\, 2^{i+j}$$

Each term $(a_i \cdot b_j)$ is a **partial product bit** generated by a single **AND gate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step execution logic (the "Why"):**
> 1. **Generate** N² partial products using N² AND gates (each row is shifted by one bit position).
> 2. **Compress** the N rows of partial products into 2 rows using a column-addition network.
> 3. **Add** the final 2 rows with a fast Carry Propagate Adder (CPA) to obtain the 2N-bit result.

## 2.2 Array Multiplier (Braun Multiplier) — Deep Theory

The **Array Multiplier** is the most direct hardware realization of the long-multiplication algorithm.

**Structural breakdown:**
- **Partial-Product Matrix:** an N × N array of AND gates.
- **Accumulator Mesh:** an (N−1) × N array of Full Adders (FA) + N Half Adders (HA) for the first column.
- **Output:** 2N bits tapped from the bottom row (sums) and rightmost column (carries).

**Propagation logic per cell:**
- Each FA receives: two bits from "above" (sum-in) and one bit from the "left" (carry-in).
- It produces: a **sum bit** going "down" and a **carry bit** going "right".

**Why does this work?**
Because every FA at row *k*, column *i* adds three input bits: the partial-product bit $a_i \cdot b_k$ and the carry and sum coming from the previous column. This exactly mimics the manual diagonal accumulation.

## 2.3 Carry Save Multiplier — Deep Theory

The **Carry Save Multiplier** accelerates the inner reduction by using **Carry Save Adders (CSA)** instead of ripple-carry adders.

**Key idea:**
> Instead of propagating carries *immediately* down a column, a CSA **saves** them to a separate word and only merges them at the **final stage** using a fast **Carry Propagate Adder (CPA)**.

**Three-input CSA (the building block):**
- Inputs: $X, Y, Z$ (three N-bit vectors).
- Outputs: $S$ (sum vector) and $C$ (carry vector, shifted left by 1).
- Relation: $X + Y + Z = S + (C \ll 1)$.

**Why is it faster?**
The CSA performs addition in **one full-adder delay** ($T_{FA}$) regardless of operand width, because there is **no carry chain** within the CSA — the carry bits are merely *saved*, not propagated.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essential. Memorize with units and assumptions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partial products (unsigned) | $N^2$ | AND-gate count |
| 2 | Full Adders in array | $N(N-1)$ | In accumulator mesh |
| 3 | Half Adders in array | $N$ | First column of each row |
| 4 | Critical path delay (Array) | $T_{AND} + (N-1)\cdot T_{FA}$ | Seconds / gate-delays |
| 5 | Critical path delay (Carry-Save) | $T_{AND} + \lceil \log_2 N \rceil \cdot T_{FA} + T_{CPA}$ | $T_{CPA} \approx \log_2 N \cdot T_{FA}$ |
| 6 | Output bit-width | $2N$ | Bits |
| 7 | FA gate delay (CMOS) | $2 \cdot T_{gate}$ | $T_{gate}$ = std. inverter delay |
| 8 | HA gate delay (CMOS) | $T_{XOR} + T_{AND} = 3\,T_{gate}$ | For first column only |
| 9 | Total adder delay (approx.) | $(2N - 1)\,T_{gate}$ | For N-bit Array Multiplier |
| 10 | Transistor count (approx.) | $28 \cdot N(N-1) + N \cdot 12 + N^2 \cdot 6$ | 28T/FA, 12T/HA, 6T/AND |

> **Note:** In the table above, the symbols use $\cdot$ for multiplication and the operator "$\ll 1$" denotes a left shift by 1 bit position.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSP chips** (filters, FFT) — need millions of MAC (Multiply-Accumulate) operations per second.
- **GPU shader cores** — array multipliers dominate because of their **regular layout** (easy place-and-route).
- **AI/ML accelerators** — quantized INT8 / INT4 multipliers are essentially modified array multipliers.
- **Microprocessor ALUs** — use a hybrid: array multiplier with a **final Wallace-tree** stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Path Delay of an N-bit Array Multiplier

We will derive the longest combinational path (the path that determines the worst-case delay, i.e., the maximum time before the output is valid).

**Step 1: Identify the structure.**
For an N × N unsigned array multiplier:
- The first diagonal of adders begins at column 1 (the LSB is just $a_0 b_0$ — no adder needed).
- Each subsequent diagonal adds one more FA in series.

**Step 2: Count the FA stages on the critical path.**
The critical path begins at the top-left AND gate ($a_0 b_0$ generation), passes **down and to the right** through the FA mesh, and exits at the bottom-right output.

The number of FA stages traversed = **N − 1** (since the first column needs no FA, the second needs 1, the third needs 2, …, the N-th needs N − 1).

**Step 3: Add the initial AND-gate delay.**
The partial-product bit must first be computed:

$$T_{partial} = T_{AND} = 1 \cdot T_{gate}$$

**Step 4: Add the FA delays along the critical path.**

$$T_{accum} = (N - 1) \cdot T_{FA}$$

**Step 5: Account for the final XOR of the last FA (or output buffer).**
The sum output of the last FA goes through one more gate (its internal XOR), which is already included in $T_{FA}$. Therefore no extra term is needed for the sum output.

**Step 6: Combine all contributions.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{mult,\,array} \;=\; T_{AND} \;+\; (N-1) \cdot T_{FA}\,}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute $T_{FA} = 2\,T_{gate}$ and $T_{AND} = 1\,T_{gate}$.**

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = 1 \cdot T_{gate} + (N-1) \cdot 2\,T_{gate}
$$

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = (2N - 1) \cdot T_{gate}
$$

**Worked example — N = 4:**

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = (2 \cdot 4 - 1) \cdot T_{gate} = 7 \cdot T_{gate}
$$

**Worked example — N = 8:**

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = (2 \cdot 8 - 1) \cdot T_{gate} = 15 \cdot T_{gate}
$$

> This **linear growth** ($O(N)$) is the main drawback of the array multiplier — doubling the bit-width **doubles** the delay.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Critical Path Delay of a Carry Save Multiplier

The carry-save structure compresses three operand rows into two in **each CSA stage**.

**Step 1: Count the number of CSA stages needed.**
For an N × N multiplier, after partial-product generation we have **N rows**. Each CSA reduces the count by one row (3 → 2), so to reduce from N rows to 2 rows we need:

$$
\text{CSA stages} = N - 2
$$

**Step 2: Realize the CSA stages in a tree (Wallace-style).**
A balanced tree of CSAs reduces the rows in $\lceil \log_{3/2} N \rceil$ stages. For board exams, the conservative (linear) estimate is used:

$$
k = N - 2 \quad \text{(worst case)}
$$

**Step 3: Add the carry-propagate-adder (CPA) delay.**
The final two rows (sum and shifted-carry) must be added by a CPA. Using a ripple-carry CPA:

$$
T_{CPA} = (2N - 1) \cdot T_{gate}
$$

Using a faster (Kogge–Stone) CPA:

$$
T_{CPA} = \log_2 (2N) \cdot T_{gate} = (1 + \log_2 N) \cdot T_{gate}
$$

**Step 4: Combine all contributions.**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{mult,\,CS} = T_{AND} + (N-2)\cdot T_{FA} + T_{CPA}\,}
$$

**Step 5: Comparison for N = 4 (using ripple CPA for both for a fair comparison):**

$$
T_{mult,\,CS} = 1 \cdot T_{gate} + 2 \cdot 2\,T_{gate} + 7\,T_{gate} = 12\,T_{gate}
$$

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = 7\,T_{gate}
$$

Wait — for very small N, the array multiplier may actually be **faster** because of its shorter CPA! The carry-save architecture shines when N is large. This is a key KTU trap-question answer.

**Step 6: True asymptotic comparison (large N, fast CPA):**

$$
T_{mult,\,CS} \approx T_{AND} + (1 + \log_2 N) \cdot T_{gate}
$$

$$
T_{mult,\,array} \approx (2N - 1) \cdot T_{gate}
$$

For N = 32:

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = 63\,T_{gate} \quad \text{vs.} \quad T_{mult,\,CS} \approx 6\,T_{gate}
$$

The carry-save multiplier is **~10× faster** for large N.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transistor Count of a 4×4 Array Multiplier

**Step 1: AND gates.**
Number = $N^2 = 4^2 = 16$. Each 2-input AND gate in CMOS uses **6 transistors** (NAND + INV).

$$
T_{AND\,total} = 16 \times 6 = 96 \text{ transistors}
$$

**Step 2: Full Adders.**
Number = $N(N-1) = 4 \times 3 = 12$. Each FA in standard CMOS uses **28 transistors**.

$$
T_{FA\,total} = 12 \times 28 = 336 \text{ transistors}
$$

**Step 3: Half Adders.**
Number = $N = 4$. Each HA uses **12 transistors**.

$$
T_{HA\,total} = 4 \times 12 = 48 \text{ transistors}
$$

**Step 4: Total.**

$$
T_{total} = 96 + 336 + 48 = 480 \text{ transistors}
$$

This matches the order-of-magnitude expected for a 4×4 unsigned multiplier in a 180-nm CMOS process.

## 3.4 Numerical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Ready)

| Architecture | Delay (4-bit) | Delay (8-bit) | Area | Regularity | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Array (Braun)** | $7\,T_g$ | $15\,T_g$ | Medium | ★★★★★ | Low |
| **Carry Save** | $12\,T_g$ (slow CPA) / $6\,T_g$ (fast CPA) | $\approx 7\,T_g$ | High | ★★ | Medium |
| **Wallace Tree** | $\approx 5\,T_g$ | $\approx 7\,T_g$ | High | ★ | High |
| **Modified Booth** | $4\,T_g$ | $5\,T_g$ | Very High | ★★ | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 4×4 Array Multiplier — Cell-Level Block Architecture

The diagram below shows the block-level functional architecture of a 4×4 unsigned array multiplier. The AND cells (partial-product generators) feed the FA mesh from above and the left. The outputs are tapped from the bottom row (sum bits) and the rightmost column (final carry bits).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A3[Multiplier A3] --> AND33[AND Cell 3-3]
    A3 --> AND32[AND Cell 3-2]
    A3 --> AND31[AND Cell 3-1]
    A3 --> AND30[AND Cell 3-0]
    A2[Multiplier A2] --> AND23[AND Cell 2-3]
    A2 --> AND22[AND Cell 2-2]
    A2 --> AND21[AND Cell 2-1]
    A2 --> AND20[AND Cell 2-0]
    A1[Multiplier A1] --> AND13[AND Cell 1-3]
    A1 --> AND12[AND Cell 1-2]
    A1 --> AND11[AND Cell 1-1]
    A1 --> AND10[AND Cell 1-0]
    A0[Multiplier A0] --> AND03[AND Cell 0-3]
    A0 --> AND02[AND Cell 0-2]
    A0 --> AND01[AND Cell 0-1]
    A0 --> AND00[AND Cell 0-0]

    B0[Multiplier B0] --> AND00
    B0 --> AND10
    B0 --> AND20
    B0 --> AND30
    B1[Multiplier B1] --> AND01
    B1 --> AND11
    B1 --> AND21
    B1 --> AND31
    B2[Multiplier B2] --> AND02
    B2 --> AND12
    B2 --> AND22
    B2 --> AND32
    B3[Multiplier B3] --> AND03
    B3 --> AND13
    B3 --> AND23
    B3 --> AND33

    AND00 --> FA1[Full Adder Row 0 Col 1]
    AND10 --> FA1
    AND01 --> FA1
    AND10 --> HA0[Half Adder Row 1 Col 0]
    AND20 --> HA0
    AND20 --> FA2[Full Adder Row 0 Col 2]
    AND30 --> FA2
    FA1 --> FA2
    AND30 --> FA3[Full Adder Row 0 Col 3]
    FA2 --> FA3
    HA0 --> FA4[Full Adder Row 1 Col 1]
    AND11 --> FA4
    AND21 --> FA4
    FA1 --> HA1[Half Adder Row 2 Col 0]
    AND31 --> HA1
    FA2 --> FA5[Full Adder Row 1 Col 2]
    AND21 --> FA5
    FA4 --> FA5
    FA3 --> FA6[Full Adder Row 1 Col 3]
    AND31 --> FA6
    FA5 --> FA6
    HA1 --> FA7[Full Adder Row 2 Col 1]
    AND12 --> FA7
    AND22 --> FA7
    FA4 --> FA8[Full Adder Row 2 Col 2]
    AND32 --> FA8
    FA7 --> FA8
    FA6 --> FA9[Full Adder Row 2 Col 3]
    AND22 --> FA9
    FA8 --> FA9
    FA7 --> FA10[Full Adder Row 3 Col 1]
    AND13 --> FA10
    AND23 --> FA10
    FA8 --> FA11[Full Adder Row 3 Col 2]
    AND33 --> FA11
    FA10 --> FA11
    FA9 --> FA12[Full Adder Row 3 Col 3]
    AND23 --> FA12
    FA11 --> FA12

    AND00 --> P0[Output P0]
    FA1 --> P1[Output P1]
    HA0 --> P2[Output P2]
    FA2 --> P3[Output P3]
    FA3 --> P4[Output P4]
    FA4 --> P5[Output P5]
    FA5 --> P6[Output P6]
    FA6 --> P7[Output P7]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each `ANDij` cell computes $A_i \cdot B_j$. Each `FAk` is a full adder in row *k* of the accumulation mesh. Outputs `P0` … `P7` form the final 8-bit product.

## 4.2 Carry Save Adder (CSA) — Block Architecture

The CSA is the fundamental building block of the carry-save multiplier. It accepts three N-bit operands and produces two N-bit outputs in **a single FA delay**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Input X N-bit] --> CSAcore{CSA Cell Array}
    Y[Input Y N-bit] --> CSAcore
    Z[Input Z N-bit] --> CSAcore
    CSAcore --> S[Sum Vector S]
    CSAcore --> C[Carry Vector C shifted left by 1]
    S --> CPA[Carry Propagate Adder]
    C --> CPA
    CPA --> Result[Final Sum Result]
```

> **Operational summary:** $X + Y + Z = S + (C \ll 1)$, where $S$ and $C$ are produced in **one $T_{FA}$** and the final $C \ll 1 + S$ is produced by the CPA in $T_{CPA}$.

## 4.3 Full Adder Cell — Internal Schematic Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    A --> XOR1[XOR Gate]
    B --> XOR1
    Cin --> XOR2[XOR Gate]
    XOR1 --> XOR2
    A --> AND1[AND Gate]
    B --> AND1
    XOR1 --> AND2[AND Gate]
    Cin --> AND2
    AND1 --> OR[OR Gate]
    AND2 --> OR
    XOR2 --> Sum[Sum Output]
    OR --> Cout[Carry Out]
```

## 4.4 Carry Save Multiplier — Top-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    PPA[Partial Product Array AND gates] --> CSA1[CSA Stage 1]
    CSA1 --> CSA2[CSA Stage 2]
    CSA2 --> CSAk[CSA Stage k]
    CSAk --> CPA[Carry Propagate Adder]
    CPA --> Out[2N bit Product]
    PPA -.generates N rows.-> CSA1
    CSAk -.reduces to 2 rows.-> CPA
```

> **Compression sequence:** N rows of partial products → (CSA tree) → 2 rows (sum + carry) → CPA → 2N-bit result.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial AND-gate delay** in the critical-path formula. Always add $T_{AND}$ to your FA chain.
> 2. **Confusing $N$ with $2N$** in the output width — the product of two N-bit numbers is **2N bits**, not N bits.
> 3. **Mixing up half-adder and full-adder counts** — there are exactly **N half adders** (one per row, at the LSB column of each row).
> 4. **Ignoring the final carry bit** — the MSB of the product is the carry-out of the *last* FA, not a sum output.
> 5. **For signed multiplication**, the array multiplier does NOT work directly — students forget the Baugh–Wooley transformation. (For Module 4 of KTU PEICT526, unsigned is the focus, but be aware of this trap.)

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a partial product in the context of a binary multiplier. How many partial products are generated in a 4×4 unsigned array multiplier?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A *partial product* is a single-bit product $a_i \cdot b_j$ generated by a 2-input AND gate, representing the contribution of bit *i* of the multiplicand and bit *j* of the multiplier at bit position $i+j$ of the final result.
- **Count (2 marks):** In a 4×4 unsigned multiplier, the number of partial products = $N^2 = 4^2 = \mathbf{16}$.
- These 16 partial products are arranged in 4 rows of 4 bits each, with each row shifted left by one bit position relative to the previous row.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the role of a Carry Save Adder (CSA) in a multiplier design? How does it differ from a Carry Propagate Adder (CPA)?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of CSA (1.5 marks):** The CSA is used to add **three** N-bit operands simultaneously, producing a **sum** vector and a **carry** vector (shifted left by 1). It compresses three rows into two in **one FA delay**, enabling fast reduction of partial-product rows in tree-based multipliers.
- **Difference from CPA (1.5 marks):**
   - **CSA:** Produces two output vectors in **1 $T_{FA}$**; carries are *saved*, not propagated.
   - **CPA:** Adds **two** operands to produce the final result; carries *propagate* through a chain (ripple) or via fast lookahead, taking $O(N)$ or $O(\log N)$ delay.
- In a carry-save multiplier, multiple CSAs are followed by a single CPA at the final stage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Draw the architecture of a 4×4 unsigned array multiplier and explain its operation with the help of a neat block diagram. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the critical path delay of an N-bit array multiplier in terms of $T_{AND}$ and $T_{FA}$. Calculate the same for a 4-bit and 8-bit multiplier assuming $T_{FA} = 2\,T_{gate}$ and $T_{AND} = 1\,T_{gate}$. (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — (a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the structure. A 4×4 unsigned array multiplier consists of:
- **16 AND gates** arranged as a 4×4 matrix (for partial-product generation).
- **12 Full Adders** ($N(N-1) = 4 \times 3 = 12$) in the accumulator mesh.
- **4 Half Adders** ($N = 4$) — one at the LSB of each accumulation row.
- **8 output bits** ($2N = 8$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe operation.
- Inputs: $A = a_3 a_2 a_1 a_0$, $B = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$.
- Partial product at row *i*, column *j* = $a_i \cdot b_j$, generated by AND gate.
- Each row of partial products is shifted left by one bit (effectively weighted by $2^{i}$).
- The accumulator mesh sums all 4 rows diagonally.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Trace one FA cell's logic. Each FA at row $k$, column $i$:
- Inputs: $a_i b_k$ (from above), $S_{i-1}$ (sum from previous column, from left), $C_{i-1}$ (carry from previous column, from above-left).
- Outputs: $S_i = a_i b_k \oplus S_{i-1} \oplus C_{i-1}$ (sent down), $C_i = \text{maj}(a_i b_k,\, S_{i-1},\, C_{i-1})$ (sent right).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Reference the block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above). Label AND cells, FA cells, and outputs $P_0$ to $P_7$.

---

### **Model Solution — (b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the critical path. It begins at the top-left AND gate, traverses the diagonal of FAs, and ends at the bottom-right output.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Count FA stages. Number of FAs on the diagonal = $N - 1$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Add the AND-gate delay. Total delay = $T_{AND} + (N-1) \cdot T_{FA}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute and compute.

For **N = 4**:

$$
T_{mult} = 1 \cdot T_{gate} + (4-1) \cdot 2\,T_{gate} = 1 + 6 = \mathbf{7\,T_{gate}}
$$

For **N = 8**:

$$
T_{mult} = 1 \cdot T_{gate} + (8-1) \cdot 2\,T_{gate} = 1 + 14 = \mathbf{15\,T_{gate}}
$$

**Conclusion (bonus 1 mark if mentioned):** The delay grows **linearly** with N — this is the chief limitation motivating the carry-save architecture.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of a Carry Save Multiplier with a neat block diagram. How does it differ from a conventional array multiplier in terms of delay and regularity? (7 marks)

**(b)** An 8-bit unsigned multiplier is to be designed.
   (i) Calculate the number of AND gates, full adders, and half adders required in the **array multiplier** realization. (3 marks)
   (ii) Calculate the critical path delay in terms of $T_{gate}$ assuming $T_{FA} = 2\,T_{gate}$. (2 marks)
   (iii) If the same multiplier is realized using a **carry-save** architecture with a ripple CPA, estimate the total delay. Comment on the trade-off. (2 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — (a)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Working principle.
- The carry-save multiplier first generates N rows of partial products using N² AND gates.
- It then **compresses** these N rows into 2 rows using a tree (or chain) of **Carry Save Adders (CSA)**.
- Each CSA adds three rows in **1 $T_{FA}$** by saving the carries to a separate vector.
- Finally, the two remaining rows (sum and carry) are added by a **Carry Propagate Adder (CPA)** to produce the 2N-bit product.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Differences from array multiplier (tabulate mentally):

| Aspect | Array | Carry Save |
|---|---|---|
| Delay | $O(N)$ | $O(\log N)$ (with fast CPA) |
| Regularity | ★★★★★ (uniform) | ★★ (irregular CSA tree) |
| Wiring | Simple | Complex (cross-overs) |
| Area | Smaller | Larger (more adders) |

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Reference the block diagram (see Section 4.4 above).

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Mention practical use — DSP and MAC units in modern processors.

---

### **Model Solution — (b) (i)** (3 marks)

For N = 8:

- **AND gates:** $N^2 = 8^2 = \mathbf{64}$
- **Full Adders:** $N(N-1) = 8 \times 7 = \mathbf{56}$
- **Half Adders:** $N = \mathbf{8}$

### **Model Solution — (b) (ii)** (2 marks)

$$
T_{mult,\,array} = T_{AND} + (N-1) \cdot T_{FA} = 1\,T_g + 7 \times 2\,T_g = \mathbf{15\,T_{gate}}
$$

### **Model Solution — (b) (iii)** (2 marks)

For a carry-save multiplier with a ripple CPA on 16-bit (2N) operands:

- CSA stages (linear): $N - 2 = 6$ → delay = $6 \times 2\,T_g = 12\,T_g$.
- CPA delay: $(2N - 1) \cdot T_g = 15\,T_g$.
- AND-gate delay: $1\,T_g$.

$$
T_{mult,\,CS} = 1\,T_g + 12\,T_g + 15\,T_g = \mathbf{28\,T_{gate}}
$$

**Trade-off comment:** For N = 8, the array multiplier (15 $T_g$) is *faster* than the linear-chain CS multiplier (28 $T_g$). However, if a **faster CPA** (Kogge–Stone, $\log_2 16 = 4$ gate delays) is used, the CS delay drops to $1 + 12 + 4 = 17\,T_g$, still not faster for N=8. The carry-save architecture becomes superior only for $N \gtrsim 16$ with a fast CPA, due to its $O(\log N)$ scaling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — must memorize before exam:**

- **Partial product count:** $N^2$ AND gates for N×N unsigned multiplication.
- **FA count:** $N(N-1)$ in the accumulator mesh.
- **HA count:** $N$ (one per row at the LSB column).
- **Output bit-width:** $2N$ bits.
- **Array multiplier critical path:** $T_{AND} + (N-1)\cdot T_{FA} = (2N-1)\,T_{gate}$.
- **Carry Save multiplier critical path:** $T_{AND} + (N-2)\cdot T_{FA} + T_{CPA}$ (linear) or $O(\log N)$ (tree).
- **CSA property:** $X + Y + Z = S + (C \ll 1)$ in one $T_{FA}$.
- **Delay growth:** Array → linear $O(N)$; Carry Save → logarithmic $O(\log N)$ with fast CPA.
- **Area growth:** Array → $O(N^2)$; Carry Save → $O(N^2)$ (similar) but with extra wiring overhead.
- **Layout regularity:** Array = best (uniform mesh → ideal for VLSI place-and-route); Carry Save = poor (irregular CSA tree).
- **FA transistor count:** ~28T (static CMOS); HA = ~12T; 2-input AND = 6T.
- **Signed multiplication caveat:** Plain array multiplier fails for negative numbers — requires Baugh–Wooley or sign-extension trick.
- **Power:** Array < Carry Save < Wallace (in general) because of reduced switching activity in the regular structure.
- **Practical rule of thumb:** For $N \leq 8$, use array multiplier. For $N \geq 16$ in a high-speed datapath, use carry-save or Wallace tree.
- **Examiner tip:** When asked for "speed up the array multiplier," your answer must mention *Carry Save* or *Wallace Tree* — never Booth encoding (that reduces partial products, not the adder chain).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shifter - Barrel Shifter - Logarithmic Shifter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT526 - VLSI DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shifter - Barrel Shifter - Logarithmic Shifter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Data Path Design: Shifters, Barrel Shifters & Logarithmic Shifters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Shifter? (Foundation Definition)

In VLSI data-path design, a **Shifter** is a combinational logic unit that performs bit-wise lateral displacement (translation) of an N-bit input word either to the **Left** (towards the MSB) or to the **Right** (towards the LSB), with controlled insertion of logic `0`, logic `1`, or sign-bit values into the vacated bit positions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A shifter is a digital arithmetic/logic unit that translates a binary vector $D[0 \ldots N-1]$ to $Y[0 \ldots N-1]$ such that $Y[i] = D[i \pm k]$ where $k$ is the user-defined shift amount. The operation is formally known as *non-restoring bit-shift multiplication/division by powers of two*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conveyor Belt" Model

Imagine an airport baggage conveyor belt:

| Component | Conveyor Belt Equivalent | Hardware Realization |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Baggage (input) | Suitcases on the belt | Input data word `D[7:0]` |
| Operator's hand (left/right) | Direction control knob | `DIR` select line |
| Number of slots to slide | Steps walked along the belt | Shift amount `S` |
| Empty slots created | Open positions | Vacated bit positions (filled with `0`, `1`, or sign) |

- A **basic shifter** is like moving each suitcase by a *fixed* number of slots — you need to walk along the belt one step at a time (one shift per clock cycle).
- A **barrel shifter** is like a *hydraulic lift* that slides the entire row of suitcases by ANY number of slots in a single, atomic action (one clock cycle).
- A **logarithmic shifter** is the *space-efficient* version of the barrel shifter — instead of one huge hydraulic arm, it uses a stack of smaller, smarter arms working sequentially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why care about shifters in VLSI?**
> 1. They form the core of **multiplication/division by powers of two** (cheaper than full multipliers).
> 2. They are critical in **Floating-Point Units (FPUs)** for mantissa alignment.
> 3. They implement **rotations**, **end-around carries**, and **bit-field extraction** in RISC processors.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Shift Operations

A complete shifter supports four primitive operations:

1. **Logical Shift Left (LSL)** — Inserts `0` at LSB; vacated LSB $\to$ MSB end.
2. **Logical Shift Right (LSR)** — Inserts `0` at MSB; MSB bits are discarded.
3. **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR)** — Inserts the *sign bit* (sign extension) at MSB; essential for two's-complement division.
4. **Rotate (ROL / ROR)** — End-around shift; bits rotated out at one end re-enter at the other; **no bits lost**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU board examiner specifically tests the difference between `LSR` and `ASR`**. Confusing them is a guaranteed 3-mark loss (see SECTION 5 valuation warning).

### 1.4 Barrel Shifter — Formal Definition

A **Barrel Shifter** is a combinational digital circuit that can shift an N-bit data word by an arbitrary amount $k$ (where $0 \leq k \leq N-1$) in **exactly one clock cycle**, realized as a regular array (matrix) of $N \times N$ pass-transistor or multiplexer switches.

- **Key Property:** Single-cycle throughput — *no iterative shifting required*.
- **Cost:** $N^2$ switching elements for a fully-unrolled direct implementation.
- **Throughput-Latency Trade-off:** Trades silicon area for latency.

### 1.5 Logarithmic Shifter — Formal Definition

A **Logarithmic Shifter** is an *area-optimized* realization of a barrel shifter. It decomposes an arbitrary shift of $k$ positions into a binary-weighted cascade of $m = \log_2 N$ stages, where stage $i$ ($i = 0, 1, \ldots, m-1$) performs either a `0`-position shift or a fixed shift of $2^i$ positions.

- **Key Property:** Reduces area complexity from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.
- **Cost:** $N \times \log_2 N$ switching elements.
- **Penalty:** Latency increases to $O(\log_2 N)$ levels of multiplexer delay.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Shifter

Let the input data word be $D = d_{N-1}d_{N-2} \ldots d_1 d_0$ and shift amount be $k$.

**Logical Shift Left by $k$:**
$$Y[i] = \begin{cases} d_{i-k} & \text{if } i \geq k \\ 0 & \text{if } i < k \end{cases}$$

**Logical Shift Right by $k$:**
$$Y[i] = \begin{cases} d_{i+k} & \text{if } i < N-k \\ 0 & \text{if } i \geq N-k \end{cases}$$

**Arithmetic Shift Right by $k$:**
$$Y[i] = \begin{cases} d_{i+k} & \text{if } i < N-k \\ d_{N-1} & \text{(sign bit) if } i \geq N-k \end{cases}$$

### 2.2 Barrel Shifter — Architecture & Operation

A direct (unrolled) barrel shifter is a **2D crossbar switch** with:
- **N rows** (one per output bit $Y_i$).
- **N columns** (one per input bit $d_j$).
- A control decoder that activates the diagonal "stripe" corresponding to shift amount $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Rule:** For a shift-left barrel shifter, output $Y_i$ is wired directly from input $d_{i-k}$ when $0 \leq k \leq N-1$. Each output row thus contains exactly one "active" pass transistor per shift value.

For each output bit $Y_i$, the selector signal to the multiplexer is:
$$Y_i = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left( d_{i-k} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{i \geq k} \right) \cdot \delta_{s,k}$$
where $\delta_{s,k}$ is the Kronecker delta selecting shift amount $k$.

### 2.3 Logarithmic Shifter — Binary Decomposition

The key mathematical insight: *any* integer shift $k$ in $[0, N-1]$ can be uniquely written as a binary-weighted sum:
$$k = s_{m-1} \cdot 2^{m-1} + s_{m-2} \cdot 2^{m-2} + \ldots + s_1 \cdot 2^1 + s_0 \cdot 2^0$$

where $s_i \in \{0, 1\}$ are the bits of $k$ and $m = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.

This means a shift by $k$ can be performed by *cascading* $m$ stages, where stage $i$ either shifts by $2^i$ or does nothing, controlled by bit $s_i$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. Every KTU question on this module uses at least one of these formulas.**

| Parameter | Barrel Shifter (Direct) | Logarithmic Shifter |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Input word size | $N$ bits | $N$ bits |
| Number of stages ($m$) | **1** (combinational) | $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ |
| Shift amount range | $0 \leq k \leq N-1$ | $0 \leq k \leq N-1$ |
| Muxes per stage | $N$ (one per output bit) | $N$ (one per output bit) |
| Total muxes | $N^2$ | $N \cdot \log_2 N$ |
| Total transistors ($\approx 4N^2$ for CMOS muxes) | $4N^2$ | $4N \log_2 N$ |
| Critical path delay | **1 mux delay** ($t_{mux}$) | $\log_2 N \cdot t_{mux}$ |
| Area complexity class | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N \log_2 N)$ |
| Delay complexity class | $O(1)$ | $O(\log_2 N)$ |
| AT product (Area $\times$ Delay) | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N (\log_2 N)^2)$ |
| Power dissipation | High (parasitic cap of all wires) | Moderate |
| Wire length | Long (cross-bar) | Short (local routing) |
| Typical use | High-speed ALUs (e.g., ARM barrel shifter) | Area-constrained designs (ASIC, IoT) |
| Control complexity | $N$-to-$N$ decoder ($\log_2 N$ inputs) | $\log_2 N$ select bits (one per stage) |

> **Notation:** All logarithms in this section are base-2 unless explicitly stated. Vertical bar characters in equations use `\vert` or `\mid` for markdown compatibility.

### 2.5 Comparative Numerical Example (N = 8)

For an **8-bit** shifter:

| Metric | Direct Barrel | Logarithmic |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Total muxes | $8^2 = 64$ | $8 \times \log_2 8 = 24$ |
| Mux delay stages | 1 | 3 |
| Savings in muxes | — | $64 - 24 = 40$ (62.5% reduction) |
| Wire count | $8 \times 8 = 64$ | $3 \times 8 = 24$ (local) |

For an **N = 32** shifter:

| Metric | Direct Barrel | Logarithmic |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Total muxes | $32^2 = 1024$ | $32 \times \log_2 32 = 160$ |
| Mux delay stages | 1 | 5 |
| Savings in muxes | — | $864$ (84.4% reduction) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ARM Cortex-A series processors** use a true combinational barrel shifter in the datapath to perform shifts, rotations, and bit-field extracts *for free* on every ALU operand — a hallmark feature of the ARM ISA.
- **RISC-V `SLL`, `SRL`, `SRA` instructions** are commonly implemented using logarithmic shifters on energy-constrained cores.
- **DSP chips** (e.g., TI TMS320 family) use barrel shifters to scale fixed-point operands after multiplication without consuming ALU cycles.
- **FPGA LUTs** inherently implement barrel shifters because the shift amount can be re-routed dynamically inside the switch matrix.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why $\log_2 N$ Stages Suffice

**Claim:** Any integer $k$ with $0 \leq k \leq N-1$ can be realized by cascading $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ stages, each shifting by a power of two.

**Proof by Binary Expansion:**

Let $N = 2^m$ (assume power of two for simplicity; the result generalizes with ceiling).

Any shift amount $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1\}$ has a unique $m$-bit binary representation:
$$k = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} b_i \cdot 2^i, \quad b_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

Define the stage-0 output (input to stage-1):
$$D_0 = D$$
Stage $i$ performs:
$$D_{i+1} = \begin{cases} \text{Shift}(D_i, 2^i) & \text{if } b_i = 1 \\ D_i & \text{if } b_i = 0 \end{cases}$$

By induction, after stage $i$, the cumulative shift equals:
$$k_{\text{cum}} = \sum_{j=0}^{i} b_j \cdot 2^j$$

At the final stage $m-1$:
$$k_{\text{cum}} = \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} b_j \cdot 2^j = k \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Mux Count of Logarithmic Shifter

Stage $i$ contains $N$ muxes (one per output bit of that stage). The total number of muxes is:

$$M_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} N = N \cdot m = N \cdot \log_2 N$$

For a direct (unrolled) barrel shifter, every output bit can be sourced from *any* of the $N$ input bits, so:
$$M_{\text{direct}} = N \cdot N = N^2$$

**Area Ratio:**
$$\frac{A_{\text{log}}}{A_{\text{direct}}} = \frac{N \log_2 N}{N^2} = \frac{\log_2 N}{N}$$

For $N = 8$: ratio = $3/8 = 0.375$ (62.5% area savings).
For $N = 64$: ratio = $6/64 = 0.094$ (90.6% area savings).

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Path Delay Comparison

Each 2-to-1 mux introduces a delay $t_{mux} \approx 3\tau$ in CMOS (where $\tau$ is the FO1 inverter delay, including NAND/NOR gates).

**Direct barrel shifter** (decoder + final-stage muxes):
$$t_{\text{direct}} = t_{\text{decoder}} + t_{\text{mux}}$$

If a logarithmic tree decoder is used, $t_{\text{decoder}} \approx \log_2 N \cdot t_{\text{mux}}$ and the final stage adds one mux, giving a comparable total. However, *the output loads* are massive ($N$ inputs per output mux), so $t_{\text{mux}}$ itself grows.

**Logarithmic shifter** ($\log_2 N$ stages in series):
$$t_{\text{log}} = \log_2 N \cdot t_{\text{mux}}$$

where each stage's mux sees a load of only 2 inputs (the previous stage's two output lines), so $t_{\text{mux}}$ stays small.

**Net delay comparison** for large $N$: the logarithmic shifter is *faster in practice* than the direct barrel because the mux cell delay is dominated by the load capacitance, and $2 C_{\text{load}} \ll N C_{\text{load}}$.

### 3.4 Worked Example: 4-bit Logarithmic Shift-Left Shifter

**Specification:** $N = 4$ bits, $m = \log_2 4 = 2$ stages. Input $D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0$, shift amount $k = s_1 s_0$ (binary).

**Stage 0 (controlled by $s_0$):** Shift by $2^0 = 1$ if $s_0 = 1$, else pass-through.

Each output $D_0[i]$ is:
$$D_0[i] = \begin{cases} d_i & \text{if } s_0 = 0 \\ d_{i-1} & \text{if } s_0 = 1 \text{ and } i \geq 1 \\ 0 & \text{if } s_0 = 1 \text{ and } i = 0 \end{cases}$$

Explicit per-bit:
- $D_0[0] = s_0' \cdot d_0$
- $D_0[1] = s_0' \cdot d_1 + s_0 \cdot d_0$
- $D_0[2] = s_0' \cdot d_2 + s_0 \cdot d_1$
- $D_0[3] = s_0' \cdot d_3 + s_0 \cdot d_2$

**Stage 1 (controlled by $s_1$):** Shift by $2^1 = 2$ if $s_1 = 1$, else pass-through.

- $Y[0] = s_1' \cdot D_0[0]$
- $Y[1] = s_1' \cdot D_0[1]$
- $Y[2] = s_1' \cdot D_0[2] + s_1 \cdot D_0[0]$
- $Y[3] = s_1' \cdot D_0[3] + s_1 \cdot D_0[1]$

**Verification with $D = 1011$ and $k = 3$ ($s_1 s_0 = 11$):**

After Stage 0 (shift by 1): $D_0 = 0110$ (LSB filled with 0)
After Stage 1 (shift by 2): $Y = 1000$

Expected: shifting $1011$ left by 3 gives $1000$ ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Signal Tracing for $N=8$, $k=5$

Binary: $k = 5 = 101_2$, so $s_2 = 1, s_1 = 0, s_0 = 1$. Three stages: $2^0, 2^1, 2^2 = 1, 2, 4$.

| Stage | Control Bit | Shift Value | Operation |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| 0 | $s_0 = 1$ | 1 | $D \to D_0 = D \ll 1$ |
| 1 | $s_1 = 0$ | 2 (skip) | $D_0 \to D_1 = D_0$ |
| 2 | $s_2 = 1$ | 4 | $D_1 \to D_2 = D_1 \ll 4$ |

Final cumulative shift: $0 + 4 = 4$? **Wait, error!** Total = $1 + 0 + 4 = 5$ ✓ (re-checking: the active shifts are at stages 0 and 2, contributing $2^0 + 2^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$).

This validates the binary-decomposition principle.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a **Logical Shift Right (LSR)** and an **Arithmetic Shift Right (ASR)** operation. State the most important consequence of using the wrong one in a two's-complement system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **LSR** fills the vacated MSB positions with logic `0`, regardless of the original sign of the operand. It treats the data as an *unsigned* bit pattern. **(1 mark)**
- **ASR** fills the vacated MSB positions with the **sign bit** (i.e., bit $d_{N-1}$ is replicated). It preserves the sign of two's-complement signed numbers. **(1 mark)**
- **Consequence:** Using LSR instead of ASR on a negative number in two's-complement code changes the sign from negative to positive after right-shifting, corrupting the result of a signed division-by-2 operation. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** A barrel shifter is designed for a 16-bit word. Calculate (i) the number of 2-to-1 multiplexers required if a logarithmic architecture is used, and (ii) the number of multiplexer-delay stages in the critical path.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) $m = \log_2 16 = 4$ stages, each with 16 muxes → $16 \times 4 = \mathbf{64}$ muxes. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Critical path = $m = \mathbf{4}$ mux delays. **(1 mark)**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Logarithmic Shifter Design)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **logarithmic shifter** for an 8-bit data word. Clearly show all stages, control bits, and the data flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the total number of 2-to-1 multiplexers required for an N-bit logarithmic shifter and compare it with that of a direct barrel shifter. Show that the logarithmic shifter achieves an asymptotic area reduction of $O(N / \log_2 N)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of 8-bit Logarithmic Shifter:**

For $N = 8$, the number of stages is $m = \log_2 8 = 3$.

| Stage Index | Shift Value | Control Bit | Muxes per Stage |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 0 | $2^0 = 1$ | $s_0$ | 8 |
| 1 | $2^1 = 2$ | $s_1$ | 8 |
| 2 | $2^2 = 4$ | $s_2$ | 8 |

Each stage consists of 8 parallel 2-to-1 multiplexers. The $i^{th}$ bit of the output of stage $j$ is given by:

$$D_{j+1}[i] = \begin{cases} D_j[i] & \text{if } s_j = 0 \\ D_j[i - 2^j] & \text{if } s_j = 1 \text{ and } i \geq 2^j \\ 0 & \text{if } s_j = 1 \text{ and } i < 2^j \end{cases}$$

Data flows $D \to \text{Stage 0} \to \text{Stage 1} \to \text{Stage 2} \to Y$. **(4 marks for diagram and explanation)**

**Worked Example:** For $D = 10110100$ and $k = 5$ (binary $s_2 s_1 s_0 = 101$):

- Stage 0 ($s_0 = 1$): Shift by 1 → $D_0 = 01011000$
- Stage 1 ($s_1 = 0$): Pass-through → $D_1 = 01011000$
- Stage 2 ($s_2 = 1$): Shift by 4 → $Y = 10000000$ **(3 marks for verification)**

**(b) Derivation of Area Comparison:**

For an N-bit logarithmic shifter:
$$M_{\text{log}} = N \cdot \log_2 N \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

For an N-bit direct barrel shifter:
$$M_{\text{direct}} = N^2 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Ratio:**
$$\frac{M_{\text{log}}}{M_{\text{direct}}} = \frac{N \log_2 N}{N^2} = \frac{\log_2 N}{N} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Asymptotic Analysis:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\log_2 N}{N} = 0$$

Therefore the logarithmic shifter achieves an asymptotic area reduction of:
$$\text{Area Reduction} = 1 - \frac{\log_2 N}{N} \to 100\% \text{ as } N \to \infty \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Trade-off Note:** The logarithmic shifter incurs a latency of $\log_2 N$ mux delays in series, whereas the direct barrel shifter has $O(1)$ mux delay (with decoder overhead). **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Barrel Shifter & Comparative Analysis)**

**(a)** Design a 4-bit **direct barrel shifter** that performs logical shift left by an arbitrary amount $k$ ($0 \leq k \leq 3$). Draw the crossbar switch architecture and write the Boolean equations for each output bit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **direct barrel shifter** with the **logarithmic shifter** in terms of (i) area, (ii) speed, (iii) regularity of layout, and (iv) wire routing complexity. Support your answer with numerical data for $N = 8, 16, 32$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 4-bit Direct Barrel Shifter (Logical Shift Left):**

Let input be $D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0$, shift amount be $k = s_1 s_0$.

| Output | Shift by 0 ($k=0$) | Shift by 1 ($k=1$) | Shift by 2 ($k=2$) | Shift by 3 ($k=3$) |
|--------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| $Y_3$ | $d_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_1$ | $d_0$ |
| $Y_2$ | $d_2$ | $d_1$ | $d_0$ | $0$ |
| $Y_1$ | $d_1$ | $d_0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $Y_0$ | $d_0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

Boolean equations (using minterm expansion):

$$Y_3 = s_1' s_0' d_3 + s_1' s_0 d_2 + s_1 s_0' d_1 + s_1 s_0 d_0$$
$$Y_2 = s_1' s_0' d_2 + s_1' s_0 d_1 + s_1 s_0' d_0$$
$$Y_1 = s_1' s_0' d_1 + s_1' s_0 d_0$$
$$Y_0 = s_1' s_0' d_0$$

Total muxes required: $4 \times 4 = 16$ (4 rows × 4 columns). **(4 marks for equations)**

**Crossbar structure:** Each row $Y_i$ contains four pass transistors gated by the four decoded shift-amount lines. Only one pass transistor per row is ON at any time. **(3 marks for diagram and explanation)**

**(b) Comparative Analysis:**

| Criterion | Direct Barrel Shifter | Logarithmic Shifter | Best Choice |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Area** | $O(N^2)$ | $O(N \log_2 N)$ | Logarithmic |
| **Speed (latency)** | $O(1)$ mux delay | $O(\log_2 N)$ mux delays | Direct |
| **Layout regularity** | Poor (long wires) | Excellent (modular) | Logarithmic |
| **Wire routing** | $N^2$ long wires | $N \cdot \log_2 N$ short local wires | Logarithmic |
| **Power** | High (parasitics) | Lower | Logarithmic |

**Numerical Data:**

| $N$ | $M_{\text{direct}}$ | $M_{\text{log}}$ | $t_{\text{log}} / t_{mux}$ | Area Saving % |
|-----|---------------------|------------------|-----------------------------|---------------|
| 8   | 64                  | 24               | 3                           | 62.5%         |
| 16  | 256                 | 64               | 4                           | 75.0%         |
| 32  | 1024                | 160              | 5                           | 84.4%         |

**(3 marks for table and inference)**

**Conclusion:** The logarithmic shifter is preferred in modern ASIC designs due to its superior area scalability and ease of physical design, despite its longer critical path. ARM-style single-cycle barrel shifters are exceptions where speed dominates area. **(4 marks for analysis and conclusion)**

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `N` and `log N`** — A 16-bit logarithmic shifter has **64 muxes**, not 16. Always multiply $N \times \log_2 N$.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Shift-by-0" path** — Every stage of a logarithmic shifter is a *2-to-1* mux (shift by 0 vs. shift by $2^i$). Drawing it as a single wire loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up shift directions** — Left shift moves data toward MSB and fills LSB with 0; right shift moves toward LSB and fills MSB. **Double-check** before answering.
> 4. **Ignoring sign extension** — A right shift in a signed system must use ASR, not LSR. The KTU paper often has a "trick" question asking what happens if you use LSR on a negative number.
> 5. **Not drawing the data path** — A block diagram earns **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question. Never write only equations.
> 6. **Skipping the decoder** — Even a direct barrel shifter needs a shift-amount decoder. Forgetting it loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong log base** — In VLSI, $\log$ is always base 2 unless otherwise stated. Writing $\log_{10}$ loses 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] A **shifter** translates bits laterally; it is the building block of multiplication/division by powers of 2.
- [ ] Four primitive shift modes: **LSL, LSR, ASR, ROL/ROR**.
- [ ] **Logical vs Arithmetic Right Shift** — only difference is the fill bit (`0` vs sign bit).
- [ ] **Barrel shifter** = single-cycle arbitrary shift; area $N^2$, delay 1 mux.
- [ ] **Logarithmic shifter** = cascaded $\log_2 N$ stages; area $N \log_2 N$, delay $\log_2 N$ muxes.
- [ ] Each stage $i$ of a logarithmic shifter performs a shift by $0$ or $2^i$, controlled by bit $s_i$ of the shift amount.
- [ ] **Number of stages** $m = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$.
- [ ] **Total muxes (logarithmic)** = $N \cdot \log_2 N$.
- [ ] **Total muxes (direct barrel)** = $N^2$.
- [ ] **Area reduction ratio** = $\log_2 N / N \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$.
- [ ] Logarithmic shifter has **modular layout** and short wires → preferred for ASIC.
- [ ] Direct barrel shifter has **single-cycle latency** → preferred for high-speed processors (ARM).
- [ ] For $N=8$: direct uses 64 muxes, logarithmic uses 24 muxes (3 stages).
- [ ] For $N=32$: direct uses 1024 muxes, logarithmic uses 160 muxes (5 stages).
- [ ] The critical-path delay of the logarithmic shifter is **linear in $\log_2 N$**, not in $N$.
- [ ] Pass-transistor muxes are often used to reduce transistor count by ~30% over CMOS muxes.
- [ ] Rotation (ROR/ROL) requires end-around feedback — not supported by a plain logarithmic shifter without extra wiring.
- [ ] KTU board loves questions that mix **barrel + log** comparison tables — memorize the comparison table verbatim.
- [ ] Always mention the **decoder** when describing a direct barrel shifter's control logic.
